We have on hand 170 bushels of wheat,
and are making calculations to gather all
we can this Fall; as we have a bountiful har-

[Column 3]
vest this season; we feel to persevere and
lay up while it is plenty.
	We were honored with a visit from our
beloved Sisters E. R. Snow and E. Davis
on the 9th inst., and had two very interest-
ing meetings. The Spirit of God was
poured out upon them, and we had a time
of rejoicing in listening to their kind and
good counsel. Our sisters appreciate the
visits of Sister Snow and the excellent in-
structions we receive from her, and pray
that she may live long and do all the good
her heart desires. We would be pleased if
she and our other good sisters could visit
us very often. Sister Snow exhorted us to
sustain home industry, and to do all in our
power to start the silk business. Congratu-
lated us on our success in the millinery;
asked all to rise to their feet who wore
homemade hats, and almost every one
arose. She blest them for their willingness
to obey counsel, and spoke many cheering
words to the sisters, which will be long re-
membered. Our President, Sister Liljen-
quist, is alive to her duties and the inter-
ests of the kingdom of God, as are also all
of the officers of our Society, and the mem-
bers generally feel to respond to every call.
	Wishing continued success to the EXPO-
NENT, I subscribe myself your sister in the
Gospel,
			N. M. NEILSEN, Secretary.


RELIEF SOCIETY QUARTERLY CON
			FERENCE

		DAVIS COUNTY STAKE.

	The Quarterly Conference of the Relief
Societies a[-]d Y. L. M. I. A's of Davis Stake
was held at Farmington, Oct. 17, 1878,
commencing at 10 a. m.; Pres. Sarah I
Holmes presiding. There were present on
the stand Pres. M. I. Horne and her Coun-
selors, E. S. Taylor and S. M. Heywood, of 
the Salt Lake Stake, Pres. S. I. Holmes and
Counselors, Mary S. and Nancy A. Clark, of
the Davis Co. Stake.
	After singing and prayer, the minutes of
the preceding meeting and also the reports
of the various Societies in the County were
read by Miss M. E. Richards, Secretary.
The reports show—
Receipts since the organization	$10200 00
Disbursements				8957 00
On hand in the Treasury			1243 00
Wheat stored				57 bu., 21 lbs
	Sisters Horne, Taylor and Heywood occu-
pied the remainder of the forenoon, ex-
horting the sisters to be diligent in the
performance of their duties; home indus-
try, storing grain, unity and punctuality
were the chief subjects, upon which they
spoke with considerable earnestness.
	The afternoon meeting was presided over
by Mrs. Nancy A. Clark, President of the
Young Ladies' Associations of the Stake.
	Secretary Miss Wealthy Richards read
the reports of three Associations, showing
that they are awake to their duties.
	Sisters Horne, Taylor, Heywood, Holmes
and Clark gave some excellent instructions
and encouraged the young ladies to con-
tinue in well doing, and to use their influ-
ence to bring about a reformation in the
young men.
	Mrs. Aurelia Rogers gave a verbal report
of the Primary Association of Farmington,
of which she is President. Manifested a
great interest in the mission assigned her.
A good spirit prevailed, and prompted
each speaker. Meeting adjourned until the
16th of January, 1879.
		MINERVA E. RICHARDS, Sec'y.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LET BYGONES BE BYGONES.

Let bygones be bygones; if bygones were clouded
	By aught that occasioned a pang of regret,
Oh, let them in darkest oblivion be shrouded;
	'Tis wise and 'tis kind to forgive and forget.

Let bygones be bygones, and good be extracted
	From ill over which it is folly to fret;
The wisest of mortals have foolishly acted—
	The kindest are those who forgive and forget.

Let bygones be bygones; oh, cherish no longer
	The thought that the sun of affection has set;
Eclipsed for a moment, its rays will be stronger,
	If you, like a Christian, forgive and forget.

Let bygones be bygones; your heart will be lighter,
	When kindness of yours with reception has met;
The flame of your love will be purer and brighter
	If, Godlike, you strive to forgive and forget.

Let bygones be bygones; oh, purge out the leaven
	Of malice, and try an example to set,
To others, who, craving the mercy of heaven,
	Are sadly too slow to forgive and forget.

Let bygones be bygones; remember how deeply
	To heaven's forbearance we all are in debt;
They value God's infinite goodness too cheaply
	Who heed not the precept, "Forgive and forget."
						—Chambers' Journal.


[Column 1]
A LEAF FROM AN AUTOBIOGRAPHY.

		CONTINUED.
	This feast lasted three days, during which
time all in the vicinity of Kirtland who
would come were invited, and entertained
as courteously and generously as if they
had been able to extend hospitality instead
of receiving it. The Prophet Joseph and
his two Counselors being present each day,
talking, blessing, and comforting the poor,
by words of encouragement and their most
welcome presence; some are now living
who were present at that Feast, and many
have passed behind the vail. The Prophet
Joseph often referred to this particular Feast,
during his lifetime, and testified of the
great blessing he felt in associating with
the meek and humble ones whom the Lord
has said "He delights to own and bless."
He often said to me that it was preferable
and far superior to the elegant and select
parties he afterwards attended, and afford-
ed him much more genuine satisfaction;
and to me it was "a feast of fat things in-
deed; a season of rejoicing never to be for-
gotten. The Temple in Kirtland being
built and dedicated, the word of the Lord
to the Saints was to build up Missouri, and 
the Saints repaired thither as fast as cir-
cumstances would permit.
	Great manifestations of power were wit-
nessed in the Kirtland Temple; it used
oftentimes to seem as though it was illumi-
nated, and many and powerful were the
manifestations to those who were humble
and participated in the ordinances bestowed
upon the faithful Saints in that house. The
first partriarchal blessing meeting over
which Joseph Smith, Sen., presided was
one of the most striking and noticeable fea-
tures of that particular period of time. In
this meeting I received the gift of singing
inspirationally, and the first Song of Zion
ever given in the pure language was sung
by me then, and interpreted by Parley P.
Pratt, and written down; of which I have
preserved the original copy. It describes
the manner in which the ancient patriarchs
blessed their families, and gives some ac-
count of "Adam ondi Ahman."

In ancient days there lived a man;
Amidst a pleasant garden,
Where lovely flowers immortal bloom'd,
And shed around a rich perfume;
Behold, his name was Adam.

One of the nobles of the Earth,
Had mighty power in blessing;

[Column 2]
Received the Priesthood, and went forth
And blessed his seed, and gave the earth
Blessings for their possession.

He sealed them for eternal life,
And all their generations,
Who should obey the Gospel plan,
Down to the latest years of man—
A multitude of nations.

Isaac and Jacob, they in turn
Had power to bless their children;
Hence, Jacob by his faith did learn,
And gave directions for his bones
To be conveyed to Canaan.

By the same spirit, Joseph gave
A great and mighty blessing
To Ephraim, and Manasseh too,
Whereby their seed were carried through
Long travels, though distressing.

By that same faith they built a ship,
And crossed the mighty ocean.
Obtain'd the choicest land of Earth,
Foretold the great Messiah's birth,
And all the great commotion,
*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*
The Holy Priesthood long remain'd
In all its power and glory,
Until the Priests of God were slain,
Their records hid from wicked men
Within the hill Cumorah.

Their remnants sank in sorrow down,
Became a loathsome people,
To misery and sorrow doom'd,
Their pleasant fields o'erspread with gloom,
Ruled by a Gentile nation.

But now the Priesthood is restored,
And we partake its blessings;
Our parents and our children dear
With Joseph's remnants have a share
To latest generations.

As Adam blest his family
In Adam ondi Ahman,
So shall our aged father bless
His seed who dwell in righteousness
Upon the land of Zion.
*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*
The Prophet Joseph promised me that I 
should never lose this gift if I would be
wise in using it; and his words have been
verified.
	My husband's partner in business had
been sent up to Jackson Co., Missouri, and
had opened a branch business there; and
according to the best of my recollection
about 1837, the Prophet Joseph called up-
on my husband to go up to Far West to pre-
side; we set about making preparations for
removal. My friends, who knew what deli-
cate health I had, and how unaccustomed I
was to any hardships, inconvenience, or
privation, looked upon it as almost certain
death for me to go into the wilderness, or
what seemed little better than a desert, or
wiiderness among Indians. My friends
tried to prevail on me to stay until my
husband should go and prepare a comfort-
able place for me and my children; but I 
had unbounded faith in the promises of Jo-
seph, that I should be able to go in safety,
and my trust in God was firm and unshrink-
ing. I bade adieu to my beloved home,
where I had anticipated spending all my
life, and where everything had been ar-
ranged according to my own ideas of taste
and beauty; to my dear friends, and my
kind and ever true Aunt Sarah, who had
been a mother indeed to me and mine. I
felt a tender yearning towards her, and 
had desired with all my heart to bring her
into the faith of the Everlasting Gospel,
but had never been even successful enough
to get her to believe in the least; she looked
upon me as one bereft of my senses; this 
was the hardest trial to me of all—to leave
one so true and faithful and devoted as she
had ever proved herself to me and mine,
alone, without children to comfort her; but
I believed I was serving my Heavenly Fa-
ther and the best interests of all my friends
by so doing. My children were so imbued
with the spirit of the Gospel, that although

[Column 3]
they were disappointed in their hopes and 
expectations in regard to obtaining a supe-
rior education such as we had sought to
stimulate them to obtain, previous to our
embracing the latter-day work; yet they
accepted this change in their worldly cir-
cumstances without a murmur. They were
devotedly attached to Joseph, and were
never weary of praying for him; and their
faith seemed perfect. In their innocence
and trust they believed truly the Lord
would hear and answer them, and they
firmly trusted in all the promises and bless-
ings pronounced upon them. With the 
earnest confidence and assurance of these
little children, I felt I could meet all oppo-
sition, and trusting in my Heavenly Father
come off victorious.
		To be continued.


IMPROPER TREATMENT OF IN-
			FANTS.

	A gentleman of much experience and
sound judgment remarks, that many a little
grave is filled from no other cause than the
imprudent and unreasonable manner in
which a tender but ignorant mother treats
her child.
	She goes with it to the table, where all
kinds of food are prepared; baby coos and
catches at her knife, or spoon, with its tiny
fingers, and if the mother herself does not
think of it, some one else imagines that
baby wants a taste of pie, or pudding, or
something else, and mother, or auntie
having no realizing sense of how small a
particle of rich food may distress the little
stomach not accustomed to it, keeps giving
it a little of first one dainty article and then
another, till presently baby begins to fret,
and then it wants a spoonful of tea or coffee
and gets it; and then cries and worries,
and mama "can't tell what ails it!" Then
it must have some camphor or panacea, and
be jolted and trotted and shaken. until its
flesh is so sore it cannot be moved or
touched without experiencing pain; so it
screams and moans until, doubtless, it be-
comes an immaculate martyr, victimized
by medicines for cold, consumption, lung
fever, &c., while the sole cause of its suf-
fering and death might justly be attributed
to its swallowing a very small amount of
food, too rich or too strenuous for the little
unprepared stomach to digest. And yet
the poor, stricken mother feels that she has
done all in her power to save the life of her
precious darling, when, she is at last com-
pelled to lay it away out of sight. Study
human nature, mothers; a proper under-
standing of it is invaluable.
						LULA.


Extract from a letter of one of the Di-
rectors of the Eastern Counties railway,
England, to Mrs. Hannah T. King:
	"You speak of your new locomotive of
thirty-two tons; ours now are more than
double that! In the early times, an engine
weighing twenty tons was considered ab-
surd, and impossible to run! George Steph-
enson, the inventor, was laughed at, before
a committee of the House of Commons, for
daring to hint that a locomotive might be
worked at fifteen miles an hour; now, it is
worked on the lines at sixty miles an hour,
with a heavy train behind it! A frightful
speed, you would think, if you could stand
and see it pass."


FEW are sufficiently wise to prefer
censure which is useful, to praise which is
treacherous.

	SALT LAKE CITY, NOV. 1, 1878.


[Column 1]
CHARITY OF MORMON WOMEN.

	Charity is always illustrated by the figure of
a woman, and mankind have silently classed
this attribute as a womanly characteristic, and
called it the sister of Faith and Hope; and in
later years, women who spend a great portion
of their time in performing deeds of kindness
and benevolence, are designated as Sisters of
Charity. So closely associated is charity with
the life of the fair sex, that it is considered a
positive requisite of true womanly character.
	The world talk a great deal of charity, and
make a great ado about its loveliness, its excel-
lence, its charm, but though it sits admirably
on others, many persons are apt to admire the
mantle as an ideal beauty, who never really
don the garb, or cultivate the element in them-
selves. It sounds well, it is a pretty sentiment,
one likes, above all things, to realize it as a
part of the elements of that society with which
one mingles, but how hard it is to cherish it as
a part and portion of one's individual every-
day life.
	There is a large-hearted, open-handed charity
that ennobles and enriches the lives of both
men and women, for certainly if women have
this attribute largely developed they will im-
part the true germ to both sons and daughters;
this is the inevitable law of nature; but every
germ wants nourishment and proper cultiva-
tion, and the attributes possessed or inherited
resemble in many respects the order of nature's
vegetable kingdom. The exquisite germs re-
ceived as a precious heritage, placed in healthy
soil and carefully tended, grow and flourish,
and by and by blossom and ripen, producing
desirable fruits or results, but left in barren soil
without cultivation, are stinted, and wither
away: Then there is a custom or theory of
cultivation for all plants and all principles,
which, when adopted, promote fuller, richer
growth than to pursue all sorts of ways.
	Mormon women are anxious to ascertain and
pursue the highest and best methods in every
department of life and labor, and also in their
charities. They have a system of charity that
surpasses any other in its fitness and adapta-
tion to the circumstances of the people. We
allude to the institution called Relief Society;
but wishing not only to provide resources for
the present needs of the poor, the aged, the in-
firm and sick, but also to extend a still wider
field of benevolence, and enlarge the scope of
woman's labor beyond the to-day, and prepare
for emergencies in the coming future, Mormon
women have adopted a large hearted system of
public charity in the storing of grain, which
forms the staff of life.
	It is now over two years since this peculiar
enterprise was established, and from all parts
of the Territory reports reach us of the enjoy-
ment the sisters have in fulfilling this mission
of good works. It is a mission which impresses

[Column 2]
all who are truly charitable in tone and senti-
ment with the idea of its loftiness and sublim-
ity.
	Sisters, do you realize the privilege you
have, to store up grain against a day of want?
If you do, prove it by your most earnest and
energetic efforts now, when the Lord has
given such a bounteous harvest. The second
Annual Meeting of the several committees for
storing grain will be held in this city, Nov. 16;
and we hope previous to that time to receive
accurate reports from all parts of the Territory
stating the amount of grain on hand, and the
manner in which it is secured.
	We invite all who are interested in this grand
enterprise to attend the meeting and hear the
reports concerning this department of woman's
work. Due notice of the time and place of
holding it will be given through the "Deseret
News." One of the most active workers in
woman's cause in the East, an editor of a pa-
per devoted to the interests and advancement
of women, says, "I'm much interested in the
industries and labors of Mormon women, and
especially the storing of grain, and intend giv-
ing place soon to an article on the subject."
								[---]


[Column 1]
		HOME AFFAIRS.

	BY LETTER we learn that Sisters E. R.
Snow and E. Davis, who are making a tour
through some of the northern settlements,
were at Echo City on the 27th of October, in
the enjoyment of health, and rejoicing in their
labors.
	UTAH CONSERVATORY OF MUSIC.—Dr. Ellen
B. Ferguson intends establishing a musical
conservatoire on the Europen style. By this
means pupils may receive competent instruc-
tion in vocal and instrumental music much
cheaper than the usual terms. Dr. Ferguson is
a lady of superior culture, and has had the ad-
vantages of training in musical institutions
in Europe. She has the happy faculty of bring-
ing her pupils forward rapidly, and certainly
deserves patronage. She is also a teacher of
languages, elocution, dramatic reading, and,
indeed, is qualified to give instruction in all
the higher branches of education.
	ON THURSDAY, Oct. 24th, through courtesy
of Hon. George Q. Cannon, we had the plea-
sure of attending the Indian Commission party
in a railroad trip to the Old Telegraph Smelt-
ing, Concentrating and Leaching Works, a
little beyond the Junction of the Bingham
Cañon Railroad. There was a sufficient num-
ber to make the party a pleasant one, and as
most of the gentlemen and ladies were unac-
quainted with the process of smelting, concen-
trating and leaching, and as Prof. Holden was
ready to elucidate and explain, as also Mrs.
Holden, who seemed quite intelligent on the
subject, going through the establishment was
made specially interesting. The party con-
sisted of Senator Saunders of Nebraska; Sena-
tor McCreery of Kentucky, General Hooker of
Mississippi, and Dr. Stuart and Mr. Caldwell
of Minnesota, of the joint Indian Commission,
with several gentlemen from Camp Douglas,
Col. Trotter, Lieut. Patterson and others; from
the city Mayor Little, wife and daughters,
Prof. L. E. Holden and wife, Prof T. B. Lewis
and wife, Bishop Sharp and daughter, the re-
porters for the "Deseret News" and "Salt Lake
Herald," and several other young people, who,
with the genial flow of wit and humor, made
the conversation lively, and the time sped
swiftly as also the train.
	THE YOUNG Ladies' Mutual Improvement
Association of the Eleventh Ward celebrated

[Column 2]
their Seventh Anniversary Friday, Oct. 18, in
the Ward Meeting House; Mrs. Mary A. Freeze
presiding. There were present as visitors Pres
Angus M. Cannon and several brethren of the
ward, Mrs. M. I. Horne, President of Relief
Societies of Salt Lake Co., and her Counselors,
Mrs. E. S. Taylor and S. M. Heywood, Mrs.
Phebe Woodruff and Mrs. S. M. Kimball.
	After singing and prayer, Ass't Secretary
Miss Alice Freeze read the minutes of the last
meeting, and Treasurer Mrs. Louie Felt read
the Financial Report for the past year, which
was a very creditable one, there having been
donated by the Society $193 00, which had
been disbursed to the Temple, to the storing of
grain, and to charitable purposes. Next fol-
lowed an essay by Mrs. E. R. Shipp, M. D.
Pres. Cannon spoke eloquently on the estab-
lishing of Mutual Improvement Associations
and their object; also upon the necessity of
sustaining home industries. Mrs. Horne made
some excellent remarks, and was followed by
the other lady visitors, all of whom gave good
instructions. Counselor E. R. Shipp, in be-
half of the Association, presented to the Presi-
dent, Mrs. Freeze, an elegant hair wreath,
nicely framed; the wreath being composed
entirely of the hair of the members, accompa-
nied by a poem written by Mrs. Shipp and
dedicated to Mrs. Freeze, who responded in a
few words, it being wholly a surprise to her.
The meeting adjourned for one year; and the 
company sat down to a choice repast which
had been arranged by the young ladies. A good
spirit pervaded the assembly, and all felt that
the time had been well spent.
	IN COMPANY with Sisters Zina D. and Martha
B. Young, we recently made a tour through
some of the settlements in Utah County, in
the interest of th[-] Relief Societies and Young
Ladies' Mutual Improvement Associations.
We left this city on Wednesday morning, Oct.
16, and was absent four days; visited Lehi,
American Fork, Spanish Fork, Goshen, Pleas-
ant Grove, and attended nine meetings. The
Bishops and brethren in the various lo[-]alities
came out to the sisters' meetings, manifesting
the greatest interest in their labors. Under
the direction of the Bishops and Relief Socie-
ties eight Primary Associctions were organized.
Much general instruction was given in regard
to woman's work in connection with these So-
cieties, all of which was sanctioned by the
Bishops; the good results of the sisters' labors
are everywhere becoming apparent.
	At Spanish Fork we found the meeting house
decorated with a great variety of needle and
ornamental work of various kinds, designs and
patterns, in honor of their Annual Meeting.
We were all both surprised and delighted to
see that so much had been done by the Society.
The report was excellent. At Goshen we met
with a similar surprise, the house being very
beautifully decorated, as though it had been
for a festival instead of a meeting; indeed, the
effect seemed to be inspiring. It was also the
Annual Meeting of the Relief Society of
Go[-]hen.  There were visitors from several other
settlements—Spanish Fork, Santaquin and
Spring Lake, among whom were Bishop Halla-
day of Santaquin, and Mrs. E. J. Stickney,
President of Santaquin R. S, Mrs. M. Fergu-
son, President of Spanish Fork. Their report
was also very creditable. In the evening we
met with the Y. L. M. I. A., a large number
of young men also being present; after the
evening had been spent in speaking and other
religious exercises, Mrs Christina Higginson
read a poem she had composed for the occasion,
as a welcome to the sisters who had come to
visit them.


Saturday morning the little children were
called together and were organized into a Pri-
mary Association. Everywhere we went we
were most cordially welcomed and hospitably
entertained, and the best feelings of the peo-
ple seemed to be drawn out in supporting the
advancement of women in the home indus-
tries, storing of grain, silk-culture, i[-] fact all
spiritual, mental and industrial progression.
	The weather was fine, the country air brac-
ing, and the picture which presented ittelf on
all sides was pleasing. Peaceful villages nes-
t[-]ed in the valleys, with fields, just shorn of
their beauty, lying bare, while the numerous
stacks and cornricks told of the plenteous har-
vests; orchards just turned brown and purple,
and shrubbery, nipped with frost, in all the va-
rieties of shade, cattle grazing on the hills, or
lowing in the farmyards, herds of sheep nip-
ping closer the short grass, stands of beehives,
and a thousand other pastoral beauties, that
bespoke the thrift and industry of the people.
How one accustomed to the city rejoices in a
visit to the country, where all is simple and
rustic, and the very air of hospitality is in it-
self exhilarating. In this mountainous land
wherever we go there is variety in the scenery,
land at this season of the year the rich hues of
autumn foliage lends another charm to the
mountain scenery. The maple, the mountain
ash, the scrub oak and ivy are ablaze with
scarlet, or royally robed in purple.
	Everywhere prosperity seems to smile upon
the homes of the Saints, and the blessings of
God crown their labors. One of the most pleas-
ing things in our experience in these visits is
to see the little children, boys and girls, called
together in the Primary Associations; their
smiling faces glowing with health, their eyes
sparkling with intelligence, watching the
speakers, anxiously expectant, and desirous
to learn their part in the programme of the
meeting.
	We return our thanks to the brethren and
sisters who so cordially entertained our party,
and conveyed us all from place, and especially
the Bishops who opened the meeting houses
for the accomodation of the sisters, extending
them every privilege possible to make the
meetings pleasant, and honoring the occasion
with their presence and hearty approval of the
sisters' works.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		85


TO THE OFFICERS AND MEMBERS OF THE Y. L.
	M. I. A. OF SALT LAL[-] STAKE OF ZION:
	GREETING!

	Beloved Sisters: Having been recently chosen
as Superintendent of the branches of Y. L. M.
I. A. in this Stake of Zion, I feel desirous to
express, in a humble manner, my thanks for
the honor conferred upon me by my sisters in
electing me to fill so responsible a position. I
am thankful to my Heavenly Father that we,
the handmaidens of the Lord, are called to be
co-workers with the brethren in His work
in this the last dispensation. Sisters, do we
fully appreciate the privileges and blessings
we enjoy through the principles and institu-
tions of the holy Gospel? I know there are
many faithful sisters among the young ladies,
and I comprehend, though in a small degree
only, something of the future that lies before
them, and the need they have to qualify them-
selves for high and holy duties.
	I shall be pleased to visit the various
branches of the Y. L. M. I. A. in this county,
from time to time, whenever it will be most
convenient for the sisters to receive me. I
would say to the officers of Societies, be faith-
ful in advising and encouraging the young sis-
ters to assist in sustaining all home industries,


also home literature. The young ladies should
read the best books and papers, those that will
give them a knowledge of the principles of
eternal truth. There are many good books of
prose and poetry. The Harp of Zion is full of
inspiration, and is musical in expression and
delineation of its subjects. There are two vol-
umes of poems by Zion's sweet songstress, our
beloved Sister Eliza R. Snow, and the WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT furnishes us with both prose
and poetry of a character adapted to the minds
of the women of Zion, and it should be sus-
tained by our young ladies as well as the older
ones; all have the privilege of contributing to
its columns. Great things will be required at
the hands of the teachers and instructors of
the young, and there is much need of wisdom
to discharge judiciously the duties devolving
upon them. May the blessing of God rest upon
the young ladies who are striving to be useful
in their day and generation, and upon all who
are co-workers in the great Latter-day work, is
the prayer of
	Your humble servant,
				MARY A. FREEZE.
			Superintendent Y. L. M. I. A.


[Column 1]
			IN MEMORIAM.

	It pleased our Heavenly Father to call to
Himself, on September 29, our beloved Sister
Lydia Ann Weiler Horne. She left a husband
and four children (one of them a little babe
six weeks old), and a circle of friends as exten-
sive as her acquaintance, to mourn her loss.
She possessed some of the most exquisite wo-
manly faculties and attributes. She exercised
a decided influence for good, which her sweet
memory will preserve. Her home was ever a
pleasant resort to those who associated with
her.
	For ten years she lived and labored with us,
in which time her example helped greatly to
elevate the tone and taste of society in our set-
tlement. For six years she was President of
our Relief Society, when her feeble health
would not admit of the responsibilities which
that position involved. She then acted for
three years as First Counselor to our present
President, Mrs. Sarah Copley, in which posi-
tion she made herself very useful till her
death. While her husband, Bishop Joseph S.
Horne, was on a recent mission to Switzerland
she was ever happy and hopeful. Her children
show what a mother can do by precept and ex-
ample. Her faith in God and His priesthood
was unfaltering, and but a few hours before her
death she said, "If it was not for the priest-
hood, I should not be here now."
	The funeral services were held in our school-
house, immediately before Br. Horne started
with the remains for S. L. City, where they,
according to her own request, were deposited.
Many loving friends assembled to take a last
look at that dear, gentle friend; and many ac-
companied them to the next settlement, Fay-
ette, where Bro. Horne warmly thanked his
friends for their sympathy and the kind feel-
ings thus expressed.				H. M.
	Gunnison, Oct. 15, 1878.

	DIED, of diphtheria, in Salt Lake City, Wed-
nesday afternoon, Oct. 16, little Roy Clawson,
youngest [-]on of H. B. and Ellen S. Clawson,
aged three years and one month. Roy was
born September 20, 1875. He did not appear
to suffer at all during his four days' illness, and
passed away as gently and sweetly as though
he was going to sleep. "Auntie, I'm done
playing," were the last words he ever spoke.
He was a most interesting child, affectionate
in his nature, and fondly attached to his little

[Column 2]
playmates, brothers and sisters; his death has
left a vacancy in the home circle that can
never be filled until the morning of the resur-
rection, when "mothers receive their dead to
life;" but the great Healer of all wounds helps
to make our griefs less poignant as he journeys
onward, and to all who seek it the Holy Spirit,
the Comforter, is given, which lifts the mourn-
er above sorrow and gives that consolation
which is an ever-abiding hope and trust in the
promises of God, and enables us to say, "God
knoweth best."
	We extend our sincere sympathies to the be-
reaved parents and family.

	"There is no flock, however watch'd and tended,
		But one dead lamb is there!
	There is no fireside, howe'er defended,
		But has one vacant chair."

	DIED, in Salt Lake City, Aug. 18, 1878, Flor-
ence Emily Dunford, youngest daughter of
Bishop George and Mrs. Sarah Dunford, aged
13 years, 8 months, and 24 days. Deceased was
born in St. Louis, Mo., Dec. 14, 1864. She was
a superior and promising girl, mature in devel-
opment, both physically and mentally, beyond
her years, and this was often remarked upon
by her relatives and those who were intimately
acquainted with her. She possessed some of
the finest womanly qualities, which rendered
her specially attractive to her schoolmates and
friends. In her home she was fondly cher-
ished, endearing herself to father, mother,
brothers and sisters, by her kindly manner and
thoughtfulness of the wishes and desires of
others. She had a tender heart, ever alive to
the sorrows of her associates, manifesting as
much interest in their troubles as if they were
her own. Miss Flossie (as she was usually
called) was a constant attendant at Miss Cook's
school for several years. At the exhibition
given at the close of the school last summer,
she recited "Infeiicissime," a very beautiful
and touching poem, the last words of which
are,

		"And the careless are the light—
			Light of heart and light of head;
		And ye robe the bride in white,—
			And in white ye shroud the dead."

Methinks I see her now as she stood there
then, in maidenly sweetness, full of life and
vigor and in the bloom of health and youthful
beauty. We sympathize most deeply with
the bereaved mother and friends, but feel as-
sured that with their dear Flossie all is well.


REMEDIES FOR THE BLUES.

	"What makes you look so down-hearted,
as if you hadn't a friend in the world?"
asked an unmarried lady of one who was
the mother of five children. "Because I
feel so," was the reply. "Well, why do
you feel so? Why don't you sing and smile
and think of the blessings which surround
you, and thank God for them and ask for
more, and be happy?" queried the single
lady, who, having no trouble in being cheer-
ful herself, could not see why others should.
To which the weary wife and mother re-
plied, "You know I never could sing.,,
"Why don,t you whistle, then?" asked the
girl. But here listen to what Faith Roches-
ter has to say about it. "Good health is 
a foundation for that which goes by the
name of 'Good nature.' A half sick woman
will usually be irritable. When a loving
and well-meaning mother finds herself
'cross,' she needs to pray for grace, and 
something else—to pray in the most practi-
cal manner for plenty of help and plenty of
rest, and for good diet and pure air, too."
Certainly, Faith is right.
						LULA.


86		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


QUARTERLY OONFERENCE.

SALT LAKE STAKE.

	The Quarterly Conference of the Salt
Lake Stake of Zion was held in the large
Tabernacle in this city, Oct. 5th, commenc-
ing at 10 a. m., Elder Angus M. Cannon
presiding. There was a fair representation
of the officers of the Stake present. All
the wards were represented, except the
20th and North Jordan Wards. After the 
prayer and singing were concluded, a sta-
tistical report of the Stake for the Quarter
ending Aug. 31st, was read; this report in-
cluding also the number of persons drawing
support, the amount of support drawn, and
the amount of fast-day donations in this
Stake.
	A financial report of the Relief Societies
of this Stake, as follows: Wheat on hand,
stored by the Central Grain Committee,
1525 bushels; stored by branch Societies of
the Stake, 4,632 bu.; stored during the
past quarter, 592 bu.; total now on hand,
6,749 bu.; received in cash and property,
$3,747 21; disbursments in cash and prop-
erty, $1,621 79.
	Letter from the Superintendent and As-
sistants of the Sunday Schools of the Stake,
representing them as everywhere in a
prosperous condition. Report from the
New Tabernacle committee, in response to
an allotment made to the various wards.
President Joseph Young made some re-
marks on the duties of the Seventies. Pres.
Joseph E. Taylor presented the authorities
of the Stake, who were unanimously sus-
tained as at last Conference, with some few
exceptions and changes. Elder Orson F.
Whitney was sustained as Bishop of the
18th Ward, in place of Elder Lorenzo D.
Young, whose resignation had been ac-
cepted; Robert Patrick and Wm. B. Barton 
as his Counselors. The leader and members
of the Tabernacle Choir, the President and
Counselors of the Scandinavian meetings
were also sustained. Also Mrs. M. I. Horne
as President of Relief Societies, and her
Counselors, Mrs. E. S. Taylor and Mrs. S.
M. Heywood. Superintendents and assis-
tants of Sunday Schools were sustained as
previously:
	Pres. Angus M. Cannon then made some
remarks upon the business matters of the
Stake, and the conduct of men holding po-
sitions. He said: "Those who disgrace
the positions they occupy by drinking and
other evil practices, will be removed, and
more worthy men appointed in their place;
for these positions are important, and those
sustained in them must wield a proper in-
fluence among the people." He said, in
answer to a question that had been asked,
that women could only hold the priesthood
in connection with their husbands; man
held the priesthood independent of woman.
The sisters have a right to anoint the sick,
and pray the Father to heal them, and to
exercise that faith that will prevail with
God; but women must be careful how they
use the authority of the priesthood in ad-
ministering to the sick.
In the afternoon the time was occupied
by Presidents J. E. Taylor and Angus M. 
Cannon, Apostle Orson Pratt and Pres. John
Taylor, in giving general instruction to the
Saints on various subjects, both of a tem-
poral and spiritual character. The Confer-
ence adjourned sine die.


HEALTH.

	Health, thou precious boon, how early
are we forsaken by thee, how difficult to


regain thee. Why must I languish here,
when my soul loves to wrestle with the
many promises of success held out by am-
bition and hope. Sweet spirit of health, I
call you cruel! I pined, and to my com-
plaint there seemed to come a wall of re-
proach in answer; “I am chained, I am
bound, I cannot come till you loose my fet-
ters.” I loose thy fetters, I who so long to
hold thee to my bosom and call thee mine?
“Even so, to you, who formed the links one
by one.” I only answered, How? “Do
you not transgress the divine laws of God
and nature day by day? Are not the hours
given for sleep, when nature clothes herself
with the mantle of darkness, purloined by
thee, and given to employment, or pastime?
Has it not been written, “Retire early that
you may be refreshed?” Many times have
I gladly held thine eyelids down to give
thee warning, all unheeded by thee. How
many times when I am thine attendant at
the well-spread board I groan, for the cup
of boiling liquid is recklessly taken, which
retards digestion, and excites the brain and
body to inordinate action, only to leave it
more debilitated than before; your food
half masticated is left for your poor stom-
ach to labor with, and strong meats, rich
gravies, and many other things that divine
revelation has said are not good for man
are recklessly taken by thee; the night air
blows rudely on thy half-protected form,
but the tyrant fashion says, as others do
so you must. Many are the links that bind
me—fain would I touch the pale cheek with
rosy bloom, the wasted form with rounded
beauty, the pale lips with carmine, the
drooping eye with light of life, and cause
the chafing spirit to rejoice in the great
blessing of health. Maiden mine, I have
done.”
	For a time I mused—then thought found
words. And I have done all this, and yet I
thought to myself I served my God. Pa-
tiently must I regain, step by step, that
which folly has wrested from me, for well I
know that genius, ambition, power and
wealth are all as naught when deprived of
health, and let my prayer ascend on high,
and be answered on earth, that wisdom be
mine to know how to enjoy the blessings
which God is willing to bestow.
	Provo, Oct., 1878.	WINNIFRED.


[Column 1]
TRANSMISSION.

	Jean Ingelow, in her poem of "Margaret
at the Mere Side," tells, in her own sweet,
dreamy fashion, the story of a fair girl—a
woman in years, but dumb and always to be
a child in intellect—who sat hour by hour,
and day by day, "on the gray stone beneath
the sycamore",—innocent, wistful, sad, al-
ways waiting, always gazing down at the mere.
Her mother had come there a six months'
bride, to see her husband's boat go down the
stream, never to return;

"And she with weeping sore
Peered in the water-flags unceasingly.
*	*	*	*	*
And, after, desolate she sat alone
And mourned refusing to be comforted,
On the gray stone—the moss embroidered stone,
Till, when the days grew short, a child was born
To the dead father underneath the wave"

—A child whose dumb, pensive soul was, by
the subtle sympathy of nature, to be stamped
for life with a grief she never knew.
	Look at anothe picture—from Miss Phelps'
"Silent Partner"—a scene in the pinched, 
worn, driven life of New England factory
girls. Catty—poor, deaf, dumb, repulsive
Catty—had fallen asleep. "Her long fin-
gers moved a little, framing broken words.

[Column 2]
Even in her dreams, this miserable creature
bore about her a dull sense of denial and
distress. Even in her dreams, she listened 
for what she never heard, and spoke that
which no one understood.'
	'Mother used to say', said sip, under her
breath, 'that it was the noise.'
	'The noise?'
	'The noise of the wheels. She said they
beat about in her head. She came home o'
nights, and says to herself, "The baby 'll
never hear in this world, unless she hears
the wheels," and sure enough,' Sip lifted her
face to Perley's with a look of awe—'it is
true enough, that Catty hears the wheels,
but never anything beside.'"
	Cowan and Mrs. Duffey, and Joseph Cook,
in his grand, brave way, will tell us of scores
of wonders like this, but let us take just one
scene from Mrs. Kirby's "Transmission."
We see a young wife stung and tortured by
the daily desertion and faithlesness of him
who should have been her stay and comfort.
We see her patiently, bravely, resolutely
turning away from the sight and feeling of
this torment. We see her—and we think she 
must have asked Heaven's help—constantly
and firmly putting aside and treading down
every burning thought, every vengeful feel-
ing. She did this not for herself, but for the
sake of an innocent soul which she knew
would reflect her own. She had her reward 
in one who grew up before her, pure, sweet,
strong and calm. She had liften him above
dark and petty things into the bright, free
atmosphere of great souls.
	When will the awful significance of these
things dawn upo[-] men and women? When
shall the ideal mother come to her high vo-
cation carefully and reverently, as an artist
comes to her marbles, clothed with strength,
dowered with wisdom, and baptized with
purity and love? When shall she be upheld
and comforted by a husband as pure, as rev-
erent, as herself?
	Do we see all the meaning of Miss Phelps'
ideal marriage? "With her, he is a crowned 
creature; with him she is a free one." Hus-
band of to-day, do you not see that so soon
as you enslave this free creature, your own
royalty, and that of your children, is gone?
So sure as you bind her down upon the altar
of a lower nature, there shall creep forth, out
of the blood of sacrificed womanhood a
swarm of evil creatures that shall look at
you and reproach you, out of the eyes of
your children. 		MARY L. GRIFFITH.
	Woman's Words.


A STANDING VOTE.

	In the address of the Executive Commit-
tee, reference is made to a "standing vote."
English women petition largely, sending in
each year over a quarter of a million of
names asking for the full right of woman
suffrage. But their method of securing
those names is very different from the way
we have worked in the past, and much less
laborious. At every woman suffrage meet-
ing those in favor are requested to atand up
and remain standing until counted. Then
the President and Secretary of the meeting
sign their own names to the petition, add-
ing, and so many others, giving the number.
For instance, if forty-nine persons stood up,
the President and Secretary would sign
their own names, adding, "and forty-nine
others." This is a much easier and more
expeditious way of getting signatures to pe-
titions, than the way the N. W. S. A. has
heretofore practiced, and is the plan recom-
mended by the Executive: Committee. Try
friends.

[Column 1]
ADDRESS

OF THE N. W. S. A. TO THE FRIENDS OF
EQUAL SUFFRAGE FOR WOMAN.

	In pursuance of instructions of the an-
nual meeting held in Rochester, N. Y.,
July 18, 1878, the Executive Committee
present to you the following satement of
work done and to be done during the 45th
Congress.

WORK DONE.

	Since the assembling of the first regular
session, Dec. 3d, 1877, forty thousand men
and women have petitioned Congress for a
16th amendment prohibiting States from
disfranchising United States citizens on ac-
count of sex. The signatures were obtained
by 1,020 women in 425 towns, 125 Congres-
sional districts, 35 States and five Territo-
ries, and duly presented to Congress by 55
Senators and 100 Representatives.
	Jan. 10th, 1878, Senator Sargent, of Cali-
fornia, introduced a joint resolution propos-
ing a 16th amendment for the enfranchis-
ment of woman.
	Jan. 11th and 12th the Senate Committee
reported adversely; their report is now on
the [-]alendar, subject to action at the next
session of Congress. The Senators endors-
ing it are Wadleigh, N. H.; McMillan,
Minn.; Saulsbury, Del.; Merriman, S. C.;
Hill, Ga., and Ingalls, Kansas. Those in
favor of the 16th Amendment, Senators
Hoar, Mass., Mitchell, Oregon and Camer-
on, Wis., are expected to bring in a favor-
able miority report at the opening of the
second session. December next.
	Feb. 5th, the House Judiciary Commit-
tee voted upon a resolution recommending
our proposed 16th amendment, which re-
sulted in a tie; Yeas, Lapham, N. Y.;
Lynde, Wis.; Frye, Me.; Butler, Mass.;
Conger, Mich. Nays, Knott, Ky.; Hart-
ridge, Ga.; Stenger, Penn.; McMahan,
Ohio; Culberson, Texas. Absent, Harris of
Virginia; who has declared himself open to
conviction.
	During the last session of Congress the
temperance women of the nation sent up a
petition of 30,000, praying for "Home Pro-
tection." Though asking to have their
opinions counted on the one question of
licensing the liquor traffic, we may right-
fully add them to our 40,000, thus making
us a 70,000 prayer for the national protec-
tion of women in their citizen's right to a
voice in the government.
	The bill admitting women to practice be-
fore the Supreme Court unanimously re-
ported by the Judiciary Committee and
passed in the House of Representatives by
a vote of 169 to 87, stands upon the Senate
calendar with adverse report of its Judi-
ciary Committee to be acted upon at the
coming session.
	The individual petitions of 300 women of
the various States, asking Congress to re-
move their political disabilities, received
no attention—were not even noticed by ad-
verse reports—while similar petitions from
nine disfranchised men were each promptly
acted upon, and their political rights re-
stored.

WORK TO BE DONE.

	1st. Let all who failed to send 16th
amendment petitions to Washington last
winter, sign, circulate and mail them, pre-
paid (letter postage, remember) to our Re-
cording Secretary, Ellen H. Shelden, No.
1011 M Street, N. W. Washington, D. C.,
on or before the 25th of December, 1878,
that they may be in the hands of Sena-
[Column 2]
tors Hoar, Mitchell and Cameron when they
present their minority report, which we
hope and expect will be in the first week
in December. Please note, friends, that
we are not asking you to roll up a new peti-
tion to a new Congress, but simply to get
all the new names you can to send to the
2d session of the same Congress to add to
the 40,000 presented last winter.
	2d. To the 70,000 petitions for woman's
right of representation now before the 45th
Congress, we wish to add the weight of the
opinions of the friends of woman suffrage
in the Legislatures of the several States
and Territories. To secure such expres-
sion, we ask the suffragists throughout the
country to petition their respective Legis-
latures, asking them to pass resolutions re-
commending Congress to submit a 16th
amendment proposition to the States.
	3d. We ask the officers of the National
Woman Suffrage Association, representing
the different States, to call conventions at
their Capital city early in the session of
their Legislature, and make special effort
to secure action on the 16th amendment
resolution.
	4th. We urge the friends, everywhere,
to call public meetings, get the best local
speakers, and at the close of each meeting
take a standing vote on our 16th amend-
ment question, duly count it and send two
reports thereof, signed by the President
and Secretary of the meeting to our Secre-
tary, Ellen H. Sheldon, No. 1011 M. Street,
N. W. Washington, D. C., for the presenta-
tion to Congress, and two to the State Vice
President of the N. W. S. A. for presenta-
tion to your Legislature.
	5th. Let the Temperance organizations
of the country secure a vote of their mem-
bers upon our question and send reports
thereof duly signed by their officers on be-
half of the number who desire the enfran-
chisement of women.
	6th. Let every woman send to one of
the Senators or Representatives of her State
an individual petition to Congress for the
removal of her own political disabilities.
	7th. Let women by the hundreds and
thousands each address the Senators and
Representatives of her State, both in Con-
gress and Legislature, asking them to vote
for the 16th amendment resolution. Such
direct personal appeals, by voice or letter
will have far more weight than the same
words in the form of a petition, and will
compel our law-makers to feel that we are
not discouraged by their indifference or ad-
verse reports, but instead, are stimulated
to more earnest and determined efforts to
secure national protection in our citizen's
right to vote.
	8th	*	*	*	*
We ask our friends to copy petitions
in ink for themselves, in a clear, legible
hand, obtain signatures immediately and
forward, postage paid, to the proper place
and person—those for Congress to the Sec-
retary of the N. W. S. A., Ellen H. Shel-
don; No. 1011 M. Street, N. W. Washington,
D. C., on or before Nov. 25, 1878; those for
the Legislature to the Vice Pres. of the
N. W. S. A. of your State, on or before Jan.
1st, 1879.
	9th. For the whole thirty years of our
woman suffrage work the one great hin-
drance has been lack of money to meet the
necessary expenses, and to-day it is no ex-
ception. We need money to publish tracts,
send out lecturers, hold conventions, and
for other manifold purposes. We therefore
urge our friends everywhere to send contri-
butions by draft on New York or by P. O.

[Column 3]
Order to our Treasurer, Ellen C. Sargent,
1733 DeSales St., Washington, D. C.
	10th. And last, To save the National
Woman Suffrage Association the labor and
expense of printing and posting the tens of
thousands calls, appeals and petitions, which
we have heretofore incurred, (the past
year, alone, at cost of $1,000) we urge all
wishing to co-operate with us to subscribe,
at once, for the "National Citizen and Ballot
Box, which will hereafter be our medium
of communication with friends and work-
ers.
	On behalf Executive Committee N. W.
S. A.
		SUSAN B. ANTHONY, Chairman.
	Rochester, Sept. 2, 1878.


SENSIBLE TALK FROM A GERMAN
PAPER.

	What shall we do with our daughters?
Give them a good school education; teach
them to cook healthy food. Teach them to
wash, to iron, to mend stockings, to sew on
buttons, to maks their own clothes and a
well-fitting shirt. Teach them to bake; to
know that good cooking saves medicine.
Teach them that a dollar is worth one hun-
dred cents; that only those are saving who
spend less than they receive, and that what-
ever more is spent tends to impoverish.
Teach them that they are much better
dressed in strong cotton garments than in
silk, if they are in debt. Teach them that
one round, full face is worth more than
fifty beautiful consumptive ones. Teach
them to wear strong shoes. Teach them to
make good purchases, and see to the reck-
oning of their accounts. Teach them that
they spoil God's image when they lace
tightly. Teach them good common-sense,
confidence, self-reliance and industry.
Teach them to do garden work and enjoy
nature. Teach them likewise, if they have
money enough, music, painting and all arts,
remembering that these things are second-
ary. Teach them that walking is much
better than riding, and that wild flowers
are very beautiful to those who observe
them. Teach them to despise all make-
believes, and that one should say yes or 
no, when one really means it. Teach them
that happiness in marriage depends neither
upon the station nor the wealth of the hus-
band, but upon his character.
	If you have taught your daughters all
this, and made them understand it fully,
then let them when the time comes, marry
in perfect confidence; they will be sure to
find their way without further assistance.—
Colorado Farmer.


WORSE THAN NONE.—A bad husband is
infinitely worse than none. Girls, never
marry a fellow who is ashamed to carry a
bundle; who lies in bed until breakfast
and until his father has opened his shop,
or office, and swept it out; who frequents
taverns, bowling-saloons, prize fights, etc.;
who owes his tailor, shoemaker, washerwo-
man, jeweler, barber, printer and landlady,
and never pays his debts; who is always
talking about his acquaintances, and con-
demning them; whose tongue is always
running about nonsense; who thinks he is
the greatest man in the neighborhood, and
yet whom every one despises and shuns.
We say never marry a fellow with all or
any of these qualifications, no matter how
handsome he is, or how agreeable he can
make himself on occasions. He will make
a bad husband.—Waverly Magazine.

PRIMARY ASSOCIATIONS.

	Dear Editor:—According to your instructions
my First Counselor and myself visited the 
houses, and a[-]certained the number of chil-
dren between the ages of four and ten to be
one hundred and four.
	We held our First Primary Meeting on 
Thursday; the 24th inst., at the school house;
there were fifty-two names enrolled, viz.
twenty-five girls and twenty-seven boys, and 
we expect more.
	Bishop Wm. Price met with us; we opened
our meeting by singing and prayer, after which
I asked the children some questions, and if
they remembered what Sister Zina taught
them; which I found they did.
	Bishop Price then talked to them a while;
among other things he told them they must
be orderly and obedient. I then asked them to
rise and thank the Bishop for what he had
said, which they did by bowing and saying
"Thank you, sir." I then appointed a little
boy as monitor for the boys' side, and a little
girl likewise to the girls.
	Sister M. A. Till spoke a few words, by re-
quest; Counselor Lucy Taylor followed. After
which I appointed the meetings for Saturdays,
at 3 o'clock p. m. precisely. Closed by singing
"Gleaners in the harvest field," and Lucy
Taylor dismissed by prayer.
	The names of the officers are as follows:

	Sarah Roberts, President.
	Lucy Taylor,			} Counselors,
	Mary Ann Gardner,	}
	M. A. Till, Secretary,
	Maggie Higginson, Ass't Sec'y.
		Very respectfully,
				SARAH ROBERTS, President.
						M. A. TILL, Secretary.
	Go[-]hen, Oct. 20, 1878.


THE SIXTH Annual Congress of the Asso-
ciation for the advancement of women was
held in Providence, R. I., Oct. 9 and 10,
Mrs. Kate M. Doggett, of Chicago, presid-
ing, "charming her audience by her genial
yet dignified manner." There were present
many noted workers in woman's pregres-
sion. There were both day and evening
sessions, and papers were read from cele-
brated writers and thinkers on popular sub-
jects of interest, and time given to discuss-
ing their merits. The notices we have
seen of this Congress agree in pronouncing
it a great success.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 7.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, NOV. 15, 1878.		No. 12.


[For the EXPONENT.
ALONE WITH MY CONSCIENCE.

	I sat alone with my conscience
		In a place where time had ceased,
	And we talked of my former-living,
		Where endless years increase.

	And I knew I should have to answer
		The questions it put to me:
	And to face the questions and answers
		Throughout an eternity.

	The ghosts of forgotten actions
		Came floating before my sight,
	And things I thought were dead things
		Were alive, with terrible might.

	And the vision of my past life
		Was an awful thing to face,
	Sitting alone with my conscience,
		In that solemn and silent place.

	And I thought of a far-away warning,
		Of a sorrow that was to be mine,
	In a land that was in the future;
		And now is the present time.

	And I thought of former reflections,
		What the judgment day would be,
	But sitting alone with my conscience
		Seemed judgment enough for me.

	And I wondered as to the future,
		In this land beyond the grave;
	But no one spake an answer,
		No one came forward to save.

	Then I felt that the future was present,
		And the present would never go by;
	For the awful weight of my past life
		Became an eternity.

	Then I woke from my timely dreaming,
		And the vision passed away;
	And I knew the far off warning
		Was a warning of yesterday.

	And I pray I may not forget it;
		In this land before the grave,
	That I may not call in the future,
		And no one come to save.

	And so I have learned a lesson
		I ought to have known before;
	And although I learned it dreaming,
		I hope to forget no more.
								MARY WRAY.
S. L. City, Sunday; Oct. 20, 1878.


[Column 1]
	[For the EXPONENT.
	THE INTEGRITY OF RUTH.

	What Christian is there of whatever
creed who has not admired the character of
Ruth? She is immortalized for her con-
stancy to her mother, Naomi, and for her
devotion to her husband's people.  The
Christian women all over the civilized
world hold her up as a model; and repre-
sentations of her beauty, her sweetness, her
grace, are given in many ways and forms;
and her strong affections so impress them-
selves upon womankind, that Ruth is really
one of the most striking illustrations to be
given characterizing Christian women.
But what if to day any woman should en-
act the part of Ruth literally, and insist as
she did, and as Naomi, her mother-in-law,
did, on the strict performance of the law
being carried out with reference to kinship;
why, the modern world would be horrified
and would want special legislation brought
to bear—because it would be "so degrad-
ing they could not possibly endure it any
longer."  Fortunately for Ruth, she died
thousands of years ago, for if she had lived
to day she would have been cast out as
evil; for in this highly enlightened Nine-
teenth century let no woman dare be as

[Column 2]
brave and heroic, or as self-sacrificing and
unselfish as Ruth, or they will be outcast
and despised by these LOVERS OF PURITY (?).
	We are told by Christian teachers in the
world to imitate the examples of the illus-
trious women of the Scriptures; but let
any one do so in reality, the finger of scorn
will be pointed at them.  What does it
mean? That these are women of a type
and class only to be cherished as a thing of
the past, but never re-produced?  Then
why are they models, and how are we to be
benefitted by their example, if we may not
imitate it?  Who does not know that Ruth
not only was anxious to become the wife of
Boaz, but that she, instructed by Naomi,
actually wooed him herself, and that in a
way and manner which to modern fashion-
able women would be entirely unwomanly
and lacking in modesty.  And yet all good
Christians reverence the character of Ruth;
they emblemize her in poetry, in prose, in
painting and in statuary.
	I would advise all those ladies who feel
so constrained to abolish "Plural Marriage"
to re-read the history of Ruth, Sarah, Han-
nah, Rachel, Leah, and the holy women
of old. I would call their attention to the
10th and 11th verses of the 3d chapter of
Ruth:
	"Blessed be thou of the Lord, my daugh-
ter; for thou hast shewed more kindness in
the latter end than in the beginning, inas-
much as thou followedst not young men,
whether poor, or rich.
	"And now, my daughter, fear not; I will
do to thee all that thou requirest; for all
the city of my people doth know that thou
art a virtuous woman."
	It is evident from what is written that
the whole conversation was not given, for
it seems Boaz comprehended that she de-
sired marriage with him.  How very in-
discreet was the widow Ruth, young and
fair and full of grace, and abounding in af-
fection as she was, to wash and anoint her-
self, and put on her raiment, and go and
lie down at the feet of a man and actually
propose to him that he take her to wife.
	Would not modern society be shocked by
the conduct of any young woman who did
the works of Ruth?  And don't you think
that Ruth should have been compelled to
go before the Grand Jury, or a Court of
Justice, and tell them why she washed and
anointed herself (it is apparent that she
had an object in doing so) and what sort of
raiment she put on when she went and laid
down at Boaz feet?
	However, the Lord blessed this peculiar
marriage, and through this lineage came
the Christ, the blessed Savior of the world.
	Does any one suppose that a man as true
and just and exact in observing the law of
kinship in regard to posterity would him-
self be willing to die without? Ruth's child
was hers and her mother Naomi's, if we
may be allowed to understand the Scrip-
tures for ourselves.  Ruth understood the
law of Celestial Marriage, and was willing
to be obedient thereunto, and she even con-
descended to marry an old man.  But we
must beware how we follow Ruth's exam-
ple, only we may talk of her integrity, her
purity, her constancy, and down to the end
of time we may quote her words so touch-
ing and beautiful, "Whither thou goest, I

[Column 3]
will go, and where thou lodgest I will lodge;
thy people shall be my people, and thy God
my God."  Rest assured, my Christian
friends, that Ruth knew Boaz to be a man
of God, a man bound by the law of God in
holy and solemn covenants to protect virtue
and chastity in woman; and I think that
all those who are so outraged by the prac-
tice of plural marriage among the Mor-
mons, had better cease holding up as
models the characters and works of holy
women of old.
	In ancient times, we are told, women
ministered in Temples, as Anna the proph-
etess who went not out day nor night.
Does any of our wise men and women
know what she was doing there?  What
business had women in the Temple?  In
fact, what business had those people to
build Temples at all? they must have "cost
enormous sums of money."
	And how dare men in these days to read
in the Bible of the great deeds and works
of women, those pure women with whom
angels conversed; women were judges and
prophetesses in those days.  Is it not a
wonder that their mouths were not stopped?
It is exceedingly lucky for them that they
lived so long ago, for had they dared to do
such things in these days they would have
been called to account.  But what of the
prophecy in the Book of Joel, 2d chap.,
28th verse;
	"And it shall come to pass afterwards
that I will pour out my spirit upon all flesh;
and your sons and your daughters shall
prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams
and your young men shall see visions."
	Don't you think that Christian ministers
will be alarmed when the young women
begin to prophesy, and will it not be very
unwomanly, and entirely contrary to Mad-
ame Grundy?  I'm very much afraid it
will.  And the newspapers and the jour-
nals will write them up as very indecent
young women, because they have dared to
step out of their sphere; for certainly
prophesying is no more a part of house-
keeping than lecturing or preaching is.
	All honor and praise to Ruth for her in-
tegrity to the law of God, and honor and
praise to the noble women  to the law of God,
though persecution wax strong against
them and their names be cast out as evil.
	AUNT EM.


"QUICK AS LIGHTNING."

	Lightning travels nearly a million times
faster than thunder. The speed of light-
ning is so great that it would go 480 times
round the earth in a minute. Whereas the
sound of thunder would go scarcely thir-
teen miles in the same space of time.
Thunder will take a second to travel 380
yards, hence a popular method of approxi-
mating the distance of a thunder cloud is
as follows: Immediately you see the light-
ning flash, put your hand upon your pulse
and count how many times it beats before
you hear the thunder; if it beats six pulsa-
tions the storm is a mile off, if twelve pul-
sations it is two miles off, and so on. In
the case of elderly people five pulsations
would measure nearly the same period of
time as six in the case of young people.

		BRIGHAM CITY, ARIZONA,

						Oct. 6, 1878.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	The Relief Society of this place was or-
ganized June 23, 1878, by our worthy
Bishop, George Lake and Counselors, with
the following officers:

	Matilda P. Killian, President,
	Morilla Bates,	} Counselors,
	Martha Stratton,	}
	Mary A. Adams,	} Secretaries,
	Annie Woods,	}
	Mary K. Overson, Treasurer

	There were also eight Teachers chosen.
	The Society numbers twenty-nine mem-
bers. As we are living in the UNITED OR-
DER we have no poor to look after, but there
are many ways that we can do good; we
can look after the sick and take care of
them, and help one another in various ways.
We feel anxious to do something to for-
ward the work which we are engaged in.
It is a general time of health with us, for
which we feel truly grateful to our Heav-
enly Father, as well as for every other
blessing we receive from His bountiful
hand.
	Ever praying for the welfare of the
Daughters of Zion, we remain your sisters
in the Covenant of Truth,
			M. P. KILLIAN, Pres't.
			MARY A. ADAMS, Sec'y.
