WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		201


TRIBUTE TO SISTER AMANDA SMITH.

Dear Sister Amanda, we meet here to-day,
With hearts full of love, a small tribute to pay
To your worth and nobility, knowing full well
That the whole of the truth only angels could tell.

		*		*		*		*		*		*

You have honored God's laws 'mid oppression and strife,
When mobs in their fury were threat'ning your life,
When husband and loved ones were slain by your side,—
You bore all in meekness, for God was your guide.

		*		*		*		*		*		*

Repeatedly driven from the homes they possessed,
You have willingly shar'd with the sorely oppressed'
Rejoicing that you were deemed worthy to go;
You said "They're my people and my faith I will show.

"I'll follow them even 'mid deserts to roam;
Where'er their abiding place there is my home."
And bravely you traveled o'er mountain and plain.
Determined through all things the right to maintain.

Then yon struggled and toiled to create a new home
Where the wild beast and savage were erst wont to roam,
Rejoicing the while that the Saints were made free
And could worship the Lord in such sweet liberty.

A blessing you've been to the daughters of light,
For your influence ever 'has been for the right;
With words of encouragement cheered many hearts,
The memory of which will never depart.

Your children you've trained in the ways of the Lord,
By example and precept you've taught them His word;
Now they're beacons in Zion, an honor to you,
Beloved by the good for integrity true.

And multiplied thousands will yet speak your praise,
For your trust and obedience to God and His ways;
Like Jesus, your motto has been and is still,
"Not my will be done, but O Father Thy will.

And now we all pray that the blessings of heaven
May rest down upon you "from morn until even,"
May your life be prolonged 'til your work is all through,
Then you be crowned queen with the righteous and true.

				With Love Sincere,
								MARY A. FREIZE.

	The 22d of February last was the Birth-
day of Sister Amanda Smith, when she
reached the goodly age of three score and
ten. A select company of her sisters went
as a surprise party to her house, and carry-
ing with them many good things, as well
as blessings for this aged sister, made the
occasion an enjoyable one.
	It is well known to the Saints that Sister
Smith has passed through much persecution
in times past, which has enfeebled her
health. Her husband and son were both
killed, and another son left for dead, at
Haun's Mill Massacre, in Missouri.


[Column 1]
THE PILGRIM MOTHERS.

WHAT MRS. BLAKE SAYS ABOUT THE NEW
ENGLAND DINNER. NO MENTION MADE
OF PLYMOUTH'S FOREMOTHERS—SENA-
TOR BLAINE'S WANT OF JUSTICE—ROSE
STANDISH IGNORED.

	There was recently given in this city a
grand dinner in honor of the landing of the
Pilgrims and the colonization of New Eng-
land. To this dinner came some two hun-
dred men. At this dinner various speeches
were made, and from the beginning to the
end of the feast there was not a word said
to indicate that women had any existence
in the world, or were in any way concerned
in that famous landing on the bleak Ply-
mouth rock two centuries and a half ago.

THE LADIES IN THE SIDE ROOM.

	It is true that the papers inform us that
after the material elements were disposed
of a few ladies were admitted into a side
room, where they remained throughout the
evening, being regarded, no doubt, by the
gentlemen present, as they sat there with
their elegant dresses and fair faces, as an
agreeable ornament of the occasion, like the

[Column 2]
flowers and the banners. How kind and
generous it was, by the way, to permit these
ladies to come "after the dinner was all
over." Doubtless, those wives and daugh-
ters of the men present eat a simple meal
at home, while their husbands and fathers
were gorging themselves with dainties,
and then meekly accepted not even the
crumbs that were cast from the table, not
even one glass of wine, but were permitted
to sit, hungry and silent, while the men
vaunted themselves and their ancestry.

IGNORING WOMEN.

	It is true that Secretary Sherman in the
course of his speech, as an illustration, said
that "the nation's faith should be like the
chastity of a woman above suspicion," it
must hardly be said, that in presence of
the gentlemen there assembled, he did not
speak of the "chastity of man," since such
a remark would probably have been looked
upon as a personal allusion by every man
present. With this single exception, how-
ever, a diligent perusal of the speeches has
failed to discover the slightest hint of the
existence, past or present, of the female
half of our people. A great deal was said
of the hardy men who colonized the land,
of the struggles of the men who built up
the country, and in reading these after din-
ner efforts one would actually think that a
race of grim masculine creatures had really
settled, populated and adorned New Eng-
land without the aid of a solitary woman.
Mr. James G. Blaine capping the climax of
forgetfulness by speaking of the hardships
endured by the men and declaring that
these "21,000 men were the progenitors" of
a great race! As if even in the matter of
increasing the census and raising families
men were quite independent of all feminine
assistance.

OUR FOREMOTHERS.

	In point of fact these 21,000 men were
hal[-] women; of the 101 persons who landed
from the Mayflower only forty-one were
men, and the hardships, the sufferings, the
devotion of these pilgrim mothers forms
the most touching part of that romance of
the landing of the pilgrims. It was the
wealthy wife of Edward Winslow whose
money enabled him to embark in the May-
flower, while the fair Rose Standish, who
left a noble home to share the hardships of
her husband, deserves a gentle remem-
brance from all who honor womanly fidelity.
Mrs. Carver, who was one of the first victims
of the heroism of seeking freedom in a for-
eign land, while to the sterling qualities of
Ann Brewster the colonists owed much of
their care and energy. Yet not one of those
heroines had the slightest mention from the
men who assembled to do honor to the
memory of that handful of brave men, and
women equally brave: women who, indeed,
sacrificed more than their husbands, inas-
much as home and home-comforts are
dearer and more important to women than
to men, while their frailer natures cause
them to suffer more in enduring hardships
than their more stalwart companions.

THE DAUGHTERS OF THE PILGRIMS.

	They were all forgotten, however, since
women count for so little in the public esti-
mation of men, though important enough
in their private lives! Not only were these
grand mothers of a noble race ignored, but
the great women of to-day, their worthy
descendants, were unmentioned. Invita-
tions were sent to all the distinguished
New England men to take part in the feast,
but the daughters of the Pilgrims were not

[Column 3]
noticed. No woman, whatever her high
achievements, had any recognition. Har-
riet Beecher Stowe and Lydia Maria Child
were not included among the representa-
tives of literature; Harriet Hosmer had no
word of honorable mention in art, and even
"Gail Hamilton," who certainly deserved a
seat beside the brother-in-law whose cause
she has so ably defended—even she had no
invitation.

ABIGAIL ADAM AND MERCY WARREN.

	The women who, in the past, by their
wealth and devotion founded the Plymouth
colony; the women who, like Abigail
Adams and Mercy Warren, adorned by the
lustre of their virtues the history of New
England; the women whose genius to-day
carries its fame to lands which never heard
of most of the men who took part in this
dinner; all were forgotten, and only as a
side show was a handful of well-dressed
ladies permitted to look on, while men ate
and drank and lauded their own and their
male ancestor's virtues. Thus did "the sons
of New England honor Forefathers' Day,"
in the very name of the feast insulting the
daughters of New England and the memory
of their foremothers.
			LILLIE DEVEREUX BLAKE.


R. S. REPORT.

	Quarterly Conference of the Relief Socie-
ties of the Juab County Stake, held Jan.
20th, 1879. Members of the Board all
present, also Patriarch J. G. Bigler, Bishop
Andrews and Elder Kendall. Meeting
opened by singing and prayer. Minutes of
last Conference read and accepted, also Fi[-]
nancial reports. The officers of the Stake
were presented and unanimously sustained,
also the local officers of Nephi, Mona and
Levan.
	Pres. Amelia Goldsbrough said: "I am
thankful for the opportunity of meeting
with you and am pleased to see so many of
my sisters here; I am also pleased that the
brethren have favored us with a visit. I
with my sisters am trying to the best of my
ability to promote the interest of the Soci-
ety. The sisters are doing a great work
feeding the poor, comforting and waiting
on the sick, clothing the living and the
dead, and I feel to bless them. I wish my
sisters who understand silk culture would
speak on that subject." Solicited the aid
of the brethren in building a granary to
store grain in. "I pray God that I may be
counted worthy to enjoy a celestial glory;
it was for that object that I came to this
land and am trying to work out my salva-
tion in connection with you all, which may
God grant for Christ's sake. Amen."
	Mrs. Eliza Sommerville, Pres. of Mona
Society, said: "I am pleased to meet with
you, and gladly embrace the opportunity of
representing the Mona Society. Since the
new organization of the Society by Pres.
Teasdale we are doing well; our meetings
are well attended and a good spirit pre-
vails."
	Bishop Andrews spoke very encouraging-
ly, and said, he would do all in his power to
aid in building the granary. Bros. Bigler,
Kendall and Allen gave some excellent in-
structions.
	Singing. Patriarch J. G. Bigler blessed
the Society, by virtue of his office, and dis-
missed the meeting.
		A. GOLDSBROUGH, Pres.
		M. PITCHFORTH, Sec'y.

SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 1, 1879.


[Column 1]
	THE WASHINGTON CONVENTION.

	We have no wise to tire our readers by dwell-
ing too much or too ardently upon our visit to
Washington, but as we have never given a 
summary of the proceedings of the convention
in order, and as it is a subject in which women
who hold the franchise should have a deep in-
terest, we propose now to take the convention in
order, commencing with the preliminary meet-
ing of the Executive Committee, held at Mrs. B.
A. Lockwood's on Wednesday evening, Jan. 8.
At this meeting the programme for two days
and evening sessions was all arranged.  Miss
Susan B. Anthony, Chairman of Executive
Committee read the call for the Convention,
and after a thorougu discussion upon the sub-
ject in question the following committees were
appointed, &c.:  Committee on Credentials,
Matilda Joslyn Gage, Elizabeth Oakes Smith,
Caroline B. Winslow; Committee on Resolu-
tions, Sara Andrews Spencer, Matilda Joslyn
Gage, Emmeline B. Wells, Helen M. Cool[--]
and Belva A. Lockwood; Committee on Fi-
nance, Ellen Clarke Sargent, Helen M. Slo-
cum, Julia B. Dunham, Zina Young Williams
and Ellen H. Sheldon; Committee on Pro-
gramme, Susan B. Anthony, Lilliee Devereux
Blake, Marilla M. Ricker.  Morning session—
Opening address, Elizabeth Cady Stanton; re-
port of the Committe on Frinance. Afternoon ses-
sion—Speeches by Lillie Devereux Blake; Eliza-
beth Oakes Smith, Lavinia C. Dundore and
Caroline B. Winslow.  Evening session—
Speeches from Emmeline B. Wells and Susan
B. Anthony.  Friday morning—General dis-
cussion on Utah and Wyoming—United States
Rights vs. States Rights, to be discussed by
Mesdames Gage, Stanton, Williams, Smith and
others. Afternoon session—Speeches by Helen
M. Slocum, Zina Young Williams and Sara
Andrews Spencer.  Evening session—Speeches
by Belva A. Lockwood, Frederic Douglas and
Elizabeth Cady Stanton.
	This was the original programme as laid out
by the Executive Committe; and here let us
say that this committee know how to make
the most of their time, and possess in a large
degree the requisite qualifications for dispatch-
ing business in an orderly and systematic man-
ner.  Women who travel hundreds and thou-
sands of miles for the sole purpose of laboring
in these Conventions are not the ones to waste
must time in dilly-dallying.
	The first morning of the convention was
very dark, and the rain literally poured down
in torrents, the wind blew furiously, and it
seemed as if the elements had combined to-
gether to show their disrespect.  However, a
tolerably fair audience was convened in Lin-
coln Hall just before eleven o'clock.  Mrs.
Stanton, who presided with dignity and grace,
called the convention to order.  Upon the plat-

[Column 2]
form were Elizabeth Cady Stanton, of Tenna-
ny, New Jersey, President, Susan B. Anthony,
of Rochester, New York, Matilda Joslyn Gage,
Editor of the "National Citizen," from Fay-
etteville, N. Y., Sara Andrewa Spencer, of
Washington, Editor of "Woman's Words,"
Lillie Devereux Blake, of New York, connect-
ed with the "Daily Evening Telegram," Caro-
line B. Winslow, M. D., Editor of the "Alpha"
at Washington, Helen M. Slocum and Helen
M. Cooke, of New York City, Belva A. Lock-
wood, lawyer of Washington, Marilla M. Rick-
er, of New Hampshire, and Lavinia C. Dun-
dore, of Maryland, also lawyers, Elizabeth
Oakes Smith, the poet and author, from North
Carolina, Julia Brown Dunham, of Iowa, Han-
nah M. Sheppard, of Washington, Misses Julia
and Rachel Foster, of Philadelphia, Mrs. Zina
Young Williams, of Utah, and your humble
servant Mrs. E. B. Wells, Editor WOMAN'S
EXPONENT, Salt Lake City.  Many delegates
from other States and Territories were scattered
through the audience.  From time to time
during the two days' session many other dis-
tinguished ladies and a few gentlemen (woman
suffrage advocates) were seated upon the plat-
form.
	First business was the reading of the call by
Miss Anthony, with a few appropriate com-
ments.  Next Mrs. Lockwood read the list of
the committees; these were confirmed and the
programme adopted.  Mrs. Stanton prefaced
her address by saying the heavens were weep-
ing over woman's wrongs, and the weather was
in keeping with the woman's rights conven-
tion, to which we would have added and while
heaven wept for woman Old Boreas, king of
the wind, with mighty power blew a loud
blast, strong and deep, for the lords of creation.
Mrs. Stanton then proceeded to deliver her elo-
quent speech in the most easy and graceful
manner.  She seems to possess in a great de-
gree the power of pleasing her hearers, though
she tells them many plain, unvarnished truths,
yet it is all done without the least apparaent de-
sire to offend the most sensitive. Mrs. Stanton
has not in the least degree the quality of sharp-
ness, not perhaps in its usual significance, but
in the sense in which it is applied to women
speakers and lecturers.  She commenced by
saying that for eleven consecutive years women
citizens of the United States had been in the
habit of assmebling in Washington to ask that
the principles of our government may be car-
ried out, making women equal with men before
the law.
	At some future time we will publish some of
these speeches in full, that our readers may
have the benefit of the expressed opinions of
some of these leading ladies in the woman
suffrage movement.
	Mrs. Sara Andrews Spencer next presented
the resolutions, which we published in our last
issue.  Upon motion the resolutions were tak-
en up separately to be discussed and voted up-
on.  Objections to some points were made by
some of the ladies and gentlemen present,
which occupied the morning hour.
	In the afternoon a much larger audience was
present.  Mrs.  Lillie Devereux Blake was the
first speaker.  Mrs. Blake is shrewd, witty and
sarcastic; she is well suited to the platform,
indeed she is perfectly at home in addressing
an audience.  Her subject was "The Propriety
of Abolishing Women."  She argued that
since woman had so often been ignored she
might as well be abelished, and all further
trouble saved by taken them to the end of the
world and dropping them off.  Judging by
present indications she thought it better to
abolish women altogether, and added that per-
haps some ingenious Yankee would invent a

[Column 3]
new kind of woman that would suit men bet-
ter; she said in this connection that the suc-
cessors of the noble progenitors of the race
spoken of by Mr. Blaine surely will be able to
hatch a new being without the help of woman.
(Loud bursts of applause.
	Mrs. Elizabeth Oakes Smith read an article
entitled "Biology and Woman's Rights." She
reads extremely well, has a very sweet, low
voice and a charmning manner; her reading was
frequently applauded.
	Mr. Frederic Douglas, by invitation of Mrs.
Stanton, made a few remarks suitable to the
occasion, the audience manifesting their plea-
sure of applauding him freely.
	Mrs. Stanton then announced as a committee
to wait upon President Hayes and inform him
of the existence of 20,000,000 women citizens in
the United States, which fact he failed to rec-
ognize in his recent message. Mrs. Elizabeth
Oakes Smith, Mrs. Matilda Joslyn Gage and
Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells.  Miss Anthony then
made some very clever, cutting and humorous
remarks.  Mrs. Dundore then addressed the
convention. In her remarks she said hers was
a guerilla warfare, she was bound by no parties
or methods.
	Evening session.  The rain poured in tor-
rents, the wind blew fiercely, and yet the hall
was crowded in every part.  Mrs. Wells, of
Utah, was the first speaker.  Miss Anthony
followed, delivering her very celebrated lecture,
"Bread and the Ballot."  She is a very earnest
woman and impresses every one with her indi-
viduality.  She was frequently applauded, and
certainly made some strong arguments and
good hits.  Mrs. Wells made a few more re-
marks.
	Friday morning.  Mrs. Blake made a few
remarks, and introduced Mrs. Helen M. Cooke,
who read the report of the suffrage association
of New York city.
	Dr. Cora Bland then spoke on the growth of
liberty, and began with the assertion that his-
tory teaches us in all ages that man is a natural
brute. After this there was some discussion
upon the resolutions, and Mrs. Gage stated
that Pres. Hayes had mentioned one woman
in his message, because she was the widow of 
some man.
	Mrs. Spencer related the interiew with the
President when Mrs. Sargent and herself had
called upon him asking him to say a good word
for women in his first message, and again in
the second.  They then asked if the bill re-
moving the poltiical disabilities of women
should pass, would he approve it.  "Probably."
"Would he indicate his friendliness in his mes-
sage?"  He could not tell; it was a huge ques-
tion, requiring great deliberation.  Mrs. Spen-
cer concluded by saying, "You see he did not
know what to say."  Whereupon Miss Antho-
ny remarked that men didn't know which
was the safe side of the fence.
	Hon. A. G. Riddle, a Washington lawyer,
then addressed the convention; his subject was
suffrage, and he spoke to the point.  He closed
by saying that the emancipation of woman
was as inevitable as any other fixed fact that is
foreshadowed.
	Mrs. Stanton made some remarks upon her
labors at the beginning of the movement; and
her husband's protest, Lucretia Mott, the sweet
Quaker lady, saying, "Now, Elizabeth, don't
thee make the whole thing ridiculous by say-
ing that the women want to vote, I beg thee."
She asked Fred Douglas to urge the resolutions
as she had not then learned to put three words
together before an audience, and the resolu-
tion was carried.
	Marshall Douglas then came forward and
gave a brief account of the first woman's con


vention. At the close he asked what he would
have been without the ballot. By it he was
made Marshall of the the District of Columbia,
and then recited in an impressive manner a 
poem upon liberty. Mrs. Zina Young Williams
of Utah was then introduced, and proceeded
to address the audience. Mrs. Wells and Mrs.
Spencer, each made a few remarks and the
morning session adjourned.
			To be continued. [handwritten] E. B. [---]

onhanci....',....="" -29="" 50="" '‘i="" l="" ja="" -j0="" iriopcrtyio="" u="">	RELIEF SOCIETY NOTICE.—The secretaries
of the various branches of the Society of this
Stake, are respectfully requested to send in
their reports to the EXPONENT Office before
the 15th of March, 1879. By order of

			M. ISABELLA HORNE, President.
			E. HOWARD, Sec'y.

	NOTICE TO SECRETARIES.—The Relief Socie-
ty is increasing in importance and usefulness
in the work of the Latter days, and for this
reason it should be the ambition of every sec-
retary in every ward in this Stake to prepare
and furnish to the Relief Society Secretary of
of the Stake, proper and business-like reports
invariably four days before the Quarterly Con-
ference, so as to enable her to prepare and cor-
rect her report to present before the general
Quarterly Conference. It seems impossible to 
do so when several of the wards are not heard
from, which has been the case heretofore.
	It is very important that the Secretaries at-
tend to this responsible duty. They will please
send their reports for the last quarter address-
ed to the Relief Society Secretary for the
Stake, in care of Mrs. Wells, Editor of WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT, before the 15th of March.
						E. HOWARD, Sec'y.

MISS ELIZA R. Snow, President of Latter-day
Saint Women's Organizations left Salt Lake
City Wednesday morning, Feb. 5th, to visit
the different organizations of Weber Co. and
assist in organizing Y. L. M. I. A. and Pri-
mary Associations in various places.
	Ogden has recently been divided into four
wards, and Miss Snow, while there, assisted
the Bishops in their respective wards and Mrs.
Jane S. Richards, President of the Relief Socie-
ties in Weber Co. to organize Y. L. M. I. and 
Primary Associations in each of these wards.
Miss Snow and Mrs. Richards afterwards visit-
ed Lynne, Marrlott, Riverdale, Mound's Fort,
Huntsville and West Weber; and assisted the
Bishops in organizing a Primary Association
in each of these respective settlements. At
Riverdale these sisters also assisted the Bishop
in organizing a Y. L. M. I. A. Miss Snow 
was absent over two weeks, and held during
that time, seventeen meetings, at all of which
she occupied considerable time in speaking,
exhorting and giving instruction to the sisters
in their various duties and callings; her visits
are of great benefit, and her age, experience,
and her illustrious example of Christian life
strengthen the force of her advice and have a 
bearing upon all her labors, which 
makes it more effective. Our sisters should
uphold and sustain "Sister Eliza" as she is af-
fectionatly designated, that she may live long
to direct and counsel the mothers in Israel, and
daughters of Zion. On the 21st day of Jan.
she attained to the advanced age of seventy-
five, her life stretching over three quarters of 
a century, still active mentally and physisic-
ally, waits upon herself, keeping no servant,
choosing to do her own work. She has ad-
ministered as Preistess for many years in the
"Lord's House." She travels from place to 
place, as duty calls her to attend the meetings

[Column 2]
and conferences of the sisters in all the various
localities in the Territory, never refusing
when it is po[-]sible for her to go. We feel con-
fident our sisters will join with us in publicly
expressing our gratitude to this dearly beloved
sister, and wishing her many more years of
usefulness and active work as a leader among
the women of the Latter-day Saints.

	ON THURSDAY, Feb. 27, we attended the joint
Conference in Farmington of Young Men's
Mutual Improvent Associations and Young
Ladies' Mutual Improvement Associations
of Davis Co. Stake, in company with Pres.
Eliza R. Snow and Elder Junius F. Wells.
The young men's reports were made in the
morning. Elder Roberts, who presides over
the Y. M. M. I. A. in that Stake, conducted
the meeting; there was not a large attendance.
After singing and prayer reports from the va-
rious branches of the Association were read by
the Secretary. The presidents of the several
branches reported verbally the condition of
their respective Associations, and then Elder
Junius F. Wells, President of Central Com-
mittee of Y. M. M. I. A., occupied the time in
an interesting discourse, giving instruction
and exhorting the young people. Elder Wells
is a very pleasing speaker, has an excellent
voice, a good flow of language, in short he is
winning, attractive and graceful in speaking.
In the afternoon Mrs. Nancy A. Clark, Super-
intendent of the young ladies of Davis Co.,
presided. County report;
Officers, in the aggregate..........................  63
Members...... ..............................................728
								      ——
Total								791
Average attendance and average meetings not
reported. Meetings are generally conducted
under the direction of a programme committee.
The reading mostly consists in reading standard
Church works, WOMAN'S EXPONENT and Juve-
nile Instructor. One library is owned by the
young ladies. 2207 Bible verses reported to
have been read by the members during the
quarter.

The Primary Association of Farmington was
represented by its President, Mrs. Aurelia S.
Rogers. The presidents of Young Ladies' So-
cieties each reported verbally, and the Super-
intendent spoke and prophesied many good
things to the young in Zion. Pres. E. R.
Snow made some truly good remarks, and we
talked a little about our visit to Washington.


OBITUARY

	At Marriottsville, Weber County, U. T. Feb.
4th, 1879. of Typhoid fever. Annie daughter
of Simon F. and Turbor Halvorson aged 17
years. Sister Annie was a girl of amiable dis-
position, possessing excellent traits of charac-
ter, she was a teacher in our Sunday School,
and an efficient member of the Y. L. M. I. A.
She was above reproach, and was respected by
all. The funeral services were conducted by
Bishop James Richie and Counselors at the
residence of her parents in a very impressive
manner, after which almost the entire ward
followed her rem[-]ins to their last resting
place, in Ogden City Cemet[-]ry. Her death
has cast a gloom [-]ver our neighborhood. May
she rest in peace.—Com.

	Died at Marriottsville, Weber County, U. T.
19th of Jan. 1879. Mrs. Martha Wayman aged


89 years 7 months and 11 days. Deceased was
born June 8th, 1789 at Fedstanton Hunting-
don shire England. She was baptized in the
year 1850, and from that time her house was
ever open to the Elders of our faith, feeding
and lodging them, and assisting to the utmost
of her ability to advance the cause of truth.
She came to Utah in 1868, and has finished her
earthly career in good faith, and buoyant hopes
of a glorious resurrection. Her remains are en-
tered in the Ogden City Cemetery.

	DIED, at her residence South Cottonwood,
of consumption, after a lingering illness, Mary
Jane, wife of Robert Maxfield; and daughter
of Jane Spencer and Daniel S. Cahoon. De-
ceased was born in Nauvoo, Ill., on the 8th da[-]e
of February A. D. 1845, and died on the 17th
of February, 1879, leaving a husband, two chil-
dren and numerous relatives and friends to
mourn her loss. She was a member of the Re-
lief Society, a faithful wife, an affectionate
mother, and a true saint. In the family circle
her example was worthy of imitation, and her
name will ever be held in honorable remem-
brance by all who knew her.
		MARY CAHOON.


ANY ONE ordering the "National Citizen"
with "Woman's Words" will be sent both
papers one year, postage paid, for $1.50,
thus saving fifty cents on the two subscrip-
tions.
	MINORITY REPORT ON SIXTEENTH
AMENDMENT.—The minority report of Sen-
ate Committee on Privileges and Elections
in favor of a sixteenth amendment to the
Constitution, protecting women citizens in
the right of suffrage, was made Saturday
morning, February 1st, by Hon. George F.
Hoar, of Mass. It had been waiting only
for the signature of Senator Cameron, of
Wisconsin, who was absent.
	The report will therefore be presented to
the Senate the first week in February. It
occupies about thirty-five pages of manu-
script, and will probably make about ten
pages of octavo print. It will be electro-
typed at the Government Printing Office,
and, with Senator Wadleigh's adverse re-
port, will be added to the pamphlet of ar-
guments before Senate Committee on Privi-
leges and Elections in behalf of a sixteenth
amendment by delegates to the Tenth
Washington Convention of the National
Woman Suffrage Association, January 11th
and 12th, 1878.
	The volume will thus consist of about
sixty pages, and will be standard material
for carrying on Woman Suffrage campaigns
until the victory is won.
	The friends of woman suffrage cannot
more effectually advance the interests of
women than to send money to the Treasurer
of the National Association, Mrs. Ellen
Clarke Sargent, 1733 De Sales street, Wash-
ington, D. C., to print this material. While
ten thousand copies could be immediately
used to advantage in preparing friends of
woman suffrage for an intelligent advance
upon the Forty-sixth Congress, for want of
means only one thousand copies can now be
printed. Thus at every step of this reform
want of money bars the work. Single
printed copies of the minority[-]report only
will be mailed to any one who encloses a
stamped envelope, properly addressed to
the applicant, to Mrs. Sara Andrews Spen-
cer, Spencerian Business College, Washing-
ton, D. C., after February 20th.—Ex.


204		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
MRS. C. FOWLER WELLS.

	The many instances which the world af-
fords of women who have excelled in art,
science, literature and business, prove be-
yond the possibility of doubt, that clear
perceptions, masterly intellects and execu-
tive ability are not peculiarly masculine
qualities.
	The lady whose sketch is here given has
not only threaded her way successfully amid
the intricacies and difficulties of a new and 
undeveloped science, but has competed fa-
vorably with the best class of men in con-
ducting a large and complicated business.
	Mrs. Wells is the sister of O. S. and L.
N. Fowler, the distinguished phrenologists,
and is herself, one of the ablest exponents
of that science the age has yet produced.
	Had it been a chemical laboratory which
her brothers established, or anything bear-
ing the stamp of antiquity upon it, her taste
in building up from penury a successful
business would have been much lighter
than it has been with the much questioned
science.
	The Fowler brothers established their
Phrenological Cabinet in New York in 1835.
They opened a branch office in Philadelphia,
and commenced the publication of their
journal in that city in 1838.
	They gave their attention largely to the 
development of their new science and the
editing of their magazine; while the sub-
ject of this sketch, who joined them in 1837,
looked carefully after the details of the
business.
	In 1842 they removed to New York, the
senior partner coming in advance, leaving
only a young and inexperienced woman at
that time to attend to the shipment of goods
and the closing up of the details of busi-
ness in Philadelphia.
	On her arrival in New York she found
her brother seriously ill; and before he
could again attend to business their stereo-
type plates and many other things belong-
ing to their peculiar business were seized,
and would have been sold at auction; as
would also the copyrights, but for the pre-
sence of mind and business sagacity of their
feminine business manager, who averted
the calamity.
	A year later she had occasion to leave
New York for a few weeks, and on her re-
turn found their office placarded "to let."
Before night, she had the obnoxious placard
removed from the door, and papers made
out leasing the premises for five years.
Handbills were immediately printed and
distributed, announcing lectures every eve-
ning is the same building, with both public
and private examinations of heads; and at
the end of a week when they counted their
cash, they had two hundred dollars above
expenses.
	Four other times in the history of the 
business has the office been about to close,
when her business sagacity and quiet deter-
mination have averted the calamity, and
under her management, as is a well-known
fact, has finally been built up one of the 
most successful publishing houses of the 
city; there, being not more than six that
rival it, either in capital or the number and 
extent of their publications.
	At the beginning of the panic in 1873 the 
firm was wholly free from any business en-
tanglements; but of course no publishing
house could meet the financial disasters of
the last five years and not suffer loss. They
still occupy one of the handsomest stores
on Broadway and are entirely solvent.
These details of business have been en-
tered into with some minuteness to show
how woman's skill has, in thousands of in-

[Column 2]
stances, saved a waning business, and how
it might have done so in many more were
they not so effectually ruled out of business
departments by the domination of sex on
the one hand, and their own easy subjection
on the other.
	Mrs. Wells was born in Cohocton, N. Y.,
August 14, 1814, making her now in her
sixty-fifth year.
	The remarkable skill with which she has
carried youthful vigor clear across life's me-
ridian, and maintained both the mental and
physical strength of early life even to that
advanced period is a strong argument in fa-
vor of the hygienic system taught through 
their extensive library.
	Another practical demonstration of the
effect of correct habits is found in the fact
that Mrs. Wells was born of a consumptive
mother, wh[-] died of that lingering disease
when she was only five years of age. But
the introduction of the water cure treat-
ment, a thorough system of hygienic living
or some unknown cause; have averted the
usual consequences which are so apt to fol-
low in such cases; and not one of the Fowl-
er family have since died of consumption
or ever shown a symptom thereof. The
youngest members are now over forty, and
all are hale, hearty and youthful for their
years. Mrs. Wells says that she expects to 
live till she is ninety, in the enjoyment of 
all her faculties.
	At the age of thirty she was married to
Samuel R. Wells, who, the year following,
became a partne[-] in the firm. His atten-
tion was first attracted to phrenology by ob-
serving a chart that had been marked for a
friend by Charlotte Fowler.
	He sought out the lady, made her acquain-
ance, and from that time became a devoted
disciple of the new science.
	Her very happy domestic life, and sym-
metrical development of character, show
that it is not fatal to woman's happiness to
remain single a few years longer than they
are generally wont.
	The "Phrenological Journal" has been
from the beginning uncompromising in its
advocacy of woman's equality. For a num-
ber of years before the Seneca Falls Con-
vention in '48, it had ably and fearlessly
sought for woman's recognition, an equality
of rights in all things. From that time
till the prosent never has a volume appeared
which has not contained one or more special
article looking to the elevation and better
education of woman.
	Mrs. Wells was one of the original foun-
ders of the Woman's Homeopathic College
of New York, and for twelve years one of 
its trustees.
	While she has acted a conspicuous part
in all the public enterprises of the day af-
fecting woman's advancement, by far the
larger share of her benevolence has con-
sisted in private charities. It has been her
aim to truly benefit those whom she be-
lieved worthy of help, without making any
public exhibition of either their necessities
or her own benevolence.
	Many a woman who reads this sketch
will remember with gratitude when the
kindness and benevolence of Mrs. Wells
was just the needed link in the chain that
enabled them to reunite their shattered for-
tunes, or the added plank that bridged
some fearful chasm of financial or social
disaster. Her labors have always been of
the unassuming kind, but whatever she has
put her hand to has been done with a quiet
determination that has seldom known de-
feat. In April, 1875, her husband, after a 
brief illness of but few days, was taken
from this world, in what seemed the prime
of a useful life.

[Column 3]
	Both of her brothers had previously with-
drawn from the firm, and thus was left en-
tirely to her care the responsibilites of a 
large and complicated business, and that,
too, at the most trying period of all classes
of commercial enterprise ever known in
the world's history.
	The ability with which she has conducted
its affairs amid the financial breakers that
have wrecked so many business crafts on
every hand, attest her skill, and add an-
other gem to woman's coronet of praise.—
C. A. Blodgett.					Ex.


EVENTS OF THE PRESENT DAY.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	We live in an age of wonderful events,
whether the people will believe it or not;
the vision of some is very opaque. they can
not or will not see anything very uncom-
mon in the marvelous events of the 19th
century--others, who watch the "signs of
the times," whose eyes are open, because
the vision of their mind is clear, have the
key that unlocks the cypher! and many
there are of that number. We hear of
"wars and rumors of wars," of fire and pes-
tilence, yes, of the most fearful kind. We
have seen empires overturned and sove-
reigns dethroned, and a few months ago
the Queen of England made Empress of
India! Though she has long had immense
possessions there, no one dreamed of
such a thing. Thirty years ago a musing,
far-seeing young mind, hid up in compara-
tive obscurity, wrote a book; he suggests a
policy for the then young Queen of Eng-
land; he elaborates the subject, shows a
neccessity for it. The book is not read by
the mass, it is "too dry, too visionary;" the
writer shows he believes in present revela-
tion, that he waits, watches for it! And
who will believe such nonsense in this en-
lightened age? He wrote other books, they
were watched and criticized by the "Litera-
ti;" there is something in them so unique,
so original, minds of strong calibre feel
there is in them an under current, a gulf-
stream if you please, that the common mind
does not see or realize; the policy he sug-
gests in his first book, thirty years ago, is
in these words: "Let the Queen of England
collect a great fleet; let her stow away all
her treasures, bullion, gold plate and pre-
cious arms, be accompanied by all her court
and chief people, and transfer the seat of
her Empire from London to Delhi! There
she will find an immense Empire ready
made, a first rate army and a large revenue;
we will acknowledge the Empress of India
as our Suzeram, and secure for her the
Levantine coast."
	He now leaves book making; he works
to become a Statesman; he seeks to enter
Parliament, he meets with great opposition;
he speaks on the Hustings; he attracts all 
ears; his language is glowing, fervid and
electrifying, his voice magnetic, and he is
felt to be a rising planet! He speaks in the
House of Commons, he is ridiculed; his
hearers forget to be gentlemen. He rises
and is put down, rises and is put down; at
last he calmly sits down, saying: "Gentle-
man, you will not hear me now, but a time
is coming when you shall hear me." Through
all he rises--he is the first of his race that
ever entered Parliament--he rises higher,
higher, till he stands Premier of England,
with the entire confidence of his Sovereign!
He stands to-day a power behind the Throne,
God being the power behind both the
Throne and the man. His first grand act
is to realize his dream of thirty years ago!


He proposes to the nation to make the
Queen Empress of India, as a necessary
policy to meet the times.  The influential
part of the nation, as usual, oppose the
measure—they feel it will compromise their
honor—but it is carried.  Queen of Eng-
land and Empress of India are her titles to-
day!  This is only a tithe of all he has ac-
complished in staying the hand of Prussia,
without shedding one drop of blood.  Is
not all this a subject for meditation when
we ponder on present events?
	Now I will come nearer home.  If the
Lord will help me, I will at some future
time try to give a brief poetical history of
the work of the Latter days.  At present I
wish to look at an event which twenty, per-
haps ten years ago would have been thought
impossible, and by some it would have
been ridiculed; to-day it is an event of the
past, and seems to have been all in the pro-
gramme.  It was mooted something in this
way: Mrs. Spencer, one of the prominent
women in the Woman Suffrage movements
wrote to Mrs. E. B. Wells, imperatively:
"Come and take your hitherto vacant chair
in the Woman's Suffrage Convention; come,
or our words concerning you will fall to the
ground. Come!"  Accordingly, Mrs. E. B.
Wells, accompanied by Mrs. Zina Young Wil-
liams, went and took part in the Convention
at Washington.  They go! are received by
the ladies as sisters.  President Hayes also
receives them with all the kindness and
affability of the true gentlemen; he talked
freely with them, and said he wished them
to put their statements in writing, and
let him have it, that he might not for-
get in the press of business all they had
told him.  He was most kind, saying:  "Tell
me what I can do for you."  The "Golden
Sceptre" was indeed held out to them at
every interview, and he himself fetched
Mrs. Hayes and introduced them to her,
and left her with them, not fearing the
least contamination, or perversion of her
mind or morals from association with the
women from Utah, and both plural
wives!  All honor to the President of the
United States of America and his true-
hearted wife! one day he will rejoice that
his feelings as a man and a gentleman over-
came all prejudice and bigotry, and enabled
him to receive them as sister women by
their brother man.  Mrs. Hayes also had
the very spirit of her husband, and treated
them with all the freedom and liberality
which ever springs from true nobility.  She
took them over the Executive Mansion
herself, with all ease and affability.  God
bless Mrs. Hayes, and may He grant her
her hearts desires in righteousness before
Him!
	Is not this an event, my sisters, in the
annals of our Church?  To me there is more
in it than meets the eye of those who look
only upon the surface of things.  God works
by instruments, sometimes the weakest as
a common observer would think, but in His
hands they become powerful, because they
are content to be "as clay in the hands of
the potter."  God will make such mighty.
They were received with the kindest
deference, and obtained interviews with
men of influence; this was true polite-
ness, for they had come from afar to
present the cause of their people.  They
spoke freely and I have no doubt wise-
ly and well; they have left an impres-
sion that God will daguerotype on the minds
of the influential men of Congress, nd
also on the women of that august assembly,
which will have a bearing, and I hope make
them forbearing.  My sisters, fear not!
Let us be prayerful and watchful--God is
with us; yes, the God of Jacob, is our refuge!


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


MEMORIAL.

TO THE SENATE AND HOUSE OF REPRESENT-
	TATVIES, BY THE LADIES' ANTI-POLYGA-
	MY SOCIETY OF SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH.

	The Ladies of the Anti-Polygamy Society
of Utah respectfully represent to Congress
that while Delegate Cannon and represent-
atives of the Mormon Church in Washing-
ton are petitioning for amnesty and promis-
ing obedience for their people to the anti-
polygamy law of 1862, the expressions and
actions of the Mormons in Utah give no
evidence that this promise will be fulfilled.
	Apostle John Taylor, president of the
Mormon Church, in an interview with a
Federal official, published by himself, de-
clared that the revelation enjoining poly-
gamy came direct from God; that it is his
religion, and that neither Congress nor the
Supreme Court has any right to interfere
with his religious views, and in doing so
they are violating their most sacred obliga-
tions. He said further that the Supreme
Court decision would have no effect except
to unite, confirm and strengthen the Mor-
mons in their faith. And this same Taylor,
when in France about the year 1853, he
having at that time five wives, denied in
toto the existence of polygamy among the
Mormons, and in order to the greater pub-
licity to this untrue statement, employed a
French lady, Madame Brouse by name, to
translate his denial of polygamy into French,
and then circulated it in pamphlet form
throughout the country. In this spirit the
Mormon people concur, and it is a matter
of common notoriety that they are contract-
ing unlawful marriages the same as ever,
that John W. Young has married Miss A. Wil-
kins, of this city, for his fifth wife since the
decision of the Supreme Court in the Rey-
nolds case was published; that James Welch
of Coalville, Summit county, has married
Ellen Vaughan for a second wife; that John
White, of East Mill Creek, Salt Lake coun-
ty, married Mrs. Samuels (a widow) for a
third wife, all since the decision above re-
ferred to.
	"The Deseret News," the official organ
of the Mormon Church, casts reproach upon
the Supreme Court, by declaring that the
decision was rendered under popular pres-
sure, by judges who are feeble-witted and
cloudy-minded; and in the ward meeting
houses, the most violent diatribes have
been uttered against the judges and the
judgment of the highest court of the land.
	We therefore respectfully ask Congress,
during its present session, so to amend the
act of 1862 that it may become operative by
making the general reputation of conjugal
relations, proof of marriage and living to-
gether in polygamy, to constitute the of-
fence. Unless the prosecuting officer of the
Government is enabled by some such appro-
priate legislation to prosecute and punish
offenders against the above named law, we
feel warranted in saying no regard will be
paid to the statute; polygamous marriages
will be as numerous as ever, and the de-
luded Mormons will be encouraged in their
violation of law from the religious belief
that the Lord has interposed in the behalf
of His people by confounding the designs
of their enemies.
	By resolution the Secretary was directed
to forward a copy of the above to Senator
Edmunds, with a request to present them
in the Senate, and a duplicate copy to Hon.
Martin Maginnis of Montana, with a re-
quest to offer them in the House, also to
send them by telegraph to the newspapers
of the United States for publication.
	The Secretary read a number of letters
from ladies and ministers in different parts
of the country, reporting the progress of
the work being done in their respective
localities, and giving assurance of the
hearty co-operation the ladies here are
meeting with from their sisters in the
States.		SARAH E. COOK, Pres.
			VICTORIA REED, Vice Pres.
			MARIAN CHISLETT, Sec'y.


GENEROSITY.

	READ AT THE Y. L. M. I. A.

	As generosity is the subject before the
house, for discussion this evening[-] I will
commence by saying that generosity[-] not
only consists in being open-handed but in
exhibiting those qualities which are prop-
erly regarded as belonging to noble minded
men and women. It is not always those
who are the most liberal in giving, but my
idea is that those who can endure the most
privations and hardships[-] taking every
thing for the best, and being contented
with what they have to endure are truly
generous. Look at our Heavenly Father
how magnanimous He is, pouring blessings
down upon all, with-holding nothing which
is for our good, nothing which is truly
beneficial. Certainly there are times when
we feel down[-]hearted and think we are not
very liberaly blessed with his holy spirit;
but all our trials and harships are for the
best. God has a purpose in all things, and
He sometimes allows stumbling-blocks to
be put in our way to try us, for[-] if we are
never tried with any thing and[-]our paths
are always flowery, of course we would
feel much better, but could not have the
same experience; God has not willed it so,
He tries us every day to see what we can
stand, some few bear their trials nobly, and
others sink under them. I do not profess
to show the magnanamity of God, but mere-
ly speak of it in my humble way, and as
generosity is our subject—I do not think
we can find a more generous, or just person
for example than Joseph Smith. Look at
the trials and hardships that this noble per-
son passed through for this people, and to
show them the right way, the greatness of
his mind, the elevation and dignity of his
soul, how he encountered dangers and trou-
ble with tranquility and firmness. He dis-
dained injustice and meanness, and his no-
ble spirit prompted him to sacrifice all for
this same noble object. And then our be-
loved President Brigham Young took up
his great work and followed in his footsteps,
giving generously ta the poor; helping and
blessing all who were in distress and need-
ful of his attentions and benevolence; nev-
er faltering until he was called to join his
brother, Joseph Smith, in the land of the
blessed, where we all sooner or later will be
called to go; that is if we prove faithful to
the end. When I picture the narrow, con-
tractedness and selfishness of our souls
with the nobleness and generosity of these
great persons it causes me to shudder.
While we are speaking of the generosity of
those great persons, we must not forget our
kind and generous hearted parents who
toil night and day striving to give us ad-
vantages, which some day will exalt us
among the[--]number of great men and wo-
men. How many [-]s yaung people now
could endure what our parents have been
called to go through in times past and
never falther, but with untiring diligence
stem the tide and at last win the victory.
I am afraid not very many, I think after
we go through as much as they have been
called to pass through, and preform as
many noble and generous acts and never
falter it will cause us all to shout "Eureka."
		MISS DESERET BRINGHURST.


206		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[For the EXPONENT.
		AMERICAN LITERATURE.

	Attention is drawn anew to this subject
by the very recent History of American
Literature by Moses Coit Tyler, Professor
of English Literature in the University of
Michigan.
	The book cannot be characterised as a
great work, yet it supplies a long felt want.
The authors of whose life and writings
these two volumes give an account, are for
the most part little known, if at all, by the
great reading public, but will be known in
future, not through their own writings but
through the copious extracts from their
works given in those volumes. This is one
of the merits of the "History". Professor
Tyler has had access to the most valuable
libraries of our country,—not merely to our
large public libraries, but to those private
libraries where only is to be found our earli-
est American Literature. These two vol-
umes cover the period from 1607 to 1765.
This fact suggests the very important ques-
tion. Have we a distinctively American
literature covering this period, or must we
mark a later date for the birth of our liter-
ature?
	Unlike the early literature of England,
we cannot point with the pride of ancestry
to our Chaucer, the Ballads of Robin Hood
the vision of Piers the Ploughman and
others. We must remember that at the
date which Professor Tyler has placed the
birth of our literature, England was at the
summit of her literary fame, was in the
glory of the Elizabethan age. Shakespeare
had written most of his plays; had become
proprietor of the Globe Theatre; was now a
respected squire, and lived in gentlemanly
competency, riding in his own carriage. At
this period Elizabeth had been dead four
years; Francis Bacon had become solicitor
general, and thirteen years after gave to
the world his Noverm Organan; Rare
Ben Jonson had written "Every Man in
his Humor" nine years before; Beaumont
and Fletcher were young men seeking pres-
ent fame and acquiring future fame; Milton
was born a year after, he whose name was
to last so long as lasts the English language.
	America can at this period present no
such names, nor since. We think that the
succeeding volumes of Professor Tyler's
mark will be the history of American liter-
ature. The subjects that the hundred
years of our national existance will present
are the cues which create the same lan-
guage as the literature of England but a dif-
ferent type of mind and manner. A. L. F.


THE BILL generally known as the Lock-
wood Bill, but technically as the Legal Disa-
bilities Bill, recently passed the House and
Senate. This bill has won a victory for
women, and after this let women be encour-
aged, however adverse circumstances may
be, for difficulties and obstructions are giv-
ing way before endurance, perseverance,
persistency and tenacity of purpose. The
bill provides that "any woman, a member
in good standing of the highest court in
any State or Territory, or of the Supreme
Court of the District of Columbia, for three
years, shall be admitted to practice in the
United States Supreme Court." The bill
passed at last without amendment, 40 to 20.
Senator Sargent, who has been an able and
magnanimous friend of woman suffrage,
has been untiring in his efforts in advocat-
ing the passage of this bill. This noble
friend and defender of the rights of all
women merits the highest approbation and
esteem of all who desire to see woman
stand free and equal beside her brother
man.


THE NEXT annual convention of the Na-
tional Woman Suffrage Association is to be
held in May in St. Louis, Mo. This city
has been chosen because of the proposition
there to license the Social Evil law. The
suffrage women consider, and all deep-
thinking women surely will agree with
them, that this licensing of vice by govern-
ment is an infringement upon the liberty of
all women. Wherever this law has been
enforced, instead of securing the object for
which it was (professedly) designed it "has
had most oppressive moral and physical
tyranny over woman. As far as women
are concerned it reverses that rule of law
which holds all persons innocent until they
are proven guilty, for under it all women
are held guilty until proven innocent." The
call for the Convention made through
the "National Citizen and Ballot Box" says,
"Now let every Western and North West-
ern suffrage woman prepare herself to be in
some way represented at this great meeting,
either personally or by delegate from her
society	No more important convention
has ever been held than this will be; none
in which all women of whatever shade of
opinion are so deeply concerned.

ROMANIA B. PRATT,

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
VOL. 7.				SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 15, 1879.				No. 20.


[For the EXPONENT.
	WE CAN.

The dearest of all precious treasures,
	The pet and the pride of our hearts;
What sunshine and sweetness and gladness
	Its heavenly presence imparts!
OUR darling will stay with us, surely;
	For its future we lovingly plan;
WE "couldn't keep house" without Baby—
Yet it goes, and we find that we can!

What wealth of intelligence reigneth
	In manhood's awakening power;
What purity crowneth our maidens,
	Like th' freshness of opening flowers.
Oh! the young folks. our hope, who are taking
	First parts in life's beautiful "play;"
Death comes and selects, oft the brightest—
	We can hear it—there's no other way.

The kindest and truest companion
	The fendest and gentlest friend,
At whost loss every prospect seems blighted,
	Life's interests all suddenly end:
Such are called, and half blind and bewildered,
	We rise from the groveling dust;
We stagger and reel, but we gather
	And stand, for we CAN when we MUST.

The leaders of people and nations,
	The men whom we value and prize,
And look upon as without equals,
	So noble and gifted and wise;
They leave us, and yet we press onward,
	As earnest and seemingly strong;
There is no standing still for the people,
	We must and we can move along.

We can live, though bereft of the blessings,
	Which seem more than half of our lives;
The babies, the youths and the maidens,
	Even parents, and husbands and wives:
Our Prophets, indeed, may be taken,
	And we bow'neath the chastening rod,
'Till the blessed, sweet Comforter cometh—
	We can do without all but our GOD!
		LULA.
L. G. Richards


"STAND STILL."

	A young friend asked me what we should
do "if Congress, or the Supreme Court of
the United States, should take us in hand
as earnestly as it appears they intend to
do?"
	I answered by opening the Bible and
reading from the fourteenth chapter of
Exodus, thirteenth verse: "And Moses
said unto the people, Fear ye not, stand
still, and see the salvation of the Lord,
which He will shew to you to-day."
	This is the way I feel, I said. If the
Lord wants us to make any particular move
in the matter, more than we have already
done, He will let us know. Otherwise, we
will "stand still." We will at least show
our enemies that we have more patience
and politeness than some of their party
possess. We will listen while they are
speaking to us and of us, no matter how
absurd their expressions may seem to us.
And that is more than some of our accusers
in this city are willing to do.
	At the Woman's Mass Meeting held in
the Theatre, two ladies of the opposing
party took seats directly back of where I
was sitting. And when Miss Snow in her
remarks said, "As true and loyal American
citizens we claim the right," &c., one of
these ladies observed, in a tone loud enough
to be distinctly heard, "Not very loyal!"
	Further on in her remarks Miss Snow re-
ferred to the Latter-day work as being es-
tablished "upon the principles of purity,


peace and righteousness," when one of the
ladies at my back echoed, "Purity! and
Righteousness!"
	Thus the speakers remarks were com-
mented upon, until she spoke of the feel-
ings of sympathy instead of revenge which
she felt for those banded together in our
midst to work evil, and who are trying to
measure arms with the Almighty. Then
came whispers from the ladies behind me,
of "Let's go!"
	And they did go. Instead of listening
to the Mormon women's vindications and
explanations of their faith and practices,
and why they held to them, these ladies
arose and left the house before the first
speaker had concluded her remarks.
	This is but one of a great many instances
of a similar kind. And such are the char-
acters who represent us, or misrepresent us,
as disloyal, unrighteous and everything
bad. They do not know us, and they refuse
to hear us tell what we are. But we can
afford to bear with them, and when we
have nothing to say or do for a little time
we can "stand still and see the salvation of
the Lord."					L. G. R.


For the EXPONENT.
				TIME.

	Time is a measured portion of duration.
It is divided into three grand divisions, viz.,
the past, the present and the future. The
past we know. Memory, that faculty of
the mind that retains so vividly the knowl-
edge of all our past thoughts and actions,
does its duty faithfully in this regard. It
brings to mind the bitter remembrance of
things that are past and connects them with
the present and the future. It makes them
live through all time, and may, perhaps,
through all eternity. It shows us all our
failings, all our imperfections, and points
out to us wherein we have failed to come
up to the standard of our expectations. It is
therefore necessary that we remember the
past, to make us not unmindful of the pre-
sent, and to better prepare us for the future.
There are some things we would not forget.
Memory may bring many pleasant recollec-
tions, that we are as anxious to retain as
we are to forget all past trials and disap-
pointments. There is in every one's life
some bright oasis to which memory will
revert with gladness, and linger on, as a
sweet strain of music to the weary heart.
The present is now. We cannot live in
time that is past, nor in time that is to
come. We actually live only now. It is
now we must bend all our energies to make
life a grand success. Now we must work
and toil for the crown of reward we are
anxious to gain; now the spade must be
struck, the soil turned up and our crop of
good deeds sown, if we wish to reap a har-
vest. It is now we lay the foundation of
future usefulness and greatness. Our time
is our life; it is given us in trust, and like
all other things that are entrusted to our
care, we will be held accountable for the
use of it. Present time is all we can call
our own and it is wise to make the best use
of it we cau. Fill it with all the good
deeds, pleasant thoughts and kind actions
in our power, so that when it becomes the


past, it will be one we will charge memory
to retain. Of the flowery future we know
nothing, but dream much. It is the place
where imagination loves to live. It is
where we look for the fulfilling of all our
fond hopes and bright dreams. It is where
we expect the grand scenes of life's drama
to be enacted. What bright hopes and
rosy dreams we have of the future. God
grant they may all be realized.
							JUNE.


THE WISE OWLS.

	[Apropos to the decision of the Supreme
Court refusing a woman the right to appear
before it.]
	In a certain place there were nine old
owls who sit all day on a bench and looked
very wise. They tried a great many hard
cases and listened solemnly to the chatter-
ing of magpies and the cawing of crows and
the twittering of tomtits. But one morning
when the blue jay came in and asked them
to hear her, they all hooted in horror and
cried out, "Go away, you are a female!"
	Moral—If you wish to succeed, do not be
a female.—A fable by Lillie Devereux Blake.
	Now that the Supreme Court have ad-
mitted Mrs. Lockwood, to practice before
that honorable body there is a good chance
for Mrs. Blake to write another fable with
a moral, and as her efforts in that direction
are certainly very pleasing we hope soon
to see one on this same subject.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	VIRGINIA F. Stephens, of Springfield,
Mass., edits an amateur monthly paper en-
titled "The Springfield Joker."
	MRS Belva A. Lockwood was admitted
to practice at the bar of the United States
Supreme Court on the third inst.—Ex.
	ONE of the courses of Cambridge lectures
to women this term is to be given by a 
lady—Miss Crofts. The subject is English
history.—Ex.
	PETITIONS have been received by both
branches of the Michigan Legislature for
the admission of girls to the Agricultural
College, on equal footing with boys, and to
make females eligible as school officers.—Ex.
	THE Suffragists of Indiana had a hearing
before their Legislature on Tuesday last. A
memorial prepared by the Indiana State
Suffrage Association was read. Mary E.
Haggart made an argument upon the just-
ness of the claims set forth in the memori-
al. Mary F. Thomas, M. D., followed in a
speech upon the legal disabilities of women.
Mrs Governor Wallace answered the object-
ions offered to giving the ballot to women.
Amy E. Dunn closed with an address on
representative women.—Ex.


THE intellect has only one failing, which,
to be sure, is a very considerable one; it
has no conscience. Napoleon is the readi-
est instance of this. If his heart had borne
any proportion to his brain, he had been
one of the greatest men in all history.—
Lowell.

S. REPORTS.

	NEWTON, CACHE Co., Feb. 14th 1879.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	A brief report of the annual meeting of
Newton Relief Society, held in the school
house Feb. 10th 1879. We were honored
with the presence of Bishop W. H. Rigby
and his Counselors J. H. Barker and W. H.
Griffin, and there were several others of
the brethren who attended the meeting by
invitation of the President, M. A. Parsons,
there was also a good attendance of the
members. After singing and prayer, our
President made some encouraging remarks,
and stated the object of the meeting. Bish-
op Rigby then addressed the meeting, he
referred to various principles pertaining to
the Relief Society, which were very in-
teresting. He informed us that it was the
largest consolidated quorum of Newton,
hence its importance, and the necessity of
the members being wise in all their move-
ments.
	The financial report of the past year was
presented and accepted. Summary of the
same:
Cash on hand from 1877......................... $12 00
Received by donations from members	  31 58

		Total						  43 58
Wheat on hand from 1877.......................  6 bu.
Wheat donated by the sisters in 1878.....  38
Received on city lot....................................  26

		Total						   70
	To the Logan Temple $26 45. By labor
and means, with the aid of the brethren, as
well as the sisters, we have fenced and
cultivated our city lot to the amount of $72,
70.
	The meeting also passed a unanimous
vote in favor of upholding the principle of
plural marriage, taking the Resolutions on
the same topic as published in the "WO-
MAN'S EXPONENT" of Jan. 1st 1879.
	Mrs. Mary Griffin, a visitor from the
Clarkston Relief Society, of which she is
the President, expressed her approval of
polygamy as revealed from God, and prac-
tised among the Latter-day Saints, in a
very decided manner.
	Mrs. J. M. Welchman, who had passed an
experience of many years as a practical
polygamist, said: she would not like to
give it up, though there were many sacri-
fices to make, but it all tended to purify
the life and character of women, she real-
ized that it was a principle of temporal
and eternal salvation.
	Singing, Benediction by Bishop W. F.
Rigby.
	A party was held in the evening, which
was well attended, we had a very pleasant
time and all present seemed to enjoy
themselves.
				ELIZABETH J. CLARKE, Sec'y.

			TOQUERVILLE, Feb. 20th, 1879.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The annual meeting of our Society was
held Jan. 18, 1879. The report of the year
1878, was as follows:
Donated to needy....................................... $40 55
Donated to Sanpete Temple cash............   6
Capital Stock in Co-op store......................    30
Capital Stock in Washington Factory            25
Cash on hand..............................................    20
Sundry articles.............................................    94 80
	We have bought a lot, to raise mulberry
trees, for which we paid $50. The brethren
turned out "en masse" and fenced the lot
entire and made other improvements. The
sisters arranged a picnic and surprised the
workers, which they received thankfully
and had a good time. The Society gave
the workers a party and supper; over one
hundred and fifty persons partook of the
supper, "and all went merry as a marriage
bell." We had a very pleasant call from
Sisters Minerva Snow, Anna Ivins, Lizzie
Calkins, Elizabeth Bently and Eyring from
St. George. They gave us some excellent
counsel and spoke very encouragingly to
the sisters, which was highly appreciated
by us.
	Wishing prosperity to the "EXPONENT."
		Your Sisters in the Gospel
			ELIZABETH KLIENMAN Pres.
			ROSILIA SPILSBURY, Sec'y

		UTAH STAKE REPORT.

	Minutes of the Quarterly Conference of
the Relief Societies of Utah Stake of Zion,
held Feb. 28th, 1879; Sister M. T. Smoot,
with her Counsel, presiding; present of the
priesthood, Pres. David John, Bro. Paxman
and others. Sisters E. R. Snow, Zina D.
Young and E. B. Wells, of Salt Lake City,
were also present.
	After singing and prayer the minutes of
former meeting were read and accepted.
The Quarterly Reports were then read by
the Secretary. Bro. Paxman addressed the
sisters for a short time, after which meet-
ing adjourned till 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
	Afternoon meeting opened by singing
and prayer. Sister E. B Wells occupied a
portion of the time in giving, in a very
pleasing manner, an account of her trip to
Washington. Sisters Zina D. Young and
E. R. Snow also gave the sisters a great
deal of good instructions pertaining to their
several duties. The presidents of the dif-
ferent wards gave a verbal account of their
wards, speaking well of the sisters over
whom they presided. A spirit of love and
union exists among the sisters, and all seem
to feel well.
	The following is our Statistical and Fi-
nancial Report for the Stake:
Members.............................................. 1875
Teachers...............................................   208
Meetings held......................................     66

	dustries, Silk.....................................   17 84
On hand in cash and property..............   4478 70

	A few of the Societies did not send in
their Quarterly Reports. Our Conference
adjourned for three months.
						C. DANIELS, Secretary.

						Y. L. M. I. A. Sec'y.

	The Second Quarterly Conference of Re-
lief Societies and Young Ladies Mutual
Improvement Associations of Box Elder
Stake of Zion was held in Brigham City,
Dec. 10th, 1878. Meeting called to order
by President Harriet Snow. Present on
the stand were Bishops of different Wards,
Patriarch Box, Presidents of R. S. and Y.
L. M. I. A., and others. After the opening
exercises, the minutes of first Quarterly
Conference were read and approved. Re-
ports from fifteen R. S. read by the Stake
Secretary, after which the presidents of R.
S. of Brigham City gave verbal reports of
their respective societies, each seeming to
fully sense the importance position to which
they were called, and expressing a desire
to aid in furthering the cause of righteous-
ness. Pres. H. Snow made a few closing
remarks, stating her pleasure and satisfac-
tion with the efforts of the sisters. Con-
ference adjourned till 2 p.m. Benediction
by Bishop Harper.
	After the opening exercises of the after-
noon, presidents of R. S. of the adjacent
settlements each gave verbal reports. The
reports of the Y. L. M. I. Associations of
the different wards of the Stake were read
by the Secretary, Lydia May Snow, after
which the presidents of Y. L. M. I. A. re-
ported verbally. Primary Associations
were also represented. The remainder of
the afternoon was devoted to business.
	Conference adjourned until March 10,
1879.
	Quarterly Report of Relief Societies of
Box Elder Stake of Zion, from Sept. 10,
1878 up to Dec. 10, 1878.

Faith evermore overlooks the difficulties
of the way, and bends her eyes only to the
end. She looks back to the Gross and is at
peace; and forward to the crown, and pants
for its possession.—Bishop Hall.
	If our religion is not true, we are bound
to change it; If it is true, we are bound to
propagate it.—Whately.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		209


[For the EXPONENT.
	"MY SON GIVE ME THINE HEART."

She rocked her baby, singing low
	Love tones that mothers
To tender words that mothers know
	How fittingly to string.
And aye! the secret of her song,
	In each and every part;
Was ever all the sweet time long,
	'My child, give me thine heart.'

When nature's fairest beauties smiled.
	She loved with him to stray,
And taught the listening trustful child,
	To fairer realms the way.
And when amid earth's scenes of ill.
	Her anxious fears did start;
The pleading of her love, was still,
	My child give me thine heart.

My child give me thine heart, he hears
	The heavenly Father's call;
For fleeing to his throne, her tears
	And prayer before him fall,
When Christ hath died that thou might'st live,
	And facing evils dart,
Thy mother's love, no less would give
	Wilt thou withold thine heart?

Alas! how e'en Immanuel's cry
	Of desolation, pain;
Pleading for strength, with him on high,
	While midnight hour did wane;
Fell on disciples loved, who slept,	
	Though soon with him to part
While He in love, and anguish wept,
	'Father give me their hearts.'

Alas! for him, who had denied
	His Master's name and power;
Of whom, e'en nature testified,
	In crucifixions hour.
Spare me the cry of souls that wait
	The dread command depart,	
Remembering at last, too late,
	'My child, give me thine heart. 
		AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
	Feb. 10, 1879.


[Column 1]
						[For the EXPONENT.
		CONSISTENCY THOU ART A 
				JEWEL.

	Will our enemies please be consistent, if
only for the novelty of the thing? How
reasonable they are! they never turn their
coats, no never! A short time since the
cry was the poor, down-trodden, broken-
hearted women of Utah, who dared not
raise their heads, much less their voices,
for fear of the vengeance of their tyrant
husband, for instance tie them up in sacks
and throw them into the Salt Lake to
pickle. They said give the poor, ignorant
creatures the franchise, and they will turn
the tables on their tyrants by pitching them
into the big pickling tub nature has pro-
vided. Well, our husbands gave us the
right of franchise, showing how little fear
they had of our vengeance, and for eight
years we have gone to the polls with those
husbands to cast our votes for those we felt
the best qualified to hold the offices we had
the power to bestow; no quarrelling, no
rowdyism, all peace and harmony.
	All of a sudden we hear a cry. What's
the matter now? Why, those women that
we have liberated, instead of voting for us,
and giving us all power to crush their ty-
rants, they are as bad as their husbands; we
thought we had thrown sand in their eyes,
and blinded their reason, but we find we
have failed; we thought they were fools,
but find they have out-witted us. Now,
what are we to do? We will move earth
and that other place, where they keep the
furnace red-hot, but we will stop your vo-
ting. What have they done here in Tooele
County? Thrown out all the precincts but
two. What for? To use the words of one
poor sniveling creature of this most liber-
al (?) party, who put in a petition asking

[Column 2]
that all returns from all the precincts voting
the peoples' ticket be thrown out, "because
the WOMEN voted." How magnanimous!
poor fellow, I wonder if he ever had a
mother, a wife, or a daughter; if so, what
do they think of him? Is he not afraid
they will pickle him?
	What is all this hue and cry about? Is
it our religion? Oh no! we don't care what
you believe in, you can believe in anything
you like only you must not practice it.
They roll up their eyes in holy horror at
plurality of wives, yet practice it them-
selves, after their own manner. The old
saying is "those that make the greatest
cry of 'stop thief!' are those that need
watching." What do such men care for wo-
man but to abuse her? Nothing; less than
nothing. The vast majority of mankind, if
women will do as they wish, and keep
quiet, they will spend their money to adorn
their person—never mind their soul—until
they get tired of them and want a change,
then if married to one they will often treat
her to magnificent contempt, twirl a cigar
between their lips, and talk of the folly
and imbecility of women, and bestow time
and means upon other women to beguile
their idle hours; if unmarried, spend their
money and degrade their manhood in gam-
bling dens and other vile places.
	They call the Mormons vile, whose lives
are spent in caring for their wives and chil-
dren, both temporally and spiritually, who
endeavor to exalt themselves and all de-
pending on them, who work early and late
to provide for their household honestly; not
striving as our enemies are doing, no mat-
ter how, to get their hands into "Uncle
Sam's" pocket, to rob him in utter shame-
lessness, following the Quaker's advice to
his son: "My son, get money; get it hon-
estly if thee can, but get money." Any one
that reads the papers cannot help but see
that the amount of fraud inflicted on the
people of the United States is stupendous,
and every pretended investigation is but an
added robbery of the nation. If our de-
famers are sincere, let them commence at
home to set things right, and they will find
so much to do that they will forget to trou-
ble us.
	Then again, how very consistent are the
friends of the Chinese Bill (these same
men); their strongest arguments are that
they have no family ties; they do not amal-
gamate; they do not seek to become citi-
zens, but as soon as they obtain sufficient
money return to their own country. Now
they must be driven out of the country, or
exterminated for not having families. The
Mormons must receive the same treatment
for having families, and seeking to become
citizens of the United States. Oh, how
very consistent! They are like the man
that was going to be hung—could not feel
satisfied. Well, they may drive John from
the country, and do many other mighty
works, but we intend to stay here so long
as we or our posterity may live.
							MARY.
	Grantsville, March 9th, 1879.


[Column 1]
OUR MISSION.

READ BEFORE THE CONJOINT MEETING OF
THE M. I. ASSOCIATIONS OF IHE 11TH,
WARD, S. L. CITY, JAN. 1879.

	My young brothers and sisters, we were 
all sent here on the earth, for a purpose,
and we all have a mission to perform. It
is the duty of each of us to understand that
mission. We have been told by many of 
our great men, that the noblest spirits were

[Column 2]
reserved to come forth in this our day, be-
cause of the great and mighty work to be
accomplished, in preparing for the second
coming of our Lord and Saviour Jesus
Christ. I wonder how many of us realize,
that ours is one of those noble spirits, and
do we ever ask ourselves the question, are
we honoring and striving to bring our bo-
dies into subjection to that pure and noble
spirit, that inhabits it, which ever invites
to deeds of virtue and holiness, and would
check us when prompted to do any evil or
wrong. How our hearts should swell with
gratitude to God, that for us was reserved
this great privilege, which so many of our
forefathers longed to enjoy but were de-
nied.
	How little we realized these great gospel
truths, until these organizations were in-
stituted for the growth and development
of the young, Now we begin to know who
we are, from where we came, and what
will be our future destiny, if we are faith-
ful to God and obey all his commands: and
let us not think for a moment, that we can
do too much good the little time we
stay; for it will be only through the utmost
diligence on our part, that we can earn a
place in the Celestial Kingdom. We must
not only be able to say, we have done
harm to no one, but we must be able to say;
we have done all in our power, for the
furtherance and advancement of the Zion of
God on the earth, being prompt in every
good word and work. Let us attend faith-
fully to all our meetings, and seek know-

taking="" part="" they="" may="" called="" upon="" by="" those="" preside="" advance="" rapid="" brides,="" know="" their,="" mission,="" and.="" yill="" become="" mighty="" pillarys="" chqrch=""
