hrth,="" glory,="" capacita.="" ted="" enjoy,="" is'the’prayer="" ofyour="" sister.="" mary="" a.reeze.="" ..="" hxii="" ol="" 11,="" t<="" div="">knowledge is power, and the more we pos-
sess the more capable we will be in assist-
ing in the great latter-day work.
	We all desire to be good and useful, so
let us put our good desire into execution,
for it is in our power so to do. It is proba-
ble that we covenanted with our Heavenly
Father before we came here, that we would
be energetic in the cause of righteousness,
if he would grant us the great and inesti-
mable privilege of having a body on the 
earth; for we could then see how great the 
blessings to thus obtained, which could
be obtained in no other way. We cannot
now see as we could then, but we have the
Holy Gospel to lead and guide us into all
truth, and teach us our duty from time to 
time, we are also blest with a living priest-
hood, through which we can receive the 
word of God, so we are not left to grope in
the dark. These associations were organ-
ized through that living priesthood, and 
consequently are from heaven; and any
young man or woman who will faithfully
attend these meetings, taking part as they
may be called upon by those who preside,
will advance with rapid strides, will know
their mission, and will become great and 
mighty pillars in the church of God upon
the earth; and finally be crowned in the
presence of God and the Lamb, while those
who take no part, or taking part shrink
from duty, will also reap the reward of the
deeds done in the body, and will receive
only what they have justly earned. May
we who here this evening put on the 
whole armor of righteousness[-] and battle
bravely to overcome sin, and establish a
reign of peace on the earth, and that we
may receive all the glory, we are capacita-
ted to enjoy, is the prayer of your sister.
		MARY A. FREEZE.


No man has come to true greatness who
has not felt in some degree that his life
belongs to his race, and what God gives
him He gives him for mankind.—Phillip
Brooks.

SALT LAKE CITY, MARCH 15, 1879.


[Column 1]
THE WASHINGTON CONVENTION.
CONTINUED.

	Friday afternoon session: On the platform
in Lincoln Hall were assembled more ladies
than at any previous session; Dr. C. B. Pervis, of
Philadelphia, and Federic Douglas occupied
front seats, with a crowded audience anxiously
awaiting. President Mrs. Stanton addressed
the Convention on the condition of the age
and the tendencies to a more one-sided suffrage
system. She said, "All are now admitted to
the ballot except idiots, Chinamen and wo-
men." that the existing state of affairs tended
to "irreverence, infidelity and corruption."
She pronounced it absurd, and added that under
man's rule the system of politics was such it
was impossible to tell whether the Chief Execu-
tive Officer of the land held his seat by fraud;
or otherwise; she referred to the cipher system
as looking exceedingly bad.
	Mrs. Matilda Joslyn Gage next delivered her
remarkable speech upon United States' Rights
vs. States' Rights.
	We consider Mrs. Gage's speech of so much
value that we intend to publish it in full, also
Mrs. Stanton's opening address, Miss Anthony's
lecture, Bread and the Ballot, Mrs. Lockwood's,
Mrs. Slocum's and our own delivered on the
first evening of the session; but our readers
will be so kind as to give us time, our paper be-
ing so small we can only publish one at a time.
	Mrs. Helen M. Slocum, of New York City, a
very finely educated and talented lady, with a
very intelligent face and an earnestness that
must have thrilled every woman's heart in
that large assembly, so impressive were her
manner and tone, plead as a mother would
plead for her own child, for the fallen and de-
praved women who sell their souls for bread,
and the innocent children born to shame and
reared in crime, if suffered to exist at all. She
appealed to the mothers of the land and asked
them if they did not think it was time they
had the privilege of owning their own chil-
dren. She informed them there were but few
States in the Union where the father had not
power to dispose of his children as he pleased,
by appointing guardians, taking them from
their mothers, and even willing away her un-
born babe if he so chooses. Mrs. Slocum stated
there were, according to the carefully prepared
statistics of the "Working Woman's Protective
Union,'. five hundred thousand women in New
York City and its immediate vicinity, outside
of domestic service, who are working for wages
which do not average over two dollars a week.
She vehemently exclaimed, "Can any one tell
how it is possible for these women to clothe,
feed and shelter themselves upon such wages
as these? *	* There is a great army of wo-
men called outcasts, whose ranks are constantly
being filled for want of bread. *	*	* The
men who withhold from women the power of

[Column 2]
self-protection through the ballot, thus pre-
venting them from being able to dictate terms
to their employers, are largely responsible for
this state of things." She appealed most elo-
quently and fervently to mothers. *	* "We
mothers cannot separate ourselves from the
great mass of women, and who of us are to f[-]r-
nish the recruits for this army whose [-]anks are
being continually refilled? for death goes hand
in hand with such foul corruption."

	Dr. Caroline B. Winslow, a resident physi-
cian and the able Editor of "The Alpha," pub-
lished in the interests and for the benefit and
elevation of women, made a thrilling speech
upon the same subject as Mrs. Slocum, al-
though Mrs. Winslow confined her remarks
more to the wickedness and corruption of the
District of Columbia.
	Mrs. Williams, of Utah, then gave a short
discourse upon Mormon women, their motives,
principles and efforts; in short made a strong de-
fense for her Mormon sisters.
	Mrs. Spencer styled Washington a Congress-
ional boarding house, and advised women
who desired the suffrage not to feed Congress-
men so well.
	Mrs. Blake emphasized "The protection of
women by men" by relating a story of a young
girl in Michigan driven out of town to die on
Christmas night, and referred to the murder
column of the newspaper. She declared
that man was not the natural protector of wo-
man, as alleged. She drew on the calendar of
crime to bear out her theory. Mrs. Lockwood
sprang up, interrupting Mrs. Blake, and said
such remarks only tended to widen the breach
between men and women, and for her part she
would not stand it; as Mrs. B. had mentioned
several cases where men had murdered their
wives, she could show half a dozen instances
in that vicinity alone where women had not
only murdered their husbands, but their chil-
dren. There was some sharp sparring between
these two, both of them women of wit and
versed in the learning of the world, but Mrs.
Stanton, with her uncommon tact, succeeded
in obtaining order and a truce, after which she
made a few remarks herself, speaking in strong
terms of commendation of men, and the after-
noon session adjourned.
	Second Evening session. The first speaker
was Mrs. Julia Browne Dunham, from Iowa,
who gave a concise annual report of the Wo-
man Suffrage Association of that State; she
thought the Iowa Legislature nearly ready to
vote for woman suffrage, and that public opin-
ion was largely in their favor. Women had
already been given many offices of trust and
honor, especially as trustees of schools.
	Mrs. Stanton rose, greeted with enthusiastic
applause, and introduced Mrs. Lockwood as
Lockwood versus the United States. Mrs. L ,
the celebrated lawyer, then made her earnest
and eloquent speech entitled "Waymarks."
She was frequently applauded, and at the
close received a very beautiful bouquet.
	After this there was a sharp discussion be-
tween Dr. Perivs, and Mrs. F. Douglas on the
fifth resolution, which referred to the Presi-
dent's message and his neglect to mention
twenty thousand women citizens. Dr. Pervis
is a very talented man and a very fluent speak-
er, and of course used strong language. He had
taken exception to the phrase "possible bull-
dozing of some of the hapless race in the
South," and felt insulted by any such connec-
tion with the President; he denounced the
policy of President Hayes. Of course there
were loud cheers and applause and hisses. Mr.
Douglas resented the saddling of everything
upon the President, and there was a great deal
of confusion created; people called out "What's

[Column 3]
this to do with woman's rights," etc. Mrs.
Stanton had to rise quite often to call to order.
During the colloquy between Messrs. Pervis
and Douglas, Mrs. Spencer arose and stated that
the resolutions had been drafted with special
care &c. Mrs. Stanton called for a copy of said
resolutions, and thought the offending clause
was in the wrong place. The construction of
the sentence was afterwards changed. Mr.
Pervis closed his remarks by saying; "I speak
for black people because I know that on this
platform they can be spoken of (applause).
Speak for them, because there is no power un-
der heaven to protect them."
	Mrs. Stanton arose and said from the events
of the evening it was very evident there was
need of women in the councils of men. That
we must never forget the right and sacredness
of free speech, which is a woman's right tenet,
and that women must teach their sons when
they go to public meetings to use their tongues
like men and not like serpents. *	* They
must learn the great American lesson of tolera-
tion of free speech.	*	*	*	*
	Mrs. Stanton was enthusiastically applauded.
She gave notice that next winter there would
be some new speakers, both men and women
Her youngest daughter, now studying in the
Boston school of oratory, would be there on
the platform and take part in the Convention.
It was then announced the Convention was
over.	[handwritten] E. B. Wells


HOME AFFAIRS.

	CLASS IN OBSTETRICS.—Having had several
applications to join my winter ob[-]te[-]rical class
rather late, I propose commencing a new class,
beginning May 1st, and ending Nov. 1st, if a
sufficient number apply. Any one desiring to
join this class please advise me by April 15th.
	Further particulars can be had by applicants
by letter.
		ROMANIA B. PRATT, M. D.

	YOUNG LADIES' MUTUAL IMPROVENENT AS-
SOCIATIONS.—The secretaries of the different
branches of the Young Ladies' Mutual Im-
provement Associations in this Stake of Zion
are repsectfully requested to forward their quar-
terly reports at once, so as to be ready for the
Quarterly Conference, to be held in the 14th
Ward Assembly Rooms, March 21 and 22.
	Address Mary P. Freeze. President, P. O.
Box 1183.
		AUGUSTA J. CROCHERON, Secretary.

	WE HAVE been requested by Pres. M. Isa-
bella Horne to announce that the fifth Quar
terly Conference of Salt Lake Stake Relief So-
ciety will convene on Friday and Saturday of
this month, in the 14th Ward Assembly
Rooms, commencing at 10 o'clock, a. m. on Fri-
day. It is expected this will be an interesting
conference, and that the secretaries will each
hand in their reports to this office, as previously
requested, a few days before the meeting, so
that the Secretary, Mrs. E. Howard, may have
time to read and arrange them. We trust the
sisters will feel the importance of attending
this conference, and that they will not only
send in written reports, but be prepared to give
a verbal report of the progress and improve-
ment made through the influence of these or-
ganizations. The Primary Associations of this
county will also please report, that a complete
knowledge of the practical and spiritual devel-
opment of those enrolled in these[-] societies, or
the need of it may be understood.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


OBITUARY 
	DIED, at South Cottonwood, Feb.—, 1879,
Mrs. Sophia Godfrey, wife of James Godfrey,
leaving a husband and large family to mourn
her loss. Sister Godfrey was baptized into this
Church when quite young, and emigrated to
Utah with her father and mother in the first
hand-cart company in 1856. The father was
taken sick on the plains, and she, although a
very young girl, aided her mother very materi-
ally during his illness; he died at the Sweetwa-
ter, and they continued their journey across

faithful to every duty; also a kind and indul-

with the bereaved husband and the orphan
children, deprived of a mother's love and pro-
tecting care, and trnst the Lord will succor
them[-] in this their severe affliction.
		M. G. YOUNG.


[Column 1]
EDITORIAL NOTES.

	WE are very grateful to our friend and
brother, C. R. Savage, Esq., of the Fine Art
Gallery, who presented us recently with an
excellent likeness of President John Taylor
neatly framed; we like to be remembered
in this way, and it is always pleasant to see
the faces of our friends looking down upon
us when at work in the office.

	PRESIDENT ELIZA R. Snow, accompanied
by Pres. M. Isabella Horne, of Salt Lake
Stake R. S., went to Pleasant Green, Brigh-
ton Ward, on Sunday the 9th inst., and
while there assisted Bishop Schoenfeld in
organizing a branch of the Relief Society
in that place. Sister Charlotte Hurst was
elected President, Sisters Ann Bertosh and
Frances Hardman, Counselors, Fanny Jen-
kins, Secretary, Alice Crockwell, Assistant
Secretary, and Sarah Coon, Treasurer.
This makes thirty-six branches of R. S. in
Salt Lake County.

	WE RECEIVED from our dear friend and
co-worker Mrs. Sara Andrews Sp[-]ncer, in
pamphlet form the Majority and Minor-
ity Report of the United States Senate Com-
mittee on Privileges and Elections for and 
against "A Sixteenth Amendment to the
Constitution prohibiting the several States
from disfranchising United States citizens
on account of sex. The Minority report
was made by Senators Hoar of Mass., Mitch-
ell of Oregon, and Cameron of Wisconsin.
The pamphlet arrived too late for us to
make much mention of it, but will give
our readers the opinions of the Minority
as soon as practicable in their own words.

	THE FIRST Quarterly Meeting of the
Primary Association of Farmington was
held in the Farmington Meeting House
Saturday, March 8th. Mrs. Aurelia Spen-
cer Rogers presiding. By invitation we
attended, accompanied by Mrs. Louie Felt
and Miss Maggie Freeze, the President and
Secretary of the 11th ward P. A. of this
city. There were 133 children present, and
good order and quiet was observed by all.
The exercises were short and very interest-
ing, especially the singing which was spirit-
ed and melodious. Mrs. Felt told them
something of the manner in which she con-
ducted her Association; the lady is a very

[Column 2]
pleasing speaker and cannot fail to interest
children. Miss Freeze, a child of 13, spoke
to that large assembly without hesitation;
it was wonderful to see this little girl
stand up and bear her testimony in such a 
sincere and earnest manner, and such a
growing interest among the young cannot
fail to bring about great and grand results.

	THE PRIMARY Association of the Elev-
enth Ward gave a grand Jubilee on Wed-
nesday evening, the 12th inst. Mrs. Louie
Felt with her counselors had charge of the
entertainment, and conducted it so perfectly
as to make it a grand success. Some of 
the children were only five years old, yet
performed their part nicely. The opening
scene was a chorus, "Sweet Bye and Bye,"
by seventy-five children, which was an in-
spiring melody chanted forth by so many
fresh young voices. The song Alice Ben
Bolt was rendered by a child five years old,
Laura Denney; she sang it through without
being prompted. There was a great variety
of songs, dialogues and recitations all by
little children. The performance closed
with the Charade of Matrimony, the part
of Lady's Maid was acted in a very charm-
ing and sprightly manner by Miss Maggie
Freeze. These Juvenile entertainments
wsll develop capabilities otherwise dormant
in the little folks, and they must have recre-
ation.


[Column 1]	
	RESPECT AND SELF-CONTROL.

	I am often led to reflect on the lack of
respect that is shown in our intercourse
with each other, especially among the most
intimately associated—husbands and wives,
parents and children, brothers and sisters,
and companions bound together by strong
and holy ties, are all more or less guilty;
and it always appeared strange to me that
disrespect should be more common among
those who have every reason to love, than
it is among friends or mere acquaintances.
It cannot spring from lack of affection, be-
cause it is often the case that those who
love most are the most guilty; as the poet
says,

	"Oft for our own the bitter tone,
	Tho we love our own the best."

	It does not spring from want of thought,
because we can nearly always be thoughtful
enough to avoid the error while in the pre-
sence of strangers; so I have to come to the
conclusion that the most fruitful source of
disrespect is the want of self-control. The
adversary often tries us sorely through our
nearest and dearest ones. 'Tis often they
who force us to drink the very dregs of bit-
terness, surely not because they design to
afflict us, but they do not gain the mastery
over the angry thought, or impatient word,
that always makes such deep wounds; for
though unkind words may fall apparently
unheeded, they never fail to make an un-
favorable impression.
	How often would we save ourselves the
humiliation of seeking forgiveness, if we
would seek to be governed more by princi-
ple and less by impulse. The older we
grow the more clearly will we see the beauty
and wisdom of self-control. It will shield
us from so many of the little vexatious
trials that spring entirely from our own
fault no matter how much we may think
others to blame. Some people pride them-
selves upon always "speaking as they feel;"
that is very good if they always feel right,
and if they do they must be exceptionally
good; but in case they should sometimes
feel as "cross as two sticks," gloomy and
impatient, they had better either be silent

[Column 2]
or "speak as they don't feel;" a little de-
ception might be better in such a case.
	We will never regret checking ourselves
when about to say something very sarcastic
and taunting, something which an evil
spirit would convince us was just the right
thing to say; but oh, how many times do
we regret the want of self-control and power
to resist the tempter. We are all capable
of inflicting pain, and often do even when
studying to avoid it, and the heart cannot
be right where there is premeditation. I
think we allow our personal feelings to in-
fluence us too much in our conduct toward
each other. We do not stop to consider a
person's age, their superior qualities and
the many claims they have upon our respect,
or even reverence, but should they offend
us, we consider ourselves perfectly justified
in treating them with any degree of disre-
spect that will satisfy a feeling of revenge,
for call it what you will it is revenge.
	Manifestations of our deep and tender
affection are not proper and wise at all
times, but respect and kindness are never
out of place; they are acceptable to all.
Especially should we be guarded in our de-
portment towards parents in the presence
of their children, for who feels capable of
bearing the responsibility of teaching, by
example, a child to disrespect its parents.
Doubtless all parents have failings that they
are striving hard to overcome, are they to
be discovered by their children? Who
would be cruel and heartless enough to
draw the vail aside, teach a child to disre-
gard that binding command to "Honor thy
father and thy mother?"
	Need I speak upon the duty of respect to
the aged? That should be among our first
lessons, and never lost sight of. There may
be things that we all understand, but it is
good and may be very profitable for some of
us to have our minds refreshed upon sub-
jects that so greatly affect our every day
happiness, and everthing that tends to our
peace and happiness is of the Gospel, and
should claim our study. May we each seek
to be guided by the Spirit of God in every
thought, word and action, and thus will we
be inspired to respect the rights, privileges
and feelings of each other, preserving peace
in our own hearts while we are laboring
here on earth for that never-fading crown
of glory that is laid up for those who falter
not by the wayside, but endure faithful to 
the end.
		LILLIE FREEZE.


FROM LAIE.

	The following items are from a private
letter written by Sister Jane E. Molen,
Feb. 5th, 1879.

	"I have just been preparing a mess of
string beans to cook for dinner; I think you
could enjoy our beans, raddishes and tur-
nips. We are having rains off and on every
day; our "dry Laie" is looking so green
and fresh it makes our Elders feel quite en-
couraged.	*	*	*	We are looking for
Brother Richards every day. He was well
when last heard from. The mission is
slowly progressing, the health of the Elders
usually good. Brother Alexander and wife
live with us; they are a nice intelligent
young couple. Sister Dean lives in the
yard, and we are all feeling first rate and
thankful things are so prosperous with us,
so far away from home and Zion. Brother
Molen joins in kind regards to yourself and
family, and to all friends who may inquire
after us"


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	[Prom the "Philadelphia Times," Jan. 19, 1879.]
MORMON LADIES CALLING AT THE 
WHITE HOUSE.

	On Saturday, when Mrs Hayes had her first
reception, the two Mormon ladies, Mrs. Em-
meline B. Wells and Mrs. Zina Young Wil-
liams, Miss Anthony, Mrs. Spencer, of this 
city and others called, and the President, when
they asked a hearing from him, appointed
Monday at 10 a. m.
	The two Mormon wives named, one of
whom, Mrs. Williams, is the daughter of Brig-
ham Young,and the other wife of Ex-Mayor
Wells, of Salt Lake, went to the White House
promptly at the hppolnted hour, accompanies
by Mrs. Matilda Joslyn Gage and Mrs. Spencer.
These two ladies and Mrs. Wells, of Utah, were
of the committee appointed by the Suffrage
Association. Miss Rachel C. Foster and Miss
Julia T. Foster, of Philadelphia, Miss Wood-
bury of this city, and myself were the only
others present during the interview with the 
P[-]esident, which began at the hour named.
Both Mr. and Mrs. Hayes are punctual in keep-
ing appointments, although they frequently
make them for half-past 9 a. m.  Their house
is always in order, too, by that time.  The
bed-chambers into which Mrs. Hayes has some-
times taken me as early as 10 o'clock, look as if
no one had used them the previous night,
though all are occupied every night.
	The President invited the ladies named into 
the library, as being more secure from interrup-
tion than his office.  The resolutions of the 
Suffrage Associations were first read to him by
Mrs. Spencer and he listened with interest,
though he sometimes wore an amused smile as
he was reproached for his neglect in failing to 
mention the political, civil, or social rights of 
women. He occasionally asked questions cour-
teously, and I was amazed, having seldom wit-
nessed a similar interview, at the direct, con-
cise, forcible and yet invariably respectful re-
plies given by the ladies. They had evidently
carefully considered what they wished to say,
and said it as briefly as possible, determined to
lose nothing by boring their auditor (the only
man in the room), who was, on his part doing
the polite thing in every way. Is not this
progress when the chief of a great nation is
willing to receive from a band of women, ap-
pointed by women, suggestions as to affairs of
state and a reproach for past omissions?
	PLEA OF THE MORMON WIVES.
	But the beauty of the interview to me was
when the two Mormon ladies laid their case
before the President and he showed such
kindly sympathy with them when they proved
what misery would follow in Utah the enforce-
ment of the act of 1862 against polygamy.  He
told them he would not trust so important a 
matter to his memory of a verbal interview,
but wished them to put their case in writing
and lay before him.  He seemed pained, evi-
dently, reflecting how little he could do to help
them since the United States Supreme Court
has rendered a decision within a month against
polygamy in Utah.
	How favorable an impression the party pro-
duced on Mr. Hayes is best proved by his offer
to bring his wife in to see them, a proposition
gratefully accepted.
	I must not omit to mention just here that
while the ladies of the Suf[-]rage Association
showed a decided sympathy with the Mormen
ladies, who had stated that by the enforcement
of the act of 1862 many thousand women,
would be made outcasts and their children il-
legitimate before the world, Mrs. Gage and Mrs.
Spencer were careful to explain to the Presi-
dent exactly on what ground the National

[Column 2]
Woman's Suffrage Association had received
the Utah delegates.  They said the Association
must not be construed as taking part in any
other branch of the Mormon question except
that of female suffrage. The women of Utah
have enjoyed the right of suffrage and now 
Congress proposes to take it from them, and 
the Association which met here last week con-
tends that as Congress has repeatedly refused
to interfere with State or Territorial govern-
ments to grant suffrage to women, it should 
not interfere to take it away when local au-
thorities have conferred it.
	Mrs. Hayes heard the Mormon wives with
such earnest sympathy as to completely win
their hearts.  She listened and spoke to them
in a sisterly manner that aroused in all present
a feeling of reverence for her noble, womanly
nature. She invited the party to visit all the 
private apartments, and, when they left, sent
them through the conservatories.
		MISS GRUNDY.
THE WOMEN OF UTAH. MRS. BLAKE'S 
ANALYSIS OF THEIR PLEA FOR PO-
LYGAMY.
	At the recent National Woman Suffrage
Convention in Washington there were present
two delegates from the Territory of Utah. They
were Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells, editor of the
WOMAN'S EXPONENT of Salt Lake City, and
Mrs. Zina Young Williams, a daughter of Brig-
ham Young. They were both of them women
of culture, well dressed and well appearing.
Mrs. Wells was a lady of perhaps fifty, with a
pleasing, intellectual face. Mrs. Williams was
young and very good looking.  They were both
Mormons, apparently from devout, religious
belief, and had come East principally "plural
marriages," as they preferred to call their social
institutions.  In pusuance of this wish they
have within the last few weeks had several
hearing before the Judiciary Committee of
the Senate, on which they have presented their
views with force and clearness.
* * * * It was quite evident
that both these ladies were by conviction firm
believers in the divine origin of their faith,
and that they had in a large degree the tem-
perament that may perhaps be described as
eminently pious, or spiritual, in the best sense
of the word--a temperament which causes its
possessors to cling closely to their faith, and, if
necessary, to suffer martyrdom for it.  In
speaking of their peculiar institution they both
admitted that there were elements in it which
were painful to a woman's heart, but added
that they were doing what was ordained by
their religion, which commanded as a sacred
duty that they should share those attentions
which a selfish human instinct would cause
them to wish to retain undivided.
* * * * Mrs. Wells was careful
to say that they did not consider all men fitted,
either by nature or outward circumstances, for
plural marriage.  It was only men of principle,
of large, kindly hearts, who were proper per-
sons to have more than one wife. Nor did she
feel that this portion of their system was adapt-
ed to all communities, but thought it would be
especially desirable for rural regions where
women are in excess in the population, and
urged that in New England, for instance, the 
army of celibate women would be better off
as wives of good men, even though they shared
the honors, than passing to their graves in the
childless coldness of single life. We can fancy
that the dignified old maids of Massachusetts
would indignantly resent so base a suggestion,
[Column 3]
but we give it in justice to a really conscien-
tious thinker. * * *
	The women of Utah have enjoyed the right
of suffrage for eight years, and it was at first
supposed that political freedom would at once
cause them to tear off the yoke of polygamy;
yet this was surely a hasty conclusion, since
there was no more reason to think that giving
them the ballot would move them to disbelieve
in their religion than to imagine that men, on
reaching manhood and being able to vote
would change their religious convictions. We
do believe that political freedom cannot fail,
with the increased facilities for education, and 
freedom of thought which will accompany it,
to lead women gradually to throw off the
shackles of superstition, while their more spir-
itual uatures will still cause them to cling to 
all that is true and pure in religion; but it is
not to be expected that such a result will follow
at once, or that Mormon women, with their
most sacred affections twined about the insti-
tution of plural marriage would violently re-
pudiate it.  Yet the right of suffrage has al-
ready had for these women the most beneficial
results. It has led to the formation among
them of societies for mutual education and im-
provement. These associations meet through-
out the whole Territory, and women are con-
stantly employed in visiting the different so-
cieties to inform their members upon topics of
interest and importance. Already the influ-
ence of the women as a power is felt in the
body politic, while it is quite evident that free
thought on all topics is doing its work among
them. Of course it could not be expected that
they would make the first use of their liberties
by tearing down their homes, and the fact that
these Mormon women cling to these homes,
poor as they are, ought to silence forever those
objectors who maintain that enfranchising wo-
men would destroy the home.
`		LILLIE DEVEREUX BLAKE.
—The Evening Telegram, N. Y., Feb. 11, 1879.
	The extracts published above were written
by ladies whom we met while in Washington.
Miss Grundy (a fictitious name by the way)
boarded at the same hotel, consequently we
saw her often.  She is a pleasing and popular
writer, a regular correspendent of the "Phila-
dephia Times" and "Boston Herald," and al-
luded to us in both, in a way which was very
kind and thoughtful, and which we appreciat-
ed. She writes also for Washington papers,
and indeed I think her letters upon society are
worthy a place in any journal or magazine.
She mingles in the best society, and is there-
fore competent to write of it, and she is on in-
timate terms with Mrs. Hayes.  She is young,
and in every respect a very sensible girl, not
spoiled with flattery at all. She is general
favorite with the ladies at the hotel, and to us
she was extremely kind.
Mrs. Lillie Devereux Blake is very witty and
very sarcastic; our readers will remember we
have republished many of her scathing articles.
Mrs. Blake is uncommonly gifted, and as
shrewd a woman as we ever met. She dresses
well, and contrary to the general argument
used by men in regard to women who defend
the woman question on the public platform,
has a nice home and no doubt a happy one,
beautiful daughters, and a husband all her
own. She speaks for women everywhere boldly,
defiantly perhaps, but certainly positively.
She has written us fairly as one could who
stands outside and knows nothing of the spirit
of the latter-day work.  She says the women's
organizatiens here have originated from our
having the Ballot. This is an error we wish to
correct, but one that strangers who do not un-
derstand Mormon women's home-missionary


work would naturally fall into.  That we
should understand the principles of govern-
ment is essential in our opinion, whether we
enjoy the franchise or are denied its privileges.
Our sisters should seek to inform themselves in
regard to all the existing forms of government
and in fact the ancient patriarchal manner of
governing. There are abundant opportunities
given the women of our day to make them-
selves acquainted with politics, and it is not at
all probable, as Mrs. Blake has said, that en-
franchising women will destroy the home.  It
has been proven here that such is not the case;
for have not Latter-day Saint women voted
for nine years, and what complaint can be
brought to condemn woman suffrage from
their experience?  Friends of woman's suffrage
in the House and Senate remarked to us they
would protest against the suffrage being taken
from our women.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


PIPSEY PAPERS.

	What extraordinary pains the Gentile la-
dies are taking to suppress that blotch on
the body politic, which we hold so sacred,
viz., Plural Marriage!  If there is anything
morally or religiously wrong in the practice
of it, it is to be hoped they will succeed;
that is, in view of their introducing a more
moral and elavating mode of carrying out
God's great command to multiply and re-
plenish the earth.  He endowed us with
principles and desires pertaining thereto,
and gave laws whereby it should be carried
out in purity.
	Marriage was instituted as a principle
whereby virtuous love might be fostered
and protected, all intercourse outside of that
being condemned as unlawful and unholy.
The marriage ceremony enjoins upon men
and women to be true to themselves and to
each other.  It is usual for either men or
women, after the death of their relative
spouse, to marry again, but according to
the doctrines of modern Christianity they
must not marry a second wife while the
first is living.  Death releases their obliga-
tions.  But the marriage tie as revealed in
these last days binds for time and for all
eternity; therefore, if a wife dies, her hus-
band must spend the remainder of his life
alone or must of necessity have a plurality
of wives.  It is not the law of God, neither
does it accord with woman's physical con-
stitution, that she should marry several
husbands at one time, but if the parties are
agreed and are true to each other, what is
there wrong or degrading in a man having
several wives?  If he supports them, ac-
knowledging their claims and raising their
children in respectability, what is there do-
grading in the principle?  In the olden
time men after God's own heart practiced
polygamy, and we read of jealousy and dis-
satisfaction sometimes arising, as is fre-
quently the case at the present time.  There
is also jealousy and dissatisfaction in mo-
nogamous life, the marriage relation being
frequently severed.  But that in no wise
condemns marriage, nor degrades the law
of home-ties and influence, of which mar-
riage, is the foundation, although the argu-
ment is often used by men who wish to live
single, for not entering into the marriage
relationship.
	Polygamy in a moral or religious view
we have no intention at present to discuss.
If people who practice it are all pure in
heart, or correct in their judgmont, are
matters we are not open to judge, and over
which we have no control; but when we
look around us and see the corruption in
the world, and our comparative free-
dom from licentious practices we are led to
infer that our systems and principles are by
no means to be condemned.  There is no
degree or condition in life but has its pecu-
liar trials connected therewith.  It is not
polygamy as a principle that our enemies
are exercised over, that is a mere pretence
-a handle with which to turn the rack
whereon they would stretch us morally, re-
gardless of the physical suffering it might
cause us to endure.
	It was long ago the cry that Mormonism
must be put down, no matter what the cost
of suffering, murder and rapine, it must be
put down.  But how?  That is a problem
they have vainly tried to solve for over forty
years, and the solution still seems a mys-
tery.  Do they not know that if not sup-
ported and protected by Almighty God, our
peculiar institutions must of themselves
die out in time, and that opposition but
fans the flame which they are trying so hard
to extinguish.  That by their slanderous
and unwarrantable attacks they bring us to
the notice of the thinking public, and peo-
ple will investigate our cause who have
never before given us much thought.
	Who are those that are coming to the
front with their abusive epithets and their
heroic efforts to overthrow our established
religion?  Pity their heroism was not ex-
ercised in a better cause.  The leaders en-
raged in this undertaking are many of them
of those who once professed to believe in
the same doctrines, who have borne their
testimony to the work; whose fathers,
mothers, or sisters have laid down their
lives, perhaps, while promulgating the doc-
trines they professed.  They have joined
hands with us in the bonds of sisterhood,
have sat at our firesides, and partaken of
our cheer; they have been protected and
sustained by the ties the bind a growing
community.  And now they would raise
their hands against us and strike a dagger
to our hearts.  Sisters, I know them, I am
personally acquainted with several of them.
My childhood was passed in their society,
and their names are connected with some
of my earliest recollections.  We danced
at the same parties, shared the same rustic
amusements, and the same religious ser-
vices.  We worshipped at the same altar
and prayed to the same God.  We saw
nothing then to shock our sense of proprie-
ty or interfere with our conscientious prin-
ciples.  Our paths have widened and sepa-
rated; what their experience has been I am
unable to say, but I will venture to suggest
that of all the strong language they use,
and the terms they apply to us, they know
comparatively nothing.  We have no home-
less waifs cast on the tender mercies of pub-
lic charity.  Our religion forbids infanti-
cide, and its institutions protect women
against it and its concomitant evils.  We
have no starving poor, no licensed dens of
gambling and infamy.  We are  sober, in-
dustrious people, trying to pursue the avo-
cations of daily life in peace and content-
ment; we ask only to be let alone, and that
we do most emphatically insist on.  We
want none of the modern vices which ouor
Gentile citizens are introducing into our
midst, but they are forced into our midst,
to the detriment of the manners and morals
of our young people.  I saw a pitiful sight
a few days ago while visiting in Salt Lake
City.  I saw two women locked in jail-
they had both been ruined and abandoned.
Was it polygamy that had done this?  I
assure you it was not.  Salt Lake City is
the only city in Utah that is to any extent
populated by the element that are so anx-
ious abont us-the pure (?) in heart who are
so shocked by our sense of morality that
they would fain bring a ranting mob into
our midst, and turn us from our homes and
firesides. And what for?  For advocating
those principles which protect virtue and
innocence. I would recommend that they
sweep the streets of Salt Lake City and
clear from among us the vices that attach
to their own apron strings, before they at-
tack a principle that in and of itself at least
protects its victims, if such they may be
called.  Let them watch their own hus-
bands, and find if their conduct will bear
inspection, before they take a course to rid
our "poor, deluded women" of their natural
protectors and open up an avenue in society
for more corrupting influences.
	Provo.	Mary.


WOMAN'S VOICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	My dear sisters of the Relief Societies
and friends of truth: This seems to be a
day fraught with great events; things that
have been plainly predicted by our Lord
and Savior, and also by all the holy pro-
phets both ancient and modern, are now
daily fulfilling before our eyes, and we see
the spirit that Jesus spoke of being made
manifest, even the spirit of betrayal, not
only among the Saints, but all over the
world, and no doubt that spirit will increase
more and more until the Savior comes.
How much it stands us in hand to watch
over ourselves and cultivate the Spirit of
God, and have our minds stored with that
eternal truth that will guide us in all the
acts of our lives. There are many things
coming along to try us to the very center,
and if our minds are not staid on God our
Father with faith and confidence, we may
give way in the hour that we would need
the most firmness and strength. My heart
is drawn out in prayer to my Father to pro-
tect His daughters from the influence of
this evil spirit, and to increase the light
and life of the Saints, that we may be
found on the watch-tower holding on to the
Rod of Iron, which is the word of God, and
be able to say as Jesus did, "The Prince of
this world cometh, but hath nothing in
me."
	We are a small Society here in Cedar
Fort, but we have embraced the same Gos-
pel that our sisters in Salt Lake City have,
and have gathered here for the same pur-
pose, namely, to be taught of the Lord and
to enjoy the blessings, ordinances and sa-
craments of His holy House; and when we
heard of the Anti-Polygamy Crusade, our
President called a Mass Meeting of the two
settlements, and we drafted resolutions
corresponding with the faith of the Gos-
pel that we have received, resolved to
to stand by the revelations that we have re-
ceived from the Lord through Joseph Smith
the Prophet, by the help and assistance of
our Father and God, trusting in His Al-
mighty arm for protection, firmly believing
that He is able to save to the uttermost all
those who put their trust in Him. We there-
fore had a desire to be represented in your
valuable paper.
	Hoping that the blessings of God may
rest upon your labors, and upon the lawful
labors of all the daughters of Zion is the
prayer of your sisters in the Gospel of peace,

Sophronia Cook, President, R. S.

S. J. Meeks,	}	Counselors,
P. Rodeback,	}

Martha Wilcox, Secretary,
Elizabeth Berry, Treasurer.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


LETTER

			No. 2652 OLIVE STREET.
		St. Louis, Mo. Feb. 20th, 1879.
MRS. EMMELINE B. WELLS : — DEAR
	MADAM:
	I have been authorized and requested to
give notice that the annual meeting of the
National Woman Suffrage Association will
be held at St. George's Hall, corner of 7th
and Locust streets, St Louis, on the 7th,
8th and 9th days of May next; on which
occassion you are most cordially invited
and requested to be present.
	It is believed that the time is auspicious
for making this the most important meet-
ing of the Association ever held, for which
purpose no efforts will be spared to make
the attendance as large and influential as
possible, so that it may prove to be a veri-
table "Ecumenical Council" of the friends
of Woman's enfranchisement. I am as-
sured that delegates will be in attendance
from every section of the country, more
especially from the South, and what is term-
ed, the Great North-West
	I have every reason to believe that the
Convention will be well worth your attend-
ance, and do much to speed the cause we
have so much at heart.
	Every attention will be paid to delegates,
and accommodations secured for them, as
far as possible, in private families.
	I trust you will give this notice as wide
a publicity as possible, and inform me
whether or not, you will attend.
	With much respect,
			Yours Truly,
				VIRGINIA L, MINOR,
			Vice President for Missouri


THE HARP IN HEAVEN.

	One of the sweetest recollections of my
girlhood is a beautiful reply my mother
once made me, when my heart was swell-
ing with childish grief.
	I had just returned from the house of a
wealthy neighbor, who had kindly given
me the use of their piano for a few hours
every day to gratify my extreme love for
music. Our own cottage home looked so
plain in contrast with t[-]e one I had just
left, and no piano within its walls, I laid
my head upon the table and gave vent to my
overflowing heart. I felt grieved, and per-
haps a little angry, that we were unable to
afford the one thing I desired above all
others—a piano—and expressed my feel-
ings to my mother.
	Never shall I forget her sweet, gentle
tone, as she simply replied, "Never mind,
daughter, if you cannot have a piano on
earth, you may have a harp in heaven."
Instantly the whole current of my feelings
was changed. Earthly things dwindled
into insignificance, and the "harp in heav-
en," with its golden strings, became the
object of my desire. I felt reproved for my
repinings against the Providence that had
placed me in a humble home, and from
that moment the enjoyment of heaven
seemed far to outweigh all the plesures of
earth. That beautiful reply has followed
me all my life, or rather has gone before
me like a bright guiding star—lifting my
thoughts above this transient life, and
opening to my spirit's vision the glorious
scenes in that "land of life and light." I
have a "piano on earth" now, but its charm
is gone. Its music no longer gladdens my
heart as it once did, for the ears that loved
best to listen to its sweet tones, are now
enraptured with the grand harmonies of


heaven. The dear fingers that so often
touched its keys now sweep the golden
harp-strings. O, that "harp in heaven!"
How my soul longs for one breath of its
rich melody!
	As I look upon the dear baby fingers in
the cradle near me' I think it matters little
whether my child be poor or rich—whether
her path be strewn with thorns or flowers—
if she may only have a "harp in heaven."
—Mother's Journal.


GENGHIS KHAN AND THE OWL.

	Nearly seven hundred years ago, a
young Mongolian prince named Genghis (or
Zinghis) while engaged in warfare with a
Tartar tribe, was defeated in battle, and so
hotly chased by his enemies that he was
like to be captured. His shattered defend-
ers lost sight of him, and he fled alone in
momentary peril of death. Exhausted at
last, and with scarcely a hope of saving
himself, he plunged into a thicket, and lay
listening with beating heart while the Tar-
tars came on, raging for his blood. They
knew he had disappeared somewhere in
the immediate vicinity, and to his horror
they stopped directly before his hiding-
place. Instead of searching the bushes,
however, they held a short consultation
and passed on; and from the words he over-
heard, Genghis learned the cause of his de-
liverance. As the pursuers approached, a
white owl was seen sitting calmly on a tree
directly over the thicket. It flew away,
frightened at their appearance and noise,
and the barbarian warriors said, "If the
man had been here, that bird would have
been disturbed before. Let us go on."
	And so Genghis was saved, to become
one of the conquerors of the world, and Em-
peror of all Tartary and Northern China and
Eastern Persia. In grateful remembrance
of his preservation, he and his followers
adopted the snowy owl as their guardian
ensign, and ever after the picture of the
bird was displayed on the Mongol and Kal-
muck battle-flags.				T. B.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
Vol. 7	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, APRIL 1, 1879.		No. 21.


CHARITY.

		A PLEA FOR PRODIGALS.

	Lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.—
Jesus' words.

	And though I bestow all my goods to feed the POOR, and
though I give my body to be burned and have not Charity
it profiteth me nothing.—St. Paul.

Out in the wilderness, out in the night,
By misfortune or misery mystified quite;
From one evil fleeing and turning aside
To evils still greater, unseen and untried;
Dangers behind them, before and below—
How shall the erring know which way to go?

There are serpents too subtle to warn ere they sting,
There are serpents that charm ere upon you they spring;
The wise may escape, but the simple and young
Too oft are betrayed by the flatterer's tongue;
Evil designers that speak you so fair,
More to be dreaded than howling wolves are.
You may arm and prepare for a clamorous foe,
But these silent ones aim to destroy at a blow;
Like the treacherous tiger, remorseless to slay—
Creeping and crouching to pounce on their prey—
The tender and trusting and feeble they seek,
'Tis the nature of brutes to destroy what is weak.
If oppressor, or tempter, assaileth the strong,
There is power, there is force to resist what is wrong.
Since Eve in the garden did Satan believe,
He knows very well who and how to deceive.
For an innoc[-]nt woman an apple sufficed;
But the world and its treasures was offered to Christ;
Who else would have measured the enemy's chain,
Or refused with a word such a princely domain?
All have not the wisdom and courage combined
To say to the tempter "Hence, get thee behind."
Temptations that move not the prosperous few
Oft try others poorer, more honest and true;
Full many would stumble who never did fall.
If they ever were troubled and tempted at all;
And many would err that in goodness delight,
If they trusted no God to direct them aright;
The soul like the body would oft be crushed down,
If He looked not in mercy instead of a frown.

Lo! in the edge of the desert a gleam!
Look up! thou lost one, a light it doth seem;
Pray! that the light may be steady and clear;
Pray! that thy duty may plainly appear.
Oh! there is One watching over thee now
Whose pity extendeth to all such as thou,
Trust Him to guide thee, nor fear aught amiss,
The tiger's fierce growl, or the serpent's low hiss;
Courage, press forward thy troubles shall end,
And thou shalt be safe in the house of a friend.

Open the door, for the night it is dreary;
Open the door for the weak and the weary;
Open it quickly and welcome them in—
Many are sinned against more than they sin.
"Judge not," said Jesus, be merciful too,
Lest ye be judged by one greater than you.
Hold the light downward, the lost silver seek,
Then call in the neighbors, the kind and the meek,
And rejoice that "'tis found," for our Father above
Declared, through a being of infinite love,
That more joy was felt by the angels divine
O'er a lost one restored than o'er ninety and nine
So screened from the storm in a sheltering fold,
Safe even in darkness from dangers untold,
Without a temptation to stray from the track,
Too faithful, or favor'd, to want to turn back.

Who never was tried, or betrayed with a snare,
Or lost in the night of oppression and care,
Or felt what the erring or tempted endure,
Should censure but lightly; compassion doth cure.
Enough, that a merciful God gives command
To extend to the penitent freely the hand.
Shall we, Pharisee-like, dare to boastingly say,
"We thank Thee, O Lord, we are better than they?"

					EMILY HILL WOODMANSEE.

	Salt Lake City.


[Column 1]
WOMAN'S VOICE.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Why do the Heathen rage, and the peo-
ple imagine a vain thing? The kings of
the earth set themselves, and the rulers
take counsel together, against the Lord, and
against his anointed saying, "Let us break
their bands asunder, and cast away their
cords from us." Psalms, ii 1, 2, 3.
	I am reminded of this saying of David's,
when I hear and also read, the acts of Con-
gress (the bills before that body) for the
destruction of this people, they seem very
fearful that the prophets words will be lit-
erally fulfilled. (I wonder if they read the
Bible) They are doing all they can to ful-
fill the decrees of Jehovah, whether they
know it or not.
	The Jews said, in the day of Jesus, that
if we let him alone, all men will believe in
him, and the Romans shall come and take
away our place and nation. John, xi 43.
	In 1830 this church was organized; it
numbered very few members, but it re-
ceived the same attention then, from the
Clergy, as it now does from the Govern-
ment, of the nation. They wished to crush
it in its infancy, it was then said of us,
"these Mormons are preaching to the Indi-
ans, they are in opposition to the govern-
ment, they vote to one interest, they are
going to protect their homes and their
families, we will drive them, we will exter-
minate them.
	In 1844 this feeling of enmity had spread
through Missouri and into the State of Ill-
inois. They took the prophets of God, Jo-
seph and Hyrum Smith, and did unto them
that which they had long desired, they
murdered them while under the protection
of the Governor of the State of Illinois; and
that same Governor, Thomas Ford, in a long
message to the House of Representatives,
screened the murderers. (one of his plea's
reads thus:)
	"But the great cause of popular fury was,
that the Mormons at several preceeding
elections had cast their votes as a unit." (As
if American citizens are not free to vote as
they choose). Unity is a crime when con-
nected with religion, according to the views
of Governor Ford and others of his class,
and will bring violence and mobocracy.
These enemies of the Mormons understand
that unity is strength, and they say if we
can divide them, bring discord among
them, follow Balaams advice, send an army
among them of young men, to seduce their
wives and daughters, and corrupt them, we
can overthrow them, and then we can feast
ourselves, as wolves on the carcasses of
the slain.
	But they know not the way of the Al-
mighty. The wicked shall not always
triumph. The Lord will come out of his
hiding place, and vex the nations in his
sore displeasure, when their cup of iniquity
is full. The Lord has spoken it by the
mouth of his holy prophets.
	But now here is another thing that must
be attended to (this polygamy) O. S. Holis-
ter Esq. in a question to President Taylor,
inquired, was not the great object, of the
institsution of polygamy to rapidly increase
the numbers of adherents to the chruch?

[Column 2]
	What could have induced that question?
	Is he and Congress, afraid that this peo-
ple will increase so fast that they out-num-
ber their enemies?
	Pharaoh was afraid of the Israelites, in
the same way and he commanded all the
male children born among the Israelites, to
be put to death. Exodus i I6: But he did
not thwart the purposes of God, though he
put into execution this cruel law.
	Herod, when the wise men were seeking
for the child Jesus endeavored by crafti-
ness, to learn where he was that he might
kill him. But an angel warned Joseph in
a dream, to take the young child and his
mother, and flee into Egypt. And then
Herod * * slew all male children that
were in Bethlehem, and in all the coast
therof from two years old and under. *
Matthew ii 16.
	How determined he was to bring to
naught, the purposes of God, how signally
he failed, and what a miserable death he
died. Acts xii 23.
	This is the work of God, and man with
all his devices cannot overthrow it.
								KANAB.


NOTES AND NEWS.

	GEORGE ELIOT is in poor health, and will
probably write no more novels.—EX.
	JANE EYRE dramatized has been present-
ed upon the stage in Lowell, Mass. by Gen-
evieve Ward.
	IT IS said Queen Victoria has expressed
a great desire to visit Canada. Report
says her son the Prince of Wales is encour-
aging her to do so and also to visit the
United States.
	IN Philadelphia, March 13, at the annual
commencement of the Woman's Medical
College, the degree of Doctor of Medicine
was conferred on twenty lady graduates
from various States in the Union.—EX.
	PRINCESSE LOUISE MARGARET, of Prussia
was married, March 13th, to Queen Vic-
toria's son Arthur, Duke of Connaught, at
Windsor Castle. The affair was exceeding-
ly brilliant, and the English papers dovote
column after column to the dresses of the
ladies and the elegant paraphernalia of the
whole affair.
	THE bill for Woman Suffrage in Colorado
passed the Senate, and in the House the
vote stood seventeen to tweny-four, and
not fifty-four as the Denver "Tribune" re-
ported. So that there was only a majority
of seven against it. Judge Haynes cham-
pioned the bill and earned the thanks of all
women, as did others who rendered good
service.—EX.
	ANNA METZ Byland's "Women of Pro-
gress" a neat little paper of four pages is
now in its first volumn. We have just
received a copy the first we have ever seen
of it, it is full of interesting reading matter
and certainly deserves patronage. One of
its mottoes is; If the public want firstrate
newspapers they must pay for them liberal-
ly. We should be pleased to have the pa-
per added to our list of exchanges, as we
have no WOMAN'S paper published in the
City of New York.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
		R. S. REPORTS.

					KANAB, Jan. 20, 1879.

	The Annual meeting of the Relief Socie-
ty of Kanab was held in the Schoolhouse,
Dec. 5th, 1878, at 2 p. m., Mrs. Harriet
Bunting presiding. After the usual exer-
cises and reading of minutes the Financial
report was read as follows:

Receipts.............................................$ 80 00
Profit on work...................................   46 20
								———
		Total					126 20
	Disbursements.
To the poor........................................      2 35
To Manti Temple...............................    28 50
For stationery....................................          50
								———
		Total disbursements              31 35

	In the Treasury:
Merchandise......................................     35 68
Sundries.............................................      22 07
Cash....................................................        5 60
Wheat, 21 bu.....................................      31 60
								   ———
		Total					    94 95

	Mrs. Elizabeth H D. Solomon resigned
her office as Treasurer, on account of mov-
ing to Arizona, and Mrs. Lovina A. Farns-
worth was appointed Treasurer, and Mrs.
Sabina A. Oliphant Assistant Secretary.
	President Bunting spoke upon the neces-
sity of duty and obedience and of listening
to the still small voice, and said the plan of
life and salvation is well defined. Sisters,
let us walk therein!
	Pres. Elizabeth Nuttall exhorted the sis-
ters to duty, and they would have faith
given them to rebuke sickness in the name
of Jesus. Coun. Lucy A. Johnson spoke
upon secret prayer.
Bishop W. D. Johnson, Jun., said we were
placed here to beautify the earth and to do
good. No blessing is oblained without la-
bor. The sisters, through their faithful-
ness, can do a vast amount of good. Let
them seek for the Spirit of the Lord, and
they will have faith to obtain any blessing
they need. There had been fifty cases of
Typhoid fever in Kanab, and only one
death; the Lord had blessed the people
through the administration of the Elders.
Let us be very thankful for these blessings,
and give God the glory.
	After singing and prayer meeting ad-
journed.
	The sisters of Kanab have just com-
menced to store up grain. We have lumber
to make a temporary granary, and hope the
coming season to do much more.
	We appreciate the privilege of living
where we have no contaminating influences
to contend with. We fully endorse the
sentiments and resolutions of our sisters
North, which we have seen published. We
are determined to defend our rights and
uphold the principles of our holy religion,
including Plural Marriage.

				KANAB, March 18, 1879.

	The Quarterly Conference of the Relief
Societies of Kanab Stake of Zion was held
in the Kanab Schoolhouse, March 7, 1879,
Mrs. E. Nuttall presiding. The presidents
of the different wards were present, and
each gave a good report. Various subjects
were spoken of by the sisters, and we had
a very good meeting; the Spirit of the
Lord was in our midst.
	By the counsel of Pres. L. J. Nuttall, Sis-
ter Mary Judd, of this place, planted quite
a number of mulberry trees, and she has at
the present time fifteen ounces of cocoons

[Column 2]
and five ounces of eggs; we intend to do
what we can to start sericulture here.
	Wishing our valuable paper success in
every sense of the word,
		Your sister in the Gospel,
					M. E. LITTLE, Sec'y.

	Report of Young Ladies' Mutual Im-
provement Association from first organiza-
tion up to Dec. 10, 1878. The Y. L. M. I.
A. of Brigham City was organized July 30,
1875, by Miss E. R. Snow. Number of
members, 235. We generally had a good
attendance, and a lively interest was mani-
fested in the various exercises.
	March 13, 1878, a special meeting was
called by Miss E. R. Snow, when it was
csnsidered policy to dissolve and re-organ-
ize, having an association in each of the
four wards of Brigham City, and effecting,
at the same time, a Stake Organization.
There are eleven organizations, nine of
which reported at the Conference, showing
a membership of 420, and giving the fol-
lowing financial report of the Stake:

[Column 1]

	[For the EXPONENT.
TO THE LADIES OF THE ANTI-
POLYGAMY CRUSADE.

	There has been so much talk from press,
and pulpit,and from ladies opposite of prin-
ciples, that I thought I would say a few words
to them on the subject of Polygamy. Now
these ladies and the clergy profess to be
Christians, and I wonder that they oppose
that which is so clearly set forth in the
New Testament as this subject is. Chris-
tians signify that they are the followers of
Christ; if they are they cannot consistently
denounce Polygamy. "And if ye be
Christ's, then are ye Abraham's seed and
heirs, according to the promise. (Galatians,
iii, 29.) The promise to Abraham was that
he should have a numerous posterity. In
the 12th chap. of Genesis, 2 verse it reads:
"And I will make of thee a great nation,
and I will bless thee and make thy name
great, and thou shalt be a blessing. And I
will bless them that bless thee and curse
him that curseth thee; and in thee shall all
families of the earth be blessed."
	22d chap., 17th and 18th verses, the Lord
says: "In blessing I will bless thee, and in
multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the
stars of the heavens and as the sand which
is upon the sea shore, and thy seed shall
possess the gate of his enemies; and in thy
seed shall all the nations of the earth be
blessed, because thou hast obeyed my
voice." The same promise is given to Isaac,
and also to Jacob. We find no promise of
this kind made to any one but Abraham and
his seed. Now to Abraham and his seed
were the promises made; He saith not to
seeds, as of many, but as one. "And to
thy seed, which is Christ." (Gal., iii, 16.)
27th verse—"For as many of you as have
been baptized into Christ, have put on
Christ." 29th verse—"And if ye be Christ's
then are ye Abraham's seed, and heirs ac-
cording to the promise." Then you see
you are adopted (through being baptized
into Christ) right into the family of a po-

[Column 2]

lygamist; and Jesus Christ himself honored
the family of that polygamist, Abraham.
Paul says: "For verily He took not on Him
the nature of an angels, but He took on Him
the seed of Abraham." (Hebrews, ii, 16.)
Abraham is called the father of the faithful
and the friend of God; those appellations
themselves are treasures.
	Seeing that Jesus took not on Him the
nature of angel, but the seed of Abraham,
we find Him teaching repentance and bap-
tism, which ordinance adopts them into
the family of Abraham, for as many as are
baptized into Christ have put on Christ, and
if ye be Christ's, then are ye Abraham's
seed, &c. "Know ye, therefore, that they
which are of faith, the same are the chil-
dren of Abraham." "And the Scripture,
foreseeing that God would justify the heath-
en through faith, preached before the
Gospel unto Abraham, saying: In thee
shall all nations of the earth be blessed. So
they which be of faith are blessed with
faithful Abraham." (Gal., iii, 7, 8, 9.) "That
the blessings of Abraham might come upon
the Gentiles, through Jesus Christ." (14th
verse.) Now, if we are the followers of
Christ we cannot discard our polygamic fa-
thers. Isaiah says, 14th chap., 8 v., "But
thou, Israel, art my servant Jacob, whom I
have chosen, the seed of Abraham my
friend. If we have no connection with
these polygamous families, we must indeed
be outcasts, for there is no promise made to
any one else. Now, I would ask those LA-
DIES who oppose us if they are willing to
fight against this principle, seeing that in
doing so they fight against Christ? Con-
sider well before you decide; do not be led
to persecute those who are honestly and
truly fulfilling the command of God, and
have been adopted into the family of Abra-
ham, who, God says, "obeyed my voice and
kept my charge, my commandments, my
statutes and my laws." (Gen., xxvi, 5.)
There is a great deal more might be told
from the New Testament than has been
used in these few remarks. How is it pos-
sible that men who profess to preach the
Gospel are so blind that they cannot see
that Jesus and his Apostles sanctioned po
lygamy.
		KANAB.
	March 17, 1879.


PROVOKING CHIROGRAPHY.— Professor
S——, whose loss is deeply lamented in the
scholastic circles of New York, was at one
time a highly valued contributor to the
journal of which he afterward took charge,
and being one day introduced to its editor,
was greeted with every expression of cor-
diality and respect. It was a great pleasure
to meet one whose learning and services
had been, etc., etc. "But, professor," add-
ed the editor, turning upon him and seiz-
ing his hand with sudden earnestness, and
with solemnity in his face, "I hope you
pray for my printers!"
	The professor replied that he was very
happy to offer his prayers in behalf of any
who were in need of them; but what was
the special urgency in this case? "Ah!"
answered the editor, shaking his head im-
pressively, "if you could but hear them
swear when they get to work on your man-
uscript!"—Harper's Magazine for January.


A LADY in Hungary owns the distaff
used by Marie Antoinette during her im-
prisonment. It is of wood and ivory inlaid
with silver, and still is wound with the
flax left upon it by the ill-fated queen on
the eve of her execution.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


THE VOICES.

AFFECTIONATELY INSCRIBED TO AUNT EM.

FIRST.—
Gone from my heart is the sunlight of gladness,
	Gone from my soul is the music of your,
For mine eyes are oft dim with warm dews of sadness,
	And I sigh for the presence of one gone before.

In the springtime and spring of her life she was taken,
	When the pitying tears of young April wer shed
O'er the slumbering germlets of May, to awaken
	The sentinel flowers to vigil the dead.

And the daintiest blossom of God's lower Eden—
	Fair centre and setting of purity's wreath—
When the spring heralds welcomed the life-giving season,
	Was slain by the frosts of the winter of death.

Ah, many to spare were our hearts better willing,
	Than the soul in whose beauty all others were blest,
But "Death loves a bright mark" and ever is filling
	His graves with the spirits of those we love best.

SECOND:—
Their spirits? Ah no, for the Gospel of heaven
	A promise of glorious destiny gives—
Time's comforing balm and eternity's leaven—
	Which tells thee, sad mourner, thy dear one still lives,

'Tis only the clay that now slumbers beneath us,
	For death o'er the spirit no sceptre can sway;
And both shall unite yet again to bequeath us
	The joy of re-union at some future day.

For the mortal but rests on a motherly pillow
	While its spirit mate shines like a jewel on high,
E'en as glimmering stars in the depths of the billow
	Have their twin fires sparkling aloft in the sky.

To the future then, heart, turn for ne'er ending gladness,
	And music lorn soul for the sweetness of yore,
Where the warm light of hope drys the fountains of sad-
		ness,
	And life is all spring and the spring evermore.
									ANONYMOUS.
	S. L City, Sept. 10, 1878.


[Column 1]

	[For the EXPONENT.
WORK FOR WOMEN.

	Without discussing the vexed question
of "what is woman's work and what is her
sphere," we will assume that it is her duty
and privilege to do whatever she can that
will promote the advancement and eleva-
tion of her own sex. We particularly be-
lieve her life work is most specially needed
and appropriate among women and chil-
dren. It is not within the capacity of our
minds to become familiar with every sci-
ence which may be of service in the course
of a lifetime, so that it becomes necessary
that some shall give particular atteniton
to one branch and some to another, each
constituting a factor of the whole. As the
preservation of life and health is of so much
importance to us all, a general knowledge
of our own organization cannot fail to be a
source of satisfaction as well as an actual
benefit to every one. If medical knowledge
to a certain degree was a part of every
daughter's education, she would be far bet-
ter fitted to fulfill her future destiny as
wife and mother, and she would know
more about the infant on her bosom than
that it is a little form of throbbing clay and
that she loves it; and there might be fewer
little mounds in the "City of the Dead."
It is only the few comparatively whose
taste it is, to make a life study of the hu-
man system, and the diseases and acci-
dents peculiar thereto; but with intelli-
gence, reason, integrity of heart and the
pure motive to do good with their might
wherever they go, this factor of the com-
munity is as invaluable and necessary as
any other. It requires long and close ap-
plication to the standard text works of this
branch of science to lay the necessary
ground-work foundation upon which to
build future observations and experience.
	It is neither safe not right for any one
[Column 2]

with a smattering knowledge picked up
promiscously to undertake the practice of
medicine, and go forth to hold the balance
of life and death in their unskillful hands,
too often unnecessarily resulting in the des-
olation of hearts and homes. I do not by
any means depreciate sound practical ex-
perience, for it is of great value and in
newly settled countries it is often an im-
perative duty for those having the nerve
and natural ability, to reach forth and as-
sist suffering humanity; but whoever finds
it her lot to fill such a responsible and im-
portant position in a community, should
feel it an incumbent duty to thoroughly
prepare herself at her earliest opportunity
for such an important work. Some may
say if we live right we will need no doc-
tors which is theoretically true, but where
is there one who can live in this fallen es-
tate without hygienically or physically
making a mistake or mishap sometime.
Nature herself often makes most grevious
errors entirely incapable of spontaneous or
natural rectification requiring the most
cultivated skill to meet the emergency.
	Our reason, the greatest gift of God to
man was given us for cultivation and our
life here on earth presents a series of oppor-
tunities of transforming circumstances into
eternal knowledge. Progress is the key-
stone of heavenly thought and plan, and for
an individual to live and die without the
world being the better for it, comes short
of the object of their existence. True knowl-
edge no matter of what science or art is
given man of God, and every acquisition
which advances us one step should only
broaden our reason and strengthen our be-
lief (which is simply physical knowledge.)
in the existence of God, and of our unbound-
ed obligations to him. True medical knowl-
edge can never corrode the soul or unfit us
for usefulness in any way. It is said in Holy
Writ that "faith without works is dead,"
and it is a matter of correct observation
that good intelligent common sense-work
is very frequently a most excellent subor-
dinate to faith.
	In every growing community there seems
to soon develop the need of a hospital de-
voted more especially to the interest of wo-
men and children, and this is now being
felt among us. It would be a place where
those, especially from a distance, needing
surgical or special medical attention could
come and have every care from physcians
and nurses. Such an Institution would
also afford most excellent opportunities for
a class of students to gain a partial knowl-
edge of medicine. (A college being requir-
ed for a thorough education) and also there
would be facilities for establishing a train-
ing school for nurses, than which there is
no greater need at the present time. Could
not such an institution be organized and
carried on under the auspices of the Latter-
day Saint ladies of Utah? It may be said
"it will require so much money"—It will
certainly require a considerable sum, but
an enterprise with such far reaching benefits
and one in which all can have individual
interests upon the co-operative plan,certain-
ly can, by the united effort of so large a
community as ours, combine the numerous
small efforts into one efficient whole. To
accomplish this it only needs that a com-
pany of active managers take the matter
in hand, and lay it before the people. It
is to be hoped that every Latter-day Saint
woman in Utah and adjacent settlements
will think on this subject, and use her in-
fluence in its behalf, that at no distant day
the result may prove a living success, and
inasmuch as the interests of the Latter-
day Saint men and women are ever mu-

[Column 3]

tual, it is also hoped that the brethren will
extend their influence and material aid in
promoting this much-needed and laudable
enterprise. In the meantime our only al-
ternative is for classes to form, and be taught
according to our best circumstances until
better facilities can be furnished.
			ROMANIA B. Pratt, M. D.


PREAMBLE AND RESOLUTIONS.

OF THE LADIES OF HYRUM.

	Whereas, We the women of the Church
of Jesus Chrsst of Latter-day Saints have
been misrepresented to the nations by
Christian Missionary Ladies dwelling in
our midst, in regard to our most holy re-
ligion. We do hereby declare most solemn-
ly our true sentiments.

	Resolved; that we the women of Hyrum
members of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints earnestly endorse the
sentiments of the ladies of Salt Lake, St.
George and other places that have reported
through the EXPONENT and with them
claim the privilege to practice the princi-
ples of our holy religion, and God being
our helper we will sustain and co-operate
with our husbands, fathers and sons in
obeying every principle of the gospel we
have embraced.

	Resolved; that we solemnly avow our
full faith in the doctrine of Celestial Mar-
riage established by the command of our-
Heavenly Father and knowing his promis-
es to the faithful, we endorse it as one of
the most important principles of our holy
religion and claim the right to put it in
practice.

	Resolved; that we will obey the com-
mands of God and leave the result with
him. That we protest against the enact-
ment of any law that deprives American
citizens, whether male or female of any con-
stitutional rights.

	Resolved; that we take this opportunity
to tender our thanks to the ladies of the
National Woman's Suffrage Association,
who have bravely defended our cause in
the halls of Congress, and that we appre-
ciate the labor of our own lady delegates
who have braved the world in defence of
our religion.

	Resolved; that we respectfully suggest
that those ladies opposed to our institutions
keep their sympathy for the poor unfortu-
nate outcast women and fatherless children
in the cities of the world, as we by the
help of God can take care of ourselves, and
don't need their assistance. We wish to
say that although we are late we are never-
theless in earnest and wish to have a
voice with the rest of our sisters.

		C. Liljenquist President,
		N. M. Neilsen,	} Secretaries.
		E. Wight,		}


MRS LYNN LINTON is engaged on a new
book that is shortly to be published. The
subject is the influence of clergy on women,
and the story is a fight between a husband
and a ritualistic cleagyman for influence
over the wife of the former.—EX.


A NEW paper edited by a woman is issued
at Louisville Kentucky by Mrs. H.E. Housh;
it contains reports of the work of women and
supports the idea that work is as honorable
for women as for men. Her son a young
man of seventeen years does her printing.
It is very neatly executed on fine paper.
