SALT LAKE CITY, MAY 1, 1879.


[Column 1]
WOMAN AGAINST WOMAN.

	"He that would live clear of envy must lay
his finger on his mouth, and keep his hand out
of the ink-pot."—LE ESTRANGE.

	This was applied to men in that day, but it
will certainly apply to women equally as well.
This is woman's era, and of course she will be
criticized, censured, sneered at, even by her
own sex. After all it is expected--women pos-
sessing more humility than men, more delicacy
of nature and true refinement of soul, more
spirituality in fact, will scarcely stoop to perse-
cution. Yet even this we find to be the case;
cruel as the grave. Pope said,

		"The world with calumny abounds,
		The whitest virtue slander wounds."

	It is well that persons of greatness of soul
possess the peculiar faculty, or tact, to judge of
those with whom they come in contact, other-
wise they might be prejudiced against the in-
nocent and good by evil-designing people.
	It seems to have been peculiarly the fate of
Mormons to be slandered, but until recently it
was confined to men mostly, Mormon women
being considered too low, degraded and igno-
rant, otherwise they certainly would not live
in plural marriage.
	Now a change has come. The franchise has
been given to Mormon women, they have been
heard from, and as they will not give up the
principles of their religion, which they know 
to be elevating and pure, they must needs be
scandalized. And because a few noble-souled
ladies who are working for universal good, as
we understand it, have dared to allow Mormon
women to come into their councils, and sit on
a public platform with them, a great many ex-
planations must be made for them, lest the
National Woman Suffrage Association should
be understood as endorsing Polygamy.
	After the women of Utah have held the
ballot ten years, and with the best possible re-
sults, they are to have no representation. "Tell
it not in Gath, publish it not in the streets of
Askelon," that Mormon women vote, or have
any education, or culture. Oh, no, don't
come near a Mormon woman, she's a plural
wife, or at any rate believes in it, and "Polyga-
my degrades womanhood." "Where Polyga-
my exists all women are essentially degraded."
Well, we are very sorry for these "wise wo-
men of Gotham," who are at sea in a bowl
without any ballast—who as[-]ert that Polyga-
my is not better than prostitution, but infin-
itely worse in its effects upon society, how-
ever it may be in individual cases. They
pity Mormon women fanatics just as they do
the Hindoo women. Well, there's every reason
to pity them, for in their midst all sorts of
abominations abound, and they have no reme-
dy. The cries of the women who have been
cruelly and wantonly betrayed and abandoned,

[Column 2]
with the blood of their murdered offspring,
has pierced the throne of the Eternal, and ere
long He will avenge their wrongs.
	We affirm just as strongly as our opponent
that instead of degrading, plural marriage ele-
vates woman; we and hundreds of others have
proven it by practical experience. Mother-
hood exalts woman; it is her crowning glory;
and in no other marriage covenant is there
such perfect freedom for woman to pursue any
pursuit or avocation she may choose, as where
there are more wives. To be sure, if she de-
pends entirely upon her husband to direct her
every thought and action, she would be un-
happy, but it gives women the highest oppor-
tunities for self-development, exercise of judg-
ment, and arouses latent faculties, making
them more truly cultivated in the actual reali-
ties of life, more independent in thought and 
mind, noble and unselfish. The women of
Utah have the ballot still, and intend to make
good use of it, and educate the rising gen-
eration and those who come from foreign
lands every year to a higher and nobler stand-
ard of thought, feeling and action than to per-
secute any people because of their religious
faith. In Zion will the standard of welcome
be unfurled to all nations, and women as well
as men will help to raise this standard, for the
work is one of mutual benefit to both, and 
many honest and true souls will come gladly;
and the Saints can afford to wait until the 
Lord's time to redeem Zion, working and watch-
ing the while, to do good to all, and "especially
those of the household of faith." [handwritten] E. B. Well


HOME AFFAIRS.

	DR. ROMANIA B. Pratt will open a class in
Obstetrics and the training of nurses, on Mon-
day, 5th inst., to continue Mondays and Thurs-
days for six months, at her office in the Old
Constitution Buildings.
	DURING THE last month Pres. E. R. Snow
has organized Primary Associations in the 1st,
2d, 12th, 13th and 20th wards of this city. Also
re-organized the Young Ladies' M. I. A. of the
2d Ward. 
	AT THE Territorial Fair which will be held
October next, it is expected there will be
great many prizes distributed to those who pro-
duce the best articles of home manufacture.
Ladies, if you wish to work for this, do your
best in useful, ornamental, or elegant work;
with all kinds of materials used in such articles.

	AT THE General Meeting of the sisters in
the Fourteenth Ward, Saturday, April 26, the
officers of the Silk Association of Salt Lake
Co. were sustained in their positions, and Bro.
Paul A. Schettler was elected as a committee
to judge of the quality of cocoons. The officers
of Relief Society agreed to act as committees
to see that all the mulberry leaves in their vi-
cinity were utilized to the best advantage.
	THE THEATRE has been rendered attractive
of late by the H. M. S. Pinafore, which has
been performed several times, the performance
being entir[-]ly composed of home talent, di-
rected by Prof. Careless. He certainly deserves
all the e[-]comiums he has received. Mrs.
Sigel, Miss White, Miss Olsen, Miss Sanders,
and several of the gentlemen also played and 
sang remarkable well Although the opera
was well performed in every part, so every-
body says; "and what everybody says must be
true.
	"A MESSENGER from Jarvis Section" was
quite sensational, but very funny. "Uncle
Daniel" was really a true Yankee in style and
in tone, and little "Clip" acted her part per-
fectly.


WE HAD the honor of attending a pleasant
entertainment on Thursday last, at the resi-
dence of Mrs. Mary Emma Van Schooven.
The hostess had prepared a delicious repast for
her guests, the occasion being the close of the
term of a class in Obstetrics taught by Dr. Ro-
mania B. Pratt, a young and rising physician,
a favorite with her students it would seem,
who all united in presenting her with an ele-
gant pencil and cuff-pins of gold and jet. Dr.
Pratt, though taken by surprise, replied grace-
fully, expressing her good feelings for all the
class, and her ardent desire to aid them and to
honor the profession in which she had enlisted
	IN COMPANY with Pres. E. R. Snow we at-
tended the Annual meeting of the Relief So-
ciety of Davis Co., in Farmington, April 17.
The reports from branch societies were very
satisfactory, and everything was conducted in
an orderly manner, Mrs. S. I. Holmes presid-
ing. Elder Anson Call was present and made
some remarks so favorable to the woman ques-
tion that we shall take pains to publish them
with a full account of the meeting. The Presi-
dents of societies made excellent verbal reports.
Pres. E. R. Snow assisted Coun. Call and the
presidency of Relief Society in organizing a
branch Silk Association in Davis Co., Mrs. Do-
rinda Robinson, President; Nancy A. Clark
and Jane Smith, Counselors. A large majority
of the sisters enrolled their names as active
workers.


EDITORIAL NOTES.

	WE SHALL be compelled to confine our col-
umns in future exclusively to Stake reports of
R. S and Y. L. M. I. A., except where the R.
S. Stakes have not been organized. These will
report as before.

	WE HAVE R. S. Reports from Samaria, Ma-
lad, Franklin, Hebron, Minersville, Kingston,
Lewiston and Ephraim; Primary Association
reports from Santaquin, 16th Ward S. L. City.
Letters from Santa Clara, St. George, Goshen
and other places, which we intend publishing.

	MASS MEETINGS have been held by the sis-
ters in nearly all the settlements great and
small in Utah Territory, to protest against the
Adti-Polygamy petitions, letters to Mrs.
Hayes, &c., and to defend one of the vital
principles of the Gospel, viz., Celestial Mar-
riage. It is impossible to publish them all. It
is specially gratifying to know that there is a
united feeling on this subject throughout the
entire Church. At Hyrum, Woodruff, Clark-
ston, Wallsburg, Midway, Tooele, these meet-
ings have all been spirited and interesting, and
the sisters in their respective localities have
either adopted the resolutions passed in Salt
Lake City or have drawn up resolutions corres-
ponding in every respect with the spirit and
sentiment therein expressed.

	WE PUBLISH in this issue an item on the
celebration of Mrs. M. A. Smoot's Surprise
Party on her birthday, and we would not wish
our dear friends, sisters and patrons to think
we were unmindful of any good sister's "Birth-
day Party," so will inform them we have in
our desk many such tributes, which we would
gladly publish,—Mrs. Louisa B Pratt's of
Beaver, Mrs. Sarah M. Peterson's of Ephraim,
Mrs. Martha J. Thomas' of Lehi. We will
mention in connection with this fact, we did
not give our readers any account of the party
held at our own house in honor of Sister E. A.
Whitney, well known to nearly all the Saints
from the days of Kirtland, nor to that of Mrs.
Zina D. Young, held at the residence of Mrs.
B. W. Smith, nor of Mrs. M. I. Horne, Presi-


dent of R. S. S. L. Stake of Zion, nor of Pres.
Rachel R. Grant or Elizabeth H. Goddard, and
many other good sisters, simply because it is
utterly impossible to mention all these things
unless we had a daily paper.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		235


A HUMBLE TRIBUTE,

TO THE MEMORY OF MRS. MARINDIA J.
CARLETON.

	Mrs. Marindia J. Carleton, wife of Mr. Geo.
H. Carleton, cashier of the Georgetown Na-
tional Bank, departed this life March 15, 1879,
at her residence in Georgetown, Mass. The
deceased was the beloved niece of Sister Vienna
Jacques of this city, and Mrs. Boynton of
Bountiful is the sister of Mrs. Carleton. Hav-
ing made her acquaintance, with that of her
worthy husband, on my recent mission to my
native State, Mass., and becoming conversant
with her blameless and holy life, I could not
do justice to my feelings did I not testify in
some public manner to her worth as a consis-
tent and devoted Latter-day Saint. Truly, in
her daily deportment as a wife, daughter, sis-
ter and frien[-], all the virtues and beatitudes
were exemp[--]led and shone pre-eminently.
	Moving in a circle of society that was inimi-
cal to her religious principles, she never faltered
or hesitated to declare her professed faith in
the restoration of the Gospel, when it was ex-
pedient and wise so to do. Nor in words only
did she manifest a vital interest in the great
latter-day work, but in her generosity freely
contributed to the varied interests of the king-
dom of God. To the Temple fund alone she
donated ten dollars monthly, preferring to lay
up treasures in heaven, rather than to expend
the means in undue personal adornment. The
Elders who were laboring in that portion of
the Lord's vineyard always received a hearty
welcome, and never departed without receiv-
ing something substantial to help them in
their mission. She ardently desired to gather
to Zion, and in her last letter to her Aunt Vi-
enna, dated Feb. 22, she expressed herself as
being strongly impressed of the nearness of the
great calamities that await the nation, and
hoped that she would be gathered out of Baby-
lon before the time came that "He that would
not take up his sword to fight against his
neighbor must needs fly to Zion for safety."
Truly has the Lord garnered the pure spirit of
Sister "Rinnie" as a choice jewel to the Zion
above, to mingle with the Saints of all ages
and there enjoy sweet rest until the time shall
arrive when Zion below shall be perfected and
prepared for the coming of the Lord.
	To the bereaved and loving husband, all who
have had the honor of his acquaintance will
sincerely condole in the great loss he has sus-
tained; and may he be soothed by the cher-
ished memory of the many virtues and beauti-
ful life of his deceased companion, and may
the recollection of his untiring devotedness for
her happiness be as a healing balm to assuage
the poignant grief of the present, and endeavor
to look forward to a happy reunion in the fu-
ture, in that peaceful realm where sorrow and
parting shall be known no more, is the sincere
desire of his well-wisher,
						GEORGE HAMLIN.
	Salt Lake City, April 13, 1879
	We have read the tribute dedicated to Mrs
Carleton in the "G[-]orgetown Advocate," and
will here quote one paragraph, attributed to
"a lady who had known her from childhood."
	"She was the b[-]st woman I ever knew. Gen-
tle, quiet, charitable, conscientious, generous
and kind. I would not exchange a single trait
in her character if I could." What a glorious
epitaph!

	CORRESPONDENCE.

		GOSHEN, APRIL 3, 1879.
TO MRS. EMMELINE B. WELLS,
	Dear Sister,—I pen these few lines to ac-
quaint you with a sad accident which befell
two young men "brothers" Lorenzo and
Thomas Wilson of this place on the 1st, inst.
caused by a cave while working in an iron
mine in Tintic; which instantly took the life
of Thomas the younger aged 21 years, and
dislocated the hip of the other and otherwise
badly hurt him; hopes are entertained of his
recovery. He has a wife and two small chil-
dren. The funeral took place yesterday, when
great respect was shown. Several young peo-
ple preceded the corpse (walking in couples)
which four young men carried to the school
house, where appropriate and consoling re-
marks were made. Their mother Mrs. Mary
Dodds, who probably you recollect, is Coun-
selor in the R. S. here. She has experienced
similar distress before, but is a person full of
integrity and faith, and places her trust in
Jehovah upon whom she relies in her great
trouble.
	We held the first meeting in our new Relief
Society House on the 28, ult. Bishop Wm.
Price met with us and dedicated the house.
We are thankful to have it so far finished, and
hope yourself Sister E. R. Snow and other
sisters will favor us with a visit, for your good
teachings still abide in our memories and help
to stimulate us to diligence.
	Ever praying God to abundantly bless you,
is the desire of your sister in the Gospel of
Christ.
		MARY ANN TILL

EDITOR EXPONENT:
	When I read in the papers of the noble
stand taken by my sisters in their several
Mass Meetings throughout Utah, in defence
of the glorious principles of their religion; I
felt desirious of raising my voice with the rest
of my sisters in solemn protest against any
interference with our religious rights.
	We have no organization of Relief Society
as yet, so I thought I would pen a few of my
thoughts upon paper. I feel to say, thank
God that we have such noble advocates who
dare stand forth in the face of the whole world,
as it were, and proclaim that they were the
wives and daughters of good and great men
who practised patriarchal marriage, and pro-
test against any interference with their sacred
religious rights.
	Our enemies may scoff and sneer as much as
they please, at the idea of polygamy being
part of our religion, in their corrupt hearts
they cannot see any thing pure and noble in
this principle. They judge us by themselves,
which is not always a righteous judgement.
And the noble self sacrificing sisters who went
to Washington to plead the cause of the Saints
of God; I feel that they deserve the lasting
gratitude of their sisters in Utah, whether
their appeal has any effect or not, it does not
make any material difference to us, let the
result be with God. As the Lord has said
through his prophet Joseph Smith, "Let my
Saints appeal to the highest court in the land,
and if they will not do them justice, then let
them appeal to their Father in heaven, and
He will hear them and come out with judg-
ments upon those unworthy servants who
have the power to do the Saints j[-]stice but
will not. They will fi[-]d when it is too late
what it is to measure [-]rms with the Almighty
God of heaven. We know this principle is of
God, and we know if we are firm in the cause
of truth, and the principles we have espoused,

[Column 2]
we will come out right, we can have no doubt
as to the result. We may in the future,(as we
have done in the past) have to pass through
trials and persecutions, to try us and prove
our faith. What is our religion worth, if we
are not willing to live for it, and die for it too,
if need be.
	Let us live up to the principle of plural mar-
riage, and teach it to our daughters as pure
and holy, revealed by the Almighty, to per-
petuate a noble race of people upon the earth,
that the noble intelligent spirits, that have
been held in reserve, in the eternal worlds, to
come forth in these last days to take part in
the great Latter-day work, and to prepare a
people for the coming of the Savior. What a
glorious privilege to come forth and receive
parentage smong the Latter-day Saints, and
what a great responsibility is resting on the
parents to bring this noble r[-]ce up, in the
principles of righteousness and truth, and set
an example worthy of imitation. How many
of us is there that realize this in the light we
should. Let each one answer for themselves.
	May the Lord of heaven bless you with
health and strength, to enable you to carry on
the work that you are so worthily engaged in,
that your valuable paper may continue to be,
as it ever has been, a true EXPONENT of the
feelings and views of the women of Utah. I
remain as ever,
	Your sister in the Gospel.
		SARAH M. POMEROY.
MESA CITY, ARIZONA, FEB. 14, 1879. 

		PAROWAN, Feb. 12, 1879.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	We feel it our duty to let our voices be heard
in these eventful times, when Satan is labor-
ing incessantly to destroy the kingdom of God,
for he understands that his reign is short, and
that the coming of the son of man draweth
nigh. Our enemies do not understand that it
is the God of heaven they are fighting against,
and if an angel from heaven should declare it
unto them, they would think some evil spirit
had possession of their senses. How true the
saying that  :darkness covers the earth and
gross darkness the minds of the people". We
wish to add our testimony to the thousands
who have already published the same, that
this is the work of the Lord, and that Joseph
Smith was a true Prophet, and that this People
are carrying out the work he began. Plural
Marriage is a part of our religion, and we will
defend it and every other principle of our faith
with our latest breath. We are willing to
obey the true laws of the land. We love the
name of Washington, and the names of the
framers of the Declaration of Independence,
and every true born son or daughter of Ameri-
ca loves Freedom and Liberty.
	Our R. S. met on the 1st Thursday of the
present month, and one sister read the inter-
view between Pres. John Taylor and Mr. Hol-
lister, after which as many of the sisters as
time would admit of, spoke and bore strong
testimonies of this work; of Celestial Marriage,
and of the true and noble spirit manifested
throughout the interview by our beloved Pres-
ident. And we pray that his days may be
lengthened out upon the earth, that be may
accomplish all the good his heart desires.
	We hold in high esteem our sisters who are
filled with the spirit of our religion, who have
so ably represented us in the Capitol of our
Nation. We pray that they may be blessed and
prospered in all they undertake in righteous-
ness, and also that God will bless all of his
faithful hand-maidens who are striving to
elevate and bless mankind, and may we all
fully appreciate our families and homes is the
earnest desire of your sisters,
		EMMA S. SMITH, Pres. R. S.
		MARY J. WEST, Coun.


236		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
A GEM FOR EVERY MONTH.

	We have been reading recently in "The
American Home," some articles which were
very interesting on the subject which forms
the heading to this chapter, from which we
select the poems for each month. "The
American home is a pleasing weekly jour-
nal published in Chicago, and contains many
items of special interest, letters from
Washington, and articles on floriculture, in
fact a great variety of literary matter that
must certainly benefit those who read it
with care.

JANUARY.

By her who in this month is born
No gems but Garnets should be worn;
They will insure her constancy,
True friendship and fidelity.

	This is a mineral combination of silicates
of alumina, lime, iron and manganese, and
is colored in variety according to the differ-
ent proportions of the ingredients.

FEBRUARY.

The February born will find
Sincerity and and peace of mind,
Freedom from passion and from care,
If they say Amethyst will wear.

The Amethyst is a sub-species of quartz,
of a bluish violet color. The name is from
the Greek.

MARCH.

Who on this earth of ours their eyes
In March first open shall be wise;
In days of peril firm and brave,
And wear a Bloodstone to their grave.

April.

She who from April dates her years
Diamonds should wear, lest bitter tears,
For vain repentance flow; this stone
Emblem of innocence is known.

MAY.

Who first beholds the light of day
In Spring's sweet flowery month of May,
And wears an emerald all her life,
Shall be a loved and happy wife.

Emeralds are found in Grenada and Siberia.
It is a silicate of alumina and glucina, col-
ored with oxyd of chromium. Its color is
a decided rich green.
	Queen Victoria's state crown contains
3,093 precious stones, among which are
eleven Emeralds, 1,363 brilliant diamonds,
and 1,273 rose diamonds.

JUNE.

Who comes with summer to this earth,
And owes to June her day of birth,
With ring of Agate on her hand
Can health, wealth and long life command.

JULY.

The glowing Ruby should adorn
Those who in warm July are born;
Then will they be exempt and free
From love's doubts and anxiety.

	The glowing Ruby serves its patrons born
in July, and so rare is the real ruby it is
considered more precious than the diamond.

AUGUST.

The August born should
Wear a Sardonyx, or for thee
No conjugal felicity.
The August born without this stone,
'Tis said, must live unloved and lone.

This is a reddish yellow stone, nearly allied
to onyx, which is commonly a light brown
and white. This is the mineral used for
Cameos, the figure being cut in one layer,
the other layer funishing the back-ground.

SEPTEMBER.

A maiden born when autumn leaves
Are rustling in September's breeze

[Column 2]
A Sapphire on her brow should bind,
'Twill cure diseases of the mind.

The oxyd alumina comes in the form of
emery. This is granular. It appears also
in crystalline forms, and when clear, in all
its beauty and rarity, is the gem known as
sapphire, which is of various colors, though
it is only the blue crystals that generally
receive this name; a red crystal of it is call-
ed the Oriental Ruby, and a yellow one the
Oriental Topaz.

OCTOBER.

October's child is born for woe,
And life's vicissitudes must know;
But lay an opal on her breast,
And hope will lull those woes to rest

Opal is a silicious mineral. The color of
the Noble Opal, so highly prized as a gem,
is commonly milky, and has a play of bril-
liant but delicate internal reflections. In
the Fire Opal the reflections are a fire red
in color. This stone is esteemed unlucky,
but the absurd superstition cannot be traced
farther back than Scott's novel of "Anne of
Gernstien," in which the Baroness of Her-
mione of Arnheim wears one.

NOVEMBER.

	November's wail finds its solace in the
thought that,

Who first comes to this world of woe
With drear November's fog and snow,
Should prize the Topaz amber hue—
Emblem of friends and lovers true.

DECEMBER.

If cold December gave you birth,
The month of snow, and ice, and mirth,
Place on your hand a Turquoise blue;
Success will bless whate'er you do.

The Turquoise is found in Persia, and the
Shah is supposed to have in his possession
all the first gem[-], as he allows only those of
inferior quality to leave the country. In
consequence large Turquoises of good qual-
ity bring great prices.
	The "Home," in writing up the gems, has
given them in Analectic Chapters, num-
bered. The writer says in looking over a 
huge package of old newspapers her eye fell
upon a poem entitled "A Gem for every
Month."
	The diamond is perhaps the most inter-
esting of all gem subjects to most readers.
"Some people believe that the time is not
far distant when some chemist will discover
the secret of making the Diamond. And
why not? Lapis-lazuli, an azure blue min-
eral, from which are manufactured costly
vases and other ornamental articles, once
worth its weight in gold, can now be manu-
factured by the ton." The arrangement of
the stones of the Diamond constitutes the
difference between it and pure charcoal. In
Amsterdam there are 10,000 Jews directly,
or indirectly, dependent upon Diamond-
cutting as a trade, and they are poorly paid.
The three forms in which Diamonds are cut
are the table, the rose, and the brilliant. The
brilliant is a double pyramid, or cone, cut
off at the top by a large plane, or table, and
at the bottom by a small one called the col-
let. The Romans, taught by the Indians,
valued the diamond entirely on account of
its supernatural virtues, and Pliny says of
it, it baffles poisons, keeps off insanity, and
dispels vain fears. The Italians asserted
that it maintained concord between hus-
band and wife.
	Doubtless, jewels are best beheld in situs,
the situs, however, being neither the mine
nor the matrix, but in their proper place—
about some fair person, which gives you a
chance of admiring two beautiful things at
one time."


MISS ANTHONY'S SPEECH AT THE

WASHINGTON CONVENTION.

BREAD AND THE BALLOT.

	Miss Susan B. Anthony took the stand,
and made an earnest and logical speech,
the subject being the relations between
"Bread and the ballot."
	Miss Anthony said w[-]man wants bread
not the ballot. It was usual with people as
long as they got all they wanted to be con-
tent. She cited the British workingman,
sketched his rise and progress from the
time when he never made a ripple on po-
litical thought down to the present, when
the franchise gives him the power behind
the throne. She said disfranchisement is
always political degradation to any class
affected by it—disfranchisement and degra-
dation being in politics synonymous terms.
The English mechanic and laborer who were
ground down and robbed by capitalists fifty
years ago mobbed their best friend, John
Bright, when he advocated the ballot for
them at a public meeting. They did not
want the ballot, they wanted bread.
	So woman may think she does not want
the ballot. Miss Anthony then proceeded
to show that parties and politicians show no
respect to any person or sex, color or condi-
tion, unless such person has power, power
to vote, power to control results, power to
influence elections, power to give success or
defeat to parties, to elect such persons to
office or to defeat them. She quoted Eng-
lishwomen voting at municipal elections,
and how the instinct of self preservation
makes party leaders court the laboring vote
here as there. Human nature is selfish. It
looks out for its own interests. It shows
favor to those upon whom it depends for
success. It is not magnanimity and gener-
osity that controls parties or men. Politics
and Republics are ungrateful. Self-interest
is the great incentive that yields homage to
the power of the workingman.
	The Grand old Democratic Party was the
party that early advocated the rights of the
workingman, of the poor man. Jefferson
opposed property qualification. Some per-
sons here perhaps are old enough to remem-
ber when a man could not vote unless he
was worth a certain amount of property.
Old Ben Franklin ridiculed this doctrine.
He said here is a man who owns a donkey
worth say $250. Because he owns this
property to-day he can vote. To-morrow
the donkey dies, and he can not vote. Was
it the man or the donkey who voted?
[Laughter.]
	Martin Van Buren advocated the right
of the poor man to vote. It was this posi-
tion toward the poor man that gave the
Democratic party its power. The word
Democrat meant opposed to aristocracy.
The Irishman, ere he left the green island
shore, was a Democrat, prepared to vote
the Democratic ticket because he was poor
and that party favored him, and ever since
that time the Democratic party has stood
ready to give him whatever he asked for,
because he had a vote and could give them
power and place and party success by his
vote.
	He wanted ten hours work for a day's
labor, and the Legislature gave it to him.
He wanted eight hours' labor to make a 
day's work and the Legislature gave it to
him, and so everything he demanded has
been at his service because he had a vote.
These votes gave the Democracy power un-
til they abandoned their record and showed
they had no real sympathy with the poor
man and the oppressed, by smiling and


[Column 1]
bending to the Irishman and consenting to
the degradation and oppression of the black
man.
	Then Democracy lost its prestige. Then
the Republican party, founded on the ruins
of the Whig party, stepped forward to
power, and the Republican party won suc-
cess by emancipating the black man, by be-
coming his friend, by giving him the ballot,
and this party, so long as it is true to its
professions, will have the vote of and the
affection and sympathy of the negro to
whom it gave liberty and the ballot.
	But it was all selfishness. The Republi-
can party did not love the negro. It was
his aid they were after, his ballot, his vote
and aid to give them victory and power.
Emancipation was not adopted as long as it
could be safely put off.
	Her description of the great change
which had taken place in the condition of the
colored race, and how much colored voters
are respected and loved by politicians since
they have the ballot power in their hands,
was vivid. She added that the wool of a 
negro is just as curly, the lip just as thick,
and the heel just as long as it ever was.
Yet these politicians see more beauty and
symmetry in them somehow—and how
would it be with the women voters? Why
these candidates for political honors would
no doubt, just before elections, imagine
they saw the wings just cropping out on 
them! [Applause.] The old masters of the 
negro now often address them thus: "Fel-
low-citizens, I thank you for the honor con-
ferred on me," etc. With what terms of
deference and endearment they will address
the female voters in the future is left for
that happy millennium to develop. [Ap-
plause.]
	She argued to show how necessary it is 
for the laboring men in this country to com-
bine together to command the respect of
politicians and capitalists. By this means
can labor maintain its dignity and self-
respect. And how essential for women to
participate in this power and means of self-
protection and preservation.
	Miss Anthony referred to the number of
women supporting themselves in this coun-
try: 300,000 engaged in farm work, half a 
million in domestic service, women at work
in factories and other employments, some
two millions outside of home life. For
these women she asked the ballot, to pro-
tect them in their wages. The women are
ready to join any political party that comes
to the front in behalf of woman suffrage.
Workmen who have the ballot must be con-
sulted. Miss Anthony resurrected dead
and gone conventions, sketched the Cali-
fornia mining strike and the Ohio railroad
strike to show the power the ballot gives
the laboring man. She gave the instance
of Gov. Hicks, of California, who aided the
capitalists to employ Chinamen in the mines.
He was defeated when next he ran and an-
other Governor elected.
	On the other hand Gov. Hayes waited for
official notice of mobs of laborers and work-
ing men—until the row was over—and lo!
he is President. Shrewd party men and
managers grant the position of working-
men, or make believe they do.
	The Grangers had revolutionized the 
western railroad system, reduced the fare,
and benefitted themselves. They had bal-
lots and used them to help themselves.
	The ignorant Irishman, the uneducated
foreigner, the benighted and almost bar-
barous negro have each been enfranchised.
They have been enabled to protect them-
selves by the ballot, but women, the moth-
ers, the wives, the sisters of our native-born
citizens, cultured, educated, the peer of

[Column 2]
their brothers, husbands and fathers, are
left to pay taxes without a voice to consent
or refuse, left to the grinding selfishness of
capitalists, the sport or the caprice or whim
of parties and legislatures who make laws
that control or oppress them and for whose
complaints and petitions they have no time
or inclination to listen. Women have no
votes. They can therefore make no im-
pression upon parties. Are women working
15 hours for a day's work in a factory? They
will ask in vain for a 10 or 8 hours law for
themselves. They have no vote to compel
the party to aid them. She quoted a strike
of women in a Troy, N. Y., collar factory,
who, after holding out for six months, were
at last obliged to go to work at less wages
than they had when they stopped work,
clearly demonstrating that the lack of the
ballot had caused this great injury.
	Justice demands that woman should have
the right to say if her property be taxed or 
not.
	Woman wants bread not the ballot, but
give her the ballot and she will vote her-
self an opportunity to get her bread.
	Give her the ballot and she will then hold
her share of public offices. Then some
delicate woman will sit at the office desk
while the yawning six footer will go out to
labor as he should at some work to develop
his manhood and strength. Women then
will sell papers of pins and yards of tape
and the tin whistles. Women will keep
the books. Women will then be in partner-
ship, will carry on the business. Wives of
laboring men and mechanics will then own
half the money that the husband earns.
	And not only will they do this, but when
women vote then whiskey will close its
reign, then will righteous laws be passed,
vice and crime be largely eliminated, taxes
reduced, honesty and economy obtain in
public affairs and justice be administered to
all. Miss Anthony gave some startling
facts proving that woman's work for tem-
perance, without the ballot, had in reality
been productive of injury to the cause of
temperance and had thrown increased
power in the hands of the liquor dealers.
She showed woman working without the 
ballot had thus killed the Prohibitory law
in Massachusetts, Local Option in Indiana,
drawn insults upon the 18,000 women of
Chicago who had opposed Sunday liquor
selling, and in Minnesota had created a for-
midable society of the liquor men, who in
one year expended a million of dollars to
defeat woman's work.
	She took a vote of the audience as to wo-
man's need of the ballot for the protection
of her wages, and in order to do effective
temperance work, receiving in reply a
hearty "aye."

[Column 1]
			CORRESPONDENCE.

	ST. CHARLES, IDAHO, APRIL 15, 1879.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	In reading Elder Woodruff's Epistle to
the saints, I was very much pleased with
the spirit that it breathed. "Do right let
the consequence follow" brought forcibly
to my mind the narrative of the Jewish
Mother and her sons, as stated in the Tal-
mud, of which the following is an extract.
"During the terrible times which followed
the fall of the Holy City, Hannah and her
seven sons were cast into prison.
	According to their ages they were brought
before the tyrant conqueror, and command-
ed to pay homage to him and his gods "God
forbid" exclaimed the eldest lad, "that I
should bow to thy image. Our command-
ments say to us, 'I am the Lord thy God' to

[Column 2]
no other will I bow." He was immediately
led out to execution, and the same demand
was made of his brother, the second son.
"My brother bowed not" he answered "and
no more will I." "Wherefore not?" asked
the tyrant. "Because" replied the lad, "the
second commandment of the Decalogue tells
us, 'thou shalt have no other God but me."
His death followed immediately his brave
words.
	"My religion teaches me 'Thou shalt wor-
ship no other God" (Ex, xxxiv: 14) said the
third son, "and I welcome the fate accord-
ed to my brothers rather than bow to thee
or thy images."
	The same homage was demanded of the
fourth son, but brave and faithful as his
brothers, he replied, "He that sacrificeth
unto any God save unto the Lord only (Ex.
xxii: 20) and was slain pitilessly. "Hear
O Israel! the Lord our God, the Lord is
One!" exclaimed the fifth lad, yielding up
his young life with the watch-word of
Israel's hosts.
	"Why art thou so obstinate" was asked
of the sixth brother, when he too, was
brought before the tyrant and scorned the
propositions made him.
	"The Lord thy God is in the midst of
thee, a mighty and a terrible God" (Deut.
vii: 21) he said; and died for the principles
he proclaimed.
	Then the seventh and youngest boy was
brought before the murderer of his rela-
tives, who addressed him kindly, saying,
"my son, come bow before my gods." And
the child answered. "God forbid! Our holy
religion teaches us 'Know therefore this
day, and reflect in thy heart that the Lord
he is God, in the heavens above and on the
earth beneath, there is none else;' (Deut. iv:
39.) Never will we exchange our God for
any other, neither will he exchange us for
any other nation, for as it is written, Thou
hast this day acknowledged the Lord, and
the Lord hath acknowledged thee this day,
that thou art a peculiar people!"
	Still the tyrant spoke smoothly and with
kind words.
	"Thou art young" he said; "thou hast
seen but little of the pleasures of this life,
not as much as has fallen to the portion of
thy brethren. Do as I wish thee and thy
future shall be bright and happy,"
	"The Lord will reign for ever and ever,"
said the lad; "thy nation and thy kingdom
will be destroyed; thou art here to-day, to-
morrow in the grave; to-day elevated to-
morrow lowly; but the Most High One en-
dures for ever."
	"See" continued the other, "thy brothers
lie slain before thee; their fate will be
thine if thou refuseth to do as I desire;
see I will cast my ring to the ground, stoop
thou and pick it up: that I will consider
allegiance to my gods-"
	"Thinkest thou that I fear thy threats!"
returned the unterrified lad; "why should
I fear a human being more than the great
God the King of kings?"
	"Where and what is thy God?" asked
the oppressor; "is there a God in the world?
"Can there be a world without a creator?"
replied the youth. "Of thy gods 'tis said,
mouths they have but speak not: of our
God the psalmist says 'By the word of the
Lord were the heavens made.' Thy gods
have eyes but they see not; but the eyes of
the Lord run to and fro in the whole earth.
Thy gods have ears, but they hear not; but
of our God 'tis written, 'The Lord heark-
ened and heard; of thy god 'tis said s nose
they have but smell not; while our God
smelled the sweet savor; hands have thy
gods but they touch not; while our God
says, 'My hand hat[-] also founded the


earth,' of thy gods 'tis written feet they
have but walk not. while Zachariah tells us
of our God, 'His feet will stand that day
upon the Mount of Olives."
	"Then" said the cruel one; "if thy God
hath all these attributes why does he not
deliver thee from my power."
	The lad replied; "He delivered Chanan-
yah and his companions from the power of
Nebuchadnezzar, but they were righteous
men, and Nebuchadnezzar was a king de-
serving of seeing a miracle performed, but
for me, alas I am not worthy of redemp-
tion, neither art thou worthy of the demo-
stration of God's power."
	"Let the lad be slain as were his bro-
thers," commanded the tyrant.
	Then spoke the mother of the boys: "Give
me my child" she cried, "Oh cruel king,
let me fold him in my arms ere thou des-
troyest his innocent young life!" she threw
her arms around the lad, clasping him
tightly to her bosom, and pressed her lips
to his. "Take my life!,' she cried "kill
me first before my child,"
	"Nay" he answered, scoffingly, "I can-
not do it, for thy own laws forbid; whether
it be ox, or sheep ye shall not kill it and its
young in one day" (Lev. 27)
	"Oh, woe to thee" replied the mother,
"thou who art so particular to regard the
laws. Then pressing her boy to her heart,
"go my dear one, she said, "say to Abra-
ham, that my sacrifice hath exceeded his.
He built one altar whereon to sacrifice
Isaac; thy mother hath built seven altars,
and sacrificed seven Isaac's in one day. He
was but tempted thy mother performed."
	After the execution of her last son she
became insane, and fell from her house-top.
Where she fell she expired.
								NESTOR.


238		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


ITEMS OF THE GOSHEN PRIMARY
ASSOCIATION.
	A little party and meeting was held in
the schoolhouse at Goshen on Thursday,
April 17. Bishop Wm. Price was present,
with some unexpected visitors from Santa-
quin, whom we were pleased to see. The
meeting was opened by singing, and prayer
by our Bishop. Singing, "Don't think
there is nothing for children to do." Bish-
op Price then addressed the children, mak-
ing appropriate remarks which were very
gratifying to those who preside as well as
to the members. Sister Louisa Taylor
made some suitable remarks, and Bro. Stick-
ney, of Santaquin, likewise spoke to the
comprehension of the little folks. Several
of the children then recited pieces, after
which their President, Sarah Roberts, di-
rected them through a series of exercises,
and questioned them upon what we had
taught them, to which they creditably re-
sponded. The meeting then closed by
siuging and benediction. The President
then arranged the children by two, and 
marched them to the new Society House
close by, where they were surprised by the
sight of buns, tarts, candy and good things
neatly arranged upon low tables, to which
they were seated; Bishop Price asked the
blessing, and while they partook of the
feast prepared, showed them the necessity
of having their names enrolled, so
they could partake of the feast, and nar-
rated the history of the Supper of the
Great Bridegroom. We had a large attend-
ance that day, and several came who never
attended the meeting before; as a large
supply of refreshments was made there was
abundance for all, also for some aged per-
sons and sick people. The children then


dispersed outside and ran races, &c., and at
an early hour they were finally dismissed.
Ever desiring the interest of Primary As-
sociations, with love we are your sisters in
the Gospel,
			SARAH ROBERTS, Pres't.
			M. A TILL, Sec'y.


THE April number W. S. J. Manchester
states that the smallest division that has
been recorded since 1870 took place on
March 7th, when Mr. Courtneys resolution
was rejected by 217 votes to 103. Majority
114. In the majority by which the motion
was defeated were 107 Conservatives, in-
cluding 24 members of the Governments, 6
Home Rulers, and 64 Liberals. The mi-
nority comprised of 62 Liberals, 29 Con-
servatives, and 12 Home Rulers.
	There were many excellent speechei on
this question while it was before the House
which we will perhaps make extracts from
soon. Suffice to say the woman question
is at present being successfully agitated.


PROGRESS IN NEW HAMPSHIRE.—There
is light and hope. The world moves. On
Saturday evening last, at a district school
meeting, which district comprises most of
the inhabitants in a town of 4000 persons,
the district elected Miss Mary Gordon, one
of the Prudential Committee. She is a
lady worthy of such a phsition, and a high-
er one. It is a victory for Woman Suffrage.
The town is three to one Republican, as
against Democrats and Greenbackers. Of
course, Republicans, like all in power, are
inimical to Woman Suffrage; but the law is,
and is destined to be supported by the wo-
men. Women are timid at first, but bold and
brave afterwards, and are seldom wanting
in strict integrity and unalloyed sympathy
with right and justice.				H.
	Exeter, March 17, 1879.—Ex.

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

VOL. 7.		SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MAY 15, 1879.		No. 24.


[For the EXPONENT.
THREE RAIN-DROPS.

	I sat at my window one morning,
		And heard the sweet splash of the rain;
	While the musings of things long-forgotten
		The musical tinkling refrain
Chimed in with my dreaming, and, haply, a thought
Embodied the lesson the rain-drops taught.

	Three crystal dew-drops were descending,
		Whispering and dancing with mirth;
	"Dear sisters," said one, "though together,
		How soon will we now reach the earth;
Sent down by cloud-mother to land or to sea,
And where, oh, where will our resting place be?"

	They sped down the wind-path, and, parting,
		Each one went a different way;
	One, sorrowing, felt her bright beauty
		Grow dim with the mud where she lay.
"Ah me," sighed the rain-drop, "and is this the last
Of all I had dreamed and hoped in the past?"

	Another fell with sweet trembling
		On the glossy green leaf of a tree.
	"You need me, bright leaf, and I'm content
		To give all my strength unto thee,
For all I asked of the future was this—
To give life to earth with my life-giving kiss."

	The last, on a velvet rose resting,
		Glistened with pride, and softly said—
	"O sweet, I am adding to beauty a charm;
		How happy am I that we're wed!"
While deeper, with shame, sank far down in the ground
The rain-drop so soiled with the home she had found,

	But earth to her bosom enfolding
		The dew, whispered, 'God knoweth all;
	And he will accept thee, though tarnished
		With filth of the street at the fall."
So; meekly content with her lot, she passed through
the changes that come to earth-moistened dew,

	When soon she came laughing and singing
		To the spring bubbling up near the hill,
	And sparkling with joy kissed the pale cups
		Of lily-bells close to the rill.
Thus; chasing her sisters with frolicsome glee;
She gains the riv[-]r and sweeps to the sea.

	When the dew-drop was sadly wishing
		Such honor had never been guessed;
	But experience had given the power
		To live for and love but the best.
Have I not felt dimmed by the mud on mo cast?
Have patience, God rules, He'll right all at last.
			ESIUS.


[Column 1]
FRIENDSHIP AND CONFIDENCE.

BY HANNAH T. KING.

	When we take a retrospective view of 
our past life, and examine our relative posi-
tion to our Heavenly Father, what is it that
gives us confidence in Him? It is to know
that we have ever loved His laws, and as
far as we know them they have ever been
our delight; that we have also loved his at-
tributes and desired to imitate them; con-
sequently, virtue and goodness have been
exemplars to us, and we have practiced
them according to the light and knowledge
we possessed; that we have loved those in 
whom such were conspicuous; have set our
foot upon all that opposed these principles,
though to do so we had to wage a continual
warfare with surrounding circumstances
and surrounding spirits, both embodied and 
invisible, the warfare being generally the 
silent battle and the secret strife connected
with the working of our own soul! and 
when no eye was upon us save those of God
and angels. When we know all this has
been our experience it gives us a confi-

[Column 2]
dence towards God that nothing else can
impart with equal force! We offer up our 
prayers with a more confiding spirit, for we
have a conviction that they will be heard
and accepted. We may lose our friends,
poverty may become our portion, isolation
our present lot, calumny may assail us with
"back-wounding" stripes, misunderstand-
ing, that hydra-headed monster, may cast
its baleful influence around us, but no mat-
ter—none of these, separate or combined,
can destroy the confidence in our God which 
such a life bestows upon us; amidst it all 
we feel the light of his countenance beam-
ing upon us, we hear the whisperings of 
His Spirit in our heart, saying, "Fear not,
I am with thee; I have redeemed thee,
thou art mine!"
	Again, when we have been so fortunate
as to find a friend "after our own heart,"
when we have proved them by oft-repeated
trials, often "weighed in the balance, but
never found wanting," in our prosperity
near to us and rejoicing with us, upholding
us in the sunshine that was flooding our
pathway, watching that we became not
dazzled by it, cautioning us to be moderate
in our joys, temperate in our pleasures,
humble in our wealth, charitable in our 
riches, grateful to the bounteous Giver for
the blessings He bestowed upon us, and re-
minding us that we are but stewards, who
will have to give an account of our steward-
ship. To such an one we might confide our 
bosom secrets, with a confidence that the
grave would not be more silent. In all the 
scenes of life they would be unchanging;

		Near us when our sun doth shine,
		But nearer still when in decline.

	When wealth had made wings and fled
away, when summer friends had departed
from us, frozen up by the chilling winds of 
adversity, when our heart indeed felt heavy
with its many trials and its adverse cir-
cumstances, who remained that had waited
on our footsteps while prosperity beamed
upon us? Who stood fondly lingering by
to speak the consoling word and point us to 
a brighter day? The friend whose love
was no earth-born passion, and therefore
the things of earth had no power to affect
it—the friend that had ever had our confi-
dence! Oh, there is a divinity in the con-
dence such souls repose in each other! Such
friendship is heavenly, Godlike, and conse-
quently eternal. Are all capable of such
sentiments? I will leave an open verdict
on that query, but I know generally educa-
tion, association, and above all that prolific
source, circumstance, make us what we are,
and I know these exalted sentiments only
thrive in a cultivated heart and mind, and 
in a warm, sincere and reserved disposi-
tion, in a self-denying, self-controlling, self-
reliant spirit. These characters are the 
few, hence we find genuine friendship is 
rare, as such but rarely meet their counter-
part. But there are few whose path of life
has been so sterile as not to furnish them
with some object by which they might
form bright ideas of the truth and beauty
of a confidential friendship. I sympathize
with those who do not carry a model in 
their own bosoms, even if it be only an
ideal one; for I verily believe the ideal will
yet be realized

[Column 3]
	Let us then cultivate everything that
will promote confidence in each other. Let
us practice sincerity, and never profess one
sentiment the duplicate of which is not
in our own bosoms. Let none profess more
than they feel is truth, for it is a fearful
and most injurious thing to be deceived—it
has ruined many a character that otherwise
might have been bright and valuable, and
woe be to the deceiver, for if conscience is
not dead, no other skeleton will be needed
in the closet than such a woeful reflection.
A kiss has often sealed many a sacred com-
pact, and should not be lightly given at
any time. There should be temperance
and a "word of wisdom" in kissing, most
assuredly; this is always observable in all
high toned, refined and cultivated people;
they never kiss indiscriminately, and gen-
erally very rarely. Confidence is the very
soul of all compacts, of whatever grade or
condition. It is the spirit of love and
friendship, and friendship is love without
his wing!


THE HOSPITAL FOR WOMEN AND CHIL-
DREN.—This subject has been laid before and
received the unqualified approbation of the
authorities of the Church as an enterprise
worthy the attention and support of all
Latter-day Saints. The plan of construction,
organization and arrangements will be soon
drawn up and presented for consideration
and approval, after which the several
stakes of Zion will be solicited to take shares
in the enterprise. We trust the people
will give due consideration to the matter,
remembering that no one knows in this 
sphere of uncertainty how soon such an
institution may be a place of greatest need
and benefit to him (or her.) It will be a 
place where each settlement can look for-
ward to for instruction to students desiring
to prepare themselves to be a source of
benefit and comfort to their sisters and also
as a center where those desiring to obtain
a knowledge of the art of nursing will find
a place of instruction.
	A special meeting was called, during the 
Quarterly Conference of the sisters at Og-
den Friday evening the 9th inst. at which
time the subject was well talked up by
Miss E. R. Snow, Mrs. S. M. Kimball,
David M. Stuart, C. F. Middleton, coun-
selor to Pres. Peery of that stake and Dr.R.
B. Pratt whose remarks and suggestions
were especially calculated to impress her
hearers with good ideas upon this im-
portant subject. An encouraging interest
was manifested in the project and a prom-
ise of assistance with faith, prayers and 
MEANS. An awakened spirit is being
manifested in many of the settlements
upon this subject which is to be hoped will
extend to all and result in a complete suc-
cess to the undertaking.


IT is one of the proofs of the indestructi-
ble religious nature of man that it is easier
to rob him of his liberty than his conscience,
even though it be a superstitious one; easier
to despoil him of his gods than of his 
goods, though he would so often gain by
the loss; easier to enslave his body than
coerce his mind.—Henry Rogers.


240		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
			R. S. REPORTS.

		SOUTH BOUNTIFUL, April 24, 1869.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	At South Bountiful Ward, Aug. 17, 1877,
a Young Ladies' M. I. A. was organized.
There were present Mrs. Sarah Holmes,
President Relief Society Davis Stake, and
Mary S. Clarke, Counselor. Nancy A. Clarke,
President of the Y. L. M.I. A. of Davis
Stake, and also Bishop Wm. Brown and
Joseph Hoyan, Counselor Brolter, Wm. At-
kinson. Officers of Y. L. M. I. A. elected:

	Lucinda Brown, President.
	Sarah Hogan,	}
	Eliza S. Nelsen,	}	Counselors.
	Phebe Hales,	}
	Evelyn Benson,	}

	After the organization was completed
some of the sisters and brethren spoke, en-
couraging the young ladies to be faithful
and united and sustain each other. The
Association now numbers fifty-seven mem-
bers, average attendance thirty-five. We
meet semi-monthly, and a good spirit pre-
vails.
	Sister Brown resigned her position on ac-
count of moving away, and on the 4th of
April the Society was re-organized as fol-
lows:

	Eliza S. Nelsen, President,
	Sarah A. Hogan,	}
	Phebe Hales,	}
	Evelyn Benson,	} Counselors,
	Melvina Moss,	}
	Helen M. Ellis,	}
	Martha Arnold,	}

	Sarah P. Moss,	} Secretaries.
	Alice E. Nobles,	}
	Olive Jonson, Treasurer.

	Wishing success to the EXPONENT, we
remain,
		Your sisters in the Gospel,
				ELIZA S. NELSEN, Pres't.
				SARAH P. MOSS, Sec'y.

			PETTYVILLE, April 21, 1879.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	We were organized into a Relief Society
Oct. 3, 1870, by Bishop Petty and Elder
J. C. Snow. The following officers were
elected unanimously:

	Jane C. Snow, President,
	Susan A. Barton,		} Counselor.
	Emeline M. Johnston,	}
	Hannah M. Henrie, Secretary,
	Almira Bunce, Treasurer.

	Two Teachers and six members enrolled.
Since then our numbers have gradually in-
creased, and we hope and trust that we
may ever be alive to our duties and respon-
sibilities that rest upon us in common with
our sisters in such a great and glorious
cause.
	Yours very respectfully,
		HANNAH M. HENRIE, Sec'y.

				FRANKLIN, Feb. 10, 1879.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	We do not wish to be forgotten, and there-
fore will endeavor to give a short sketch of
our progression. Although we are back-
ward in reporting ourselves we are in a 
thriving condition. We were reorganized
last May by Sister Eliza R. Snow and E.
Davis. I think it aroused the sisters and
caused them to realize more fully their po-
sition in the kingdom of God, for we have

[Column 2]
a better attendance at meeting, and all
manifest a desire to press onward in the
work that we are engaged in. We feel
truly thankful to our beloved sisters for
their visit and good instructions; would be
pleased to see them oftener. We appreciate
their good counsel and feel to say God bless
them in their labors. Our officers are as
follows:
	Elizabeth Fox, President,
	Sophia Meechan,	} Counselors,
	Mary Hawks,	}
	Susie Parnell,	} Secretaries.
	Annie Hatch		}
	Agnes Durant, Treasurer.
	Quite a number of Teachers and the
names of fifty-two members enrolled; thirty
average attendance. Our meeting house,
that has been in contemplation so long, is
now completed with the exception of seats,
which will be finished soon. It was dedi-
cated Dec. 12, 1878. We had a supper and
ball given the evening before on the occa-
sion. Pres. Moses Thatcher and Counselor
Hammond, accompanied by Bro. Ormsby,
were present. We had a time of feasting
and rejoicing, in our prosperity not forget-
ting the poor. The brethren gave much
good instruction in regard to the poor,
storing grain, and raising our children,
which if we regard will prove a great bene-
fit to us. We hold our meetings semi-
monthly. We send our annual report end-
ing June 18, 1878.
Total receipts................................$333 63
Disbursements:
To needy........................................ 48 11
Relief House................................. 80 03
Logan Temple............................... 14 02
Total amount on hand................... 191 47
We have 100 bu. and 44 lbs. of wheat on
hand at present.
	With our estimable President at our
head we are trying to do our duty and keep
up with the spirit of the times. We are
always glad to receive your valuable paper,
and fully endorse the remarks of the sisters
contained in its pages. I remain
		Your sister in the Gospel,
				SUSIE PARNELL, Sec'y.

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Brief report of the annual meeting of
Minersville Relief Society, held in the
Meeting House Jan. 2, 1879. We were
honored by the presence of Bishop James
McKnight and his two counselors William
Wood and Joseph Banks, Sen.; many more
of the brethren attended. After singing
and prayer, our President made some en-
couraging remarks and stated the object of
the meeting. The Bishop and his counsel-
ors instructed the sisters in the various du-
ties pertaining to Relief Societies, which
were very interesting. The Financial re-
port of the past year was read and accepted.

			PANGUITCH, March 12, 1879

EDITOR EXPONENT:

	In behalf of the Panguitch Ward Relief
Society, we send you the following report:
There has been donated since our last Semi-
Annual Conference (aside from anything
paid to our Relief Society house) the sum
of $62 58.
Disbursed to Manti Temple.............$26 75
Missionary..........................................  17 60
Needy..................................................    9 95

	At a recent meeting of our R. S. our
President said, although we were a little
behind the times she would like to have
an expression of the sister's feelings in re-
gard to polygamy and the opposition that
is manifested against it. It was carried
unanimously that we heartily endorse and
sustain the sentiments of our sisters in Salt
Lake and other places with regard to the
anti-polygamy movement of our enemies.
			MYRA HENRIE, Pres't.
			MARY B. HEYWOOD, Sec'y.


MRS. STANTON AND MORMON
WOMEN.
	Mrs. Stanton enquires, "Did you see in
Lucy’s paper of the 29th a correspondent
makes some strictures on the Mormon wo-
men on our platform, which Lucy does not
defend as she should? If George Q. Can-
non can sit in the Congress of the United
States without compromising that body on
the question of Polygamy, I should think
Mormon women might sit on our platform
without making us responsible for their re-
ligious faith. If, as the husband of four
wives, he can be tolerated in the Councils
of the nation and treated with respect,
surely the wives of only the fourth part of
man should be four times as worthy of ten[-]
der consideration. When the women of [-]
whole Territory are threatened with dis[--]
franchisement where should they go to
make their complaint but to the platform o[-]
the National Suffrage Association? If yo[-]
have not already rebuked the ‘Woman'[-]
Journal,’ do say this much."
	We have not rebuked the “Woman'[-]
Journal” for this and many other evidenc[--]
of the slave spirit, because we have only
pity for those women who turn and rend
their sister women working differently from
themselves. We so well know the sharp
wrongs women have endured, crushing in
many instances all their self-respect, th[-]
we do not like to rebuke them, and [-]-spe[-]
cially those who are our fellow-laoore[-]
For all slavish women a large amount[-]
pity mingles with our contempt. Whe[-]
we assumed the editorship of the "Ball[-]
box," adding to it the name of "Nation[-]
Citizen," as peculiarly significant of the d[-]
mands of the National Woman Suffrag[-]
Association that the right of suffrage shoul[--]
be secured by National law to all citizens [--]
the United Staies, thus taking the pow[--]
over the ballot out of the hands of State[-]
we issued a prospectus in accordance wi[--]
our purpose. * * *
	In accordance with this announcemen[-]
we have striven to act. There is not a wo-
man in the vilest slums and streets of New
York to whose protest against wrong the
National Association will not give heed
and the "National Citizen" espouse their 
cause.

For the EXPONENT.
				BE FIRM.

	My sisters be firm! If trials we have
		Either in pleasant or stormy weather
	Let us c[-]ing to each other in union and love
		And go hand in hand together

	Oftimes we are sad when clouds appear
		Yet cheerful when days are bright,
	The Spirit of God our hearts will cheer
		And show us the truth and the light.

	If chastened in love let us kiss the r[-]d
		And face all kinds of weather,
	Be true to each other, ourselves and our God
		And walk hand in hand together.

									—M. W. W.
Salt Lake City.


GROWING OLD GRACEFULLY.

Softly, oh softly, the years have swept by thee,
	Touching thee lightly with tenderest care:
Sorrow and care did they often bring n'gh thee;
	Yet they have left thee but beauty to w[-]ar,
		Grorwing old gracefully,
			Grac[-]fu[-]ly fair.

Far from the storms that are washing the oc[-]an; 
	Nearer each day to the pl[-]a[-]ant home light;
Far from the waves that are big with commotion, 
	Under full sail and the harbor in sight!
		Growing old cheerfully,
			Cheerful and bright.

Past all the winds that were adverse and ch[-]ll[-]ng;
	Past all the islands that lured thee to rest;
Past all the currents that wooed thee unwilling,
	Far from the port of the land of the blest,
		Growing old peacefully,
			Peacefully and blest.

Never a feeling of envy or [-]orrow,
	Where the bright faces of children are seen;
Never a year from their youth wouldst thou borrow,
	T[-]ou dost remember what lieth between,
		Growing old willingly,
		Gladly, I ween!

Rich in experience that angels might covet;
	Rich in a faith which has grown with thy year[-];
Rich in a love that grew from and above it;
	Soothing thy sorrows and hushing thy fears.
		Growing old wealthily,
			Loving and dear.

[-]earts at the sound of thy coming are lightened,
	Ready and willing thy band to relieve;
Many a face at thy kind words has brightene[-],
	"It is more blessed to give than receive!"
		Growing old happily;
			Blest, we believe.
				—Christian Globe


[Column 1]
	SPRING TIME AND VIOLETS.

	The sweet breath of the young maiden
Spring is on the air, and her pathway is
strewn with flowers, rare. She has come
in all her blushing beauty, and our hearts
go out to her in cheery welcome. The
whole machinery of nature is in motion;
all that was still and drear, gloomy and dis-
mal, in its outward, aspect, warms into
vigor and life at her approach. The swal-
lows fly back to the same old places in the
eaves, the porches and balconies, where be-
foretime they reared their fledgelings,, and
twitter away as familiarly as though they
knew the places and people rejoiced to see
them again. The robin sings in his sweet-
est strain to welcome the light.footed maid-
en in her flowery garb and fresh green
mantle, as she softly glides along o'er val-
ley, hill and plain, breathing fragrance ev-
erywhere, and revivifying earth-life in all
its varied forms. Her drapery is fringed
with evergreen, and fluttering white in the
breeze and sifting round her in cloudy
mists are the apple-blossoms the very per-
fection of sweetest perfume. Violets peep
up from their lowly beds, too modest to
open wide the[-]r bright eyes, but linger in
the shade of the soft grasses, till they hea

[Column 2]
the voices of the brooklets murmuring, the
chorus of the forest birds tumultuously
trilling glad notes, and then the bright-
eyed violets whisper softly to the forget-
me-nots she approaches and the gentle
breeze faintly wafts their sweet odors, like
a messenger of welcome. Daisies and lilies
nod to each other and bend gracefully as
she passes by.
	All nature gives evidence of some great
change, some life-giving power that mor-
tals comprehend not, but name it mnst
have, and what title could be more signifi-
cant of the processes that are going forward
in earth's realms than Spring?
	Gentle maiden, thou hast been most gra-
cious in thy favors, for earlier than they
were wont the roses bud and lilacs bloom;
profusely hast thou scattered garlands of
woodland flowers and wreaths of trailing
evergreens; loyally didst thou bequeath
thy heritage of bloom in the merry May-
day festivities. And the birds, who ever
wait thy coming and watch for the first
tender leaves ere they begin to weave their
nests, are pouring forth their highest notes
not only in the orchard and meadow, but
"in the heart of the dim old woods."
	Many are the pleasures the pretty maid-
en brings and scatters round our pathway;
how joyously we welcome her fair young
face, on which the sunshine lingers long
and tenderly. No burning heat, no scorch-
ing rays, but subdued by the gentleness of
her modest manner, the sun is like a bashful
young lover who takes his cue from the
freaks and fancies of his lady-love. Beau-
teous maiden, we welcome thee to our moun-
tain home; we are better for thy coming, for
all things brighten in the sunshine of thy
smiles. We greet thee proudly! for thou
bringest us royal blessings—in thy hand is
the wand of enchantment, that clothes the 
whole [-]arth in freshness and beauty, and
opens up the fountains of water, that give life
to all animate nature. We talk to thee, and
thou answerest us in the voices of birds, and
trees, and rills, and flowers; these speak to
the heart pathetically, and impart a con-
sciousness of the inherent germ that warms
iuto being through auspicious influences,
but stinted and chilled by winter's frost
and snow lies dormant. Thou teachest us
many lessons, and thy methods are simple
and grand, and we may learn of thee if we
will, for thou art most gentle towards earth's
children. There's not a footprint thou hast
made but bears the impress of a living
power inherited from God, the genuine
stamp of Him, Who lives and moves in all
things by his infinity.
	Who does not love the Spring and the
crown of glory that she wears so regally?
Let me see, her jewels are the flowers—the
violet, it is suggestive of modesty; the
forget-me-not is memory; the rose of love;
the pansy of thoughts, be they sad or gay;
the water lily of grace; all these and many
more she brings to the laps of the fresh
young maidens who wait for her with eager
anticipation, knowing that she has been a
long journey far over the seas. Year after
year our Father sends us this sweet messen-
ger, and should we be forgetful of the ten-
der love and devotion manifested in the
harmony and order of the seasons? And
while we sew the seeds in springtime, let
us engraft into our hearts the moral lesson
of good seed sown in good ground, that
shall spring up and yield an abundant har-
vest in our hearts and the hearts of our
children, and thank the Great Creator for
the beautiful methods of instruction in wis-
dom, faith and hope vouchsafed to us
through the poetry of Springtime.
							AUNT EM.


[For the EXPONENT.
	WOMAN’S VOICE.

	After listenting to the reports of the last
General Conference, I cannot help reflecting
upon the wise providences of God with re-
gard to the Latter-day work. Who is there
among the Latter-day Saints that cannot
see the great and mighty work that eventu-
ally w[-]ll be brought about through that so
much despised principle called plural mar-
riage. How honored are the women of
the Latter-day Saints, to bear the souls of
the men and women who will build up the
kingdom of God in these last days. Not-
withstanding the power of the destroyer,
the rapid increase of children that have
been born in Zion in the last few years is a
testimony to every honest lover of truth
that “the stone the builder rejected will
become the head of the corner.”
	How careful we ought to be to train the
minds of the rising generation, to give
them a correct understanding of the duties
and obligations that will rest upon them.
Let us as mothers be alive to our responsi-
bilities, by setting them an example that is
worthy of imitation. I think our Gentile
neighbors are helping the work along a
great deal in trying to stop the practice of
plural marriage. I feel like saying, Father,
forgive them, they know not what they do.
They can never do us any harm. I believe
these things are necessary to keep us on
our guard against the hour of temptation,
and remind us of the covenants we have
made to our Heavenly Father. They can-
not realize that our spirits have been re-
served for this great Latter-day work; they
are envious toward us because they cannot,
help themselves. Our help is in the God
of Jacob; we have proved Him to be our
friend when we have been true to Him and
true to ourselves. Let us show to our ene-
mies our integrity and confidence in God
the Eternal Father, and they will hide
their faces in shame. I never trouble about
the plans and devices of the wicked against
us, for it will all recoil upon themselves,
and if they do not believe it now, if they
wait a little while then they will realize
the truth of it. Our Lord and Master said
when he was on the earth, that through
much tribulation we should enter his king-
dom. Let us as mothers in Israel never be
found, either in public or private, speaking
evil, or using any influence against the de-
signs of the Almighty, for if we do we
shall surely feel the chastening rod; and 
better it would have been for us if we had
never known for ourselves the will of God
concerning us. If we wish to receive the
reward of the faithful we must be true and
faithful ourselves; we must not stand still
and think we have done all that is required
of us, each day as long as we live in mor-
tality we will always have outside influ-
ences to contend with, until we entirely
overcome ourselves and bring our wills into
perfect sujection to the will of our Father
in Heaven. The sooner we do this the less
power our enemies will have over us. The
Lord has promised that no weapon that is
formed against us shall prosper. Sisters,
let us be thankful that our lot is cast with
the Saints of God, and let us strive to dis-
charge the duties of life honorably before
the Lord, and all will be well with us.
		SARAH LAYTON.
Kaysville, April 21, 1879.


﻿THE new Secretary for the Territory of
Utah Arthur L. Thomas arrived in this
city on the 12th inst. He took the oath of
office the following day. The oath was ad-
ministered by Judge Emerson.
