SALT LAKE CITY, MAY 15, 1879.


[Column 1]
﻿PUBLIC DEMONSTRATION.

RECEPTION GIVEN TO GEN. D. H. WELLS.

	Never has there been such a grand public
demonstration by this people as that given to
Gen. Wells on his release from the Peniten-
tiary, Tuesday, May 6. The people were in-
dignant—words are too weak to express the
feelings that were aroused in the hearts of the
Latter-day Saints by his sentence to two days'
imprisonment and $100 fine—for not betraying
that which he held more sacred than his life.
His words in the Court Room on that momen-
tous occasion will be handed down to posterity,
and his name will forever be cherished by all
who reverence and honor integrity to principle.
"It has ever been and is a principle of my life
never to betray a friend, a brother, my country,
my God or my religion."
	At a large meeting of the brethren and sis-
ters held in the Council House, Sunday, May
4, after the services in the Tabernacle, commit-
tees were appointed to arrange a programme
of the proceedings: Mayor Lit[-]le, Hon.
John Sharp, Col. Theo. McKean, Hon. Wm.
Jennings, Col. John R. Winder, Marshal A.
Burt, Geo. Crismon, Esq.

ASSISTANT COMMITTEES:

	Judge Elias Smith and Col. McKean for Salt
Lake County;
	First Presidents of Seventies, Presidents and
Counselors of the Quorums of Priests, Teachers
and Deacons;
	Mrs. M. Isabella Horne, Mrs. Sarah M. Kim-
ball, Mrs. Bathsheba W. Smith, Mrs. Hannah
T. King, Mrs. Matilda M. Barratt, Mrs. Eliza-
beth Howard for Relief Societies;
	Messrs. Junius F. Wells, Joseph H. Felt,
George C. Lambert, John W. Taylor, R. R.
Badger, M. H. Hardy, Young Men's Improve-
ment Associations;
	Mrs. Louie Felt and Clara Conrad; for Young
Ladies' Societies;
	Messrs. George Goddard, Wm. Willis, S. L.
Evans, F. A. Mitchell, L. W. Richards, J. C.
Cutler, for the Sunday Schools;
	C. W. Penrose, John T. Caine, Miss Annie
Wells, Jos. A. West, J. C. Graham, Geo C.
Lambert, A. W. Winberg, editors and re[-]re-
sentatives of the press;
	J. C. Sanberg and Henry Reiser for the Scan
dinavian and German citizens.
	Order of p[-]ocession: Band. Pr[-]s. Taylor
and escort. Territorial, County and C[-]ty offi-
cers, Mayors and City Councils from various
places, and invited guests. Repr[-]sentatives of
the press. Salt Lake City fire bigade. Band.
Relief Societies with banners. Band. Sab-
bath Schools with banners. Band. Mutual
Improvement and Retrenchment Societies
with banners. Band. Seventies, High Priests,
Elders. Bishops and the lesser Priesthood

[Column 2]
with banners. Band. Scandinavian and Ger-
man citizens with banners. Band. General
citizens on foot and on horseback.
	The trains from the North and the South
brought a laage number of leading citizens and
people from the counties adjacent to Salt Lake
Co.
	At 9 o'clock a. m. the carriages to convey
Pres. John Taylor and escort were drawn up in
the vicinity of the President's office, and start-
ed out to receive Gen. Wells. First was the
Tenth Ward Brass Band in uniform, in a large
band-carriage drawn by four spanking gr[-]y
horses. Next came a carriage with the Com-
mittee, then a barouche drawn by four white
horses, containing Pres. John Taylor, Mayor
F. Little, Bishop Edward Hunter, Pres. Angus
M. Cannon. Next, carriages with Elders C. C.
Rich, Lorenzo Snow, F. D. Richa[-]ds, Jo[-]eph
F. Smith and Albert Carrington of the Apos-
tles. After these, carriages with the Mayors
and City Councils of this and ten other muni-
cipalities, Territorial and County officers, re-
presentatives of the press, the various quorums
of the Priesthood, Relief Societies, Young
Men's and Young Ladies' Improvement Asso-
ciations, with several bands of music and a
long train of carriages containing ladies and
gentlemen, followed by a company of horse-
men. The co[-]tege proceeded to Gen. Burton's
farm, where Gen. Wells had spent the night
after his release from imprisonment. Arriving
at Gen. Burton's, Gen. Wells was g[-]eeted by
the party in the following

EXTEMPORANEOUS SPEECH MADE BY PRESI-
DENT JOHN TAYLOR.

	President Wells: In behalf of the commu-
nity who feel outraged by the treatment you
have received, I propose to pr[-]sent to you the
confidence, the respect, the honor and the es-
teem of the people whose hearts, feelings and
affections are with you.
	We do not come here to interfere with any
matters pertaining to the action of courts; we
leave an intelligent public to act upon that
matter, and to judge according to their wisdom
and discretion. But we have come to exhibit
to you our esteem and to show you that whilst
you are true to your friends, your principles,
your country and your God, that your friends
are equally true to you. You see exhibited be-
fore you the spontaneous feeling of this vast
people who have come to meet and to honor
the man who rather than submit to betray his
friends, his country, and his God, would per-
mit himself to be deprived of liberty and be
immured in prison. We have come, General
Wells, to wipe away a stain sought to be heaped
upon you by the bigoted, unreflecting and
thoughtless, and this demonstration is the
spontaneous impulse and action of a generous,
patriotic, kind-hearted and friendly people.
For this purpose we have assembled here to-
day; and in behalf of this whole community,
I tender to you our sincere regards and our
most heartfelt sympathy."
	To which the following impromptu response
was made by

GEN. D. H. WELLS.

	"I will simply say that I should feel exceed-
ingly sorry for myself if I felt, for a single mo-
ment, that any of my friends supposed, or
could entertain the idea, that I could do other-
wise than I have done. I would be sorry for
myself to feel, or to even think for a moment,
that I could swerve from my integrity to the
covenant which I have had the privilege, yes,
the inestimable privilege, of making with my
God. I can well afford to suffer bonds, fines
and imprisonment, and even death if necessa-
ry—which by the way has no terrors for me—

[Column 3]
but to forfeit my fellowship with you, my
brethren, or a single particle of that confidence
which is reposed in me by the people of God,
through violating the sacred and holy cove-
nants we have entered into, I could not afford.
That would indeed be a source of sorrow and
regret, not only through time but for all eter-
nity.
	My treatment at the hands of the United
States officers during my imprisonment has
been civil and courte[-]us.
	I feel happy and well, and am rejoiced to
meet you, though I did not expect any such
demonstration as this. I thank you heartily;
but not, however, so much for myself as the
expression of your feelings to sustain the prin-
ciple. I knew that you are my friends, and
that you are the friends of the Most High God;
and I pray that I may ever be worthy of your
confidence and esteem, and be the friend of the
Lord. I will not detain you; but in the fulness
of my heart I say, God bless you forever; and
again thank you for this expression of your
kindness and love."

	Gen. Wells entered the carriage with Pres.
Taylor, the most spirited music was played by
the bands in attendance, and the cortege start-
ed for the city at a fast trot. On East Temple
Street were formed in order the Relief Socie-
ties, Sunday Schools, Improvement Associa-
tions, High Priests, Seventies, Elders, Bishops,
Priests, Teachers, Deacons; the City Fire Bri-
gade in uniform, Scandinavian and German
citizens besides a vast concourse of people, on
the streets,and filling the porches and balconies,
and standing on the roofs. Stores were closed
and hundreds of flags, banners, and stream-
ers, with appropriate mottoes and sentiments
fluttered in the breeze, giving enthusiasm to
the populace. It was an immense gathering;
never was there such a day in Israel. Over three
hundred carriages were in the procession, be-
sides the long line of people on foot. They pro-
ceeded to the large Tabernacle, which was soon
filled in every part with people, and many thou-
sands could not gain admittance. A magnificent
sight, never to be forgotten by those who were
present, Seven bands of music were seated in
the choir; banners, flags and mottoes were ar-
ranged around the gallery; and when Gen.
Wells appeared upon the stand the cheers were
deafening; ladies waved their handkerchiefs,
banners and streamers moving, added to the
general enthusiasm, which for a few moments
was unrestrained. After inspiring music by
the several bands, the congregation was called
to order by Pres. Taylor, and Elder F. D.
Richards offered up, in behalf of all assembled,
a most eloquent, inspiring and significant
prayer, dedicating the meeting and all assem-
bled there to the Lord. Closed by saying,
May the doings of this day be sanctified to the
good of many people; and we ascribe all honor,
praise and power to Thee O God now and for-
ever, in the name of Jesus Christ, our Redeemer.
Amen.

BRIEF SPEECH DELIVERED BY COUNSELOR D,
H. WELLS.

	My Friends, my Brethren, my Sisters and
Fellow Citizens;—I sincerely thank you for
this demonstration of your love, sympathy
and respect: probably this is a demonstration
of such a character as never was seen before.
No words can express the indignation and re-
sentment which burns within our bosoms, un-
utterable! Has it come to this that in free
America a man cannot keep his covenant with
his God, nor worship him in accordance with
the dictates of his own conscience without
suffering the penalties of bonds, fines, im-
prisonment and death? Oh tell it not in


[Column 1]
Britain, nor publish it in the streets of Rome
or Spain! Acts speak louder and are more ex-
pressive than words. Therefore, after again
thanking you, in the name of insulted freedom,
and in accordance with the dictates of wis-
dom, we will close these proceedings and re-
tire in silence to our happy and undisturbed
homes. And now may the peace of heaven
and the blessing of Israel’s God rest in rich
abundance upon you all, and upon every one
whose soul responds to the sound, the echoes
of truth and integrity, in the name of Jesus.
Amen.

PRESIDENT JOHN TAYLOR

Delivered an address expressing the most
kindly feelings, and lofty sentiments.
	We are not assembled here to-day for any
other purposes than those of peace. We have
no quarrel with our Government; we have no
quarrel with our Judiciary, nor with the
United States; we have no quarrel with any
people upon the face of the earth. Our mis-
sion is, “Peace on earth and good will to all’
men.” And although men have [-]ought to in-
terfere with our religious faith and to control
our consciences, it is understood by us that
God and His laws have never been palatable
to the great mass of mankind generally. And
therefore if men in the different ages have had
to endure persecutions and prosecutions for the
sake of the eternal truths of God, it is nothing
strange that we should have to partake a little
of the same thing.
	We have met here, however, to welcome our
friend and brother, President Daniel H Wells,
to our midst; to evince a feeling that exists
and permeates among the Latter-day Saints
and that dwells in their bosoms, as well as in
the bosoms of all honorable men, namely, that
of maintaining and sustaining their friends
that dare be true to God, to their country and
its institutions, to their religion and their
friends; who have the moral courage and dare
to act upon the principles of honor in the face
of narrow prejudice, proscriptions, penalties,
and the evils which even honorable men have
to meet in life, frequently, from the hands of
those whose positions ought to raise them
above the narrow limits of bigotry and in-
tolerance; and knowing what the feelings of
the Latter-day Saints were in relation to those
things, we wished them to be gathered to-
gether to evince that respect to General Wells
which, independent of his honorable position
for years in our midst, his conduct merits. We
wish to evince a disposition to sustain correct
principles under all circumstances.
* * * * * *
	Do not then, my brethren, let us have any
quarrel with these people. Our mission is
“peace on earth and good will to men.” And
if men are in error we will try our utmost to
correct them; if they are weak and ignorant
or tyrannical, we will try to bear with
them and then leave them in the hands of
God, for we can afford to wait. There is a God
that rules in the heavens. There is an old say-
ing, that “the mills of the gods grind slowly,
but that they grind exeeding fine.” We can
wait the time, the Lord’s just time in relation
to th[-]se matters, placing ourselves in His
hands. We appeal to the great God for re-
dress, and let all Israel say “amen!” (the vast
concourse of people re ponded, “amen!”) We
can afford to treat all people right whether
they can us or not.
* * * * * *
	We will not try to place ourselves in op-
position to the government, but stand shoulder
to shoulder with all true patriots and lovers of
their country and its institutions; as long as

[Column 2]
there are any to maintain .these, principles we
will be with them and on the side of right.
Amen. (Great applause.)	
	After the speeches were over there was music
by a martial band, “See the conquering hero
comes.” Col. John Sharp, of the committee,
said there had been preparations made to have
a cold lunch in the Tabernacle for invited
guests and visitors from the country. Benedic-
tion by Elder Joseph F. Smith.
	The bands were playing while the people
dispersed. About 10,000 people took part in
the procession, and 15,000 more were spectators.
	In the evening Gen. Wells was serenaded at
his home by the Tabernacle Choir and Scandi-
navian vocal club. Pres. John Taylor, Elder
Joseph F. Smith, the Committee of ladies and
other frends called upon him and participated
in the enjoyment of a pleasant social enter-
tainment with the family.
	The day was a most remarkable one, and the
memory of that day's occurrences will live in
the hearts of the Latter-day Saints to the end
of time. It is impossible to do justice to it in
the few paragraphs we are able to publish, but
it will be written of in story and in song, and
will be a theme worthy the author.

HOME AFFAIRS.

	THE SENATE Judiciary Committee consid-
ered the nomination of Corbin as Chief Jus-
tice for Utah Territory, and decided to report
adversely, unless the president should with-
draw the nomination.

	THE CASE of Elder G[-]orge Reynolds, in
which the Latter day Saints are so deeply in-
terested, has recently been receiving direct at-
tention from the Supreme Court of the United
States. The decision is, "And that this cause
be and the same is hereby remanded to the
said Supreme Court (Utah), with the instruc-
tions to cause the sentence of the District Court
to be set aside and a new one entered on the
verdict in all respects like that before imposed,
except so far as it requires the imprisonment
to be at hard labor."

	THE "CARTHAGE Republican" (an Illinois
paper) states that Mrs. Emma Bidamon, died
in Nauvoo on the 30th of April. Among the
Latter-day Saints, in days gone by, she was
familiarly known as "Sister Emma," wife oi
the Prophet Joseph Smith. She was consid-
ered rather a remarkable woman, possessing
great influence and unusually strong charac-
teristics, which if properly directed, as in the 
ea[-]ly days of this Church, would have made
her name illustrious in the history of the wo-
men of the Latter-day Saints down to the end
of time.

	THIS MORNING our beloved sisters Pres. E.
R. Snow and Pres. M. I. Horne start on a tour
to some of the settlements in Sanpete and Se-
vier Counties. They will meet with the Re-
lief Societies and Y. L. M. I. A in the settle-
ments upon their way, transacting such busi-
ness with these institutions as may be essential
and practicable; also organizing branches of
the Deseret Silk Association and Primary As-
sociations. They expect to reach Richfield to
attend the Stake Conference, which will be
held on the 24th and 25th of this month.

	DR. ELLIS R. Sh'pp has just returned from
a professional visit in the North, and by per-
sonal observation has become cognizant of the 
increasing need of woman physicians—women
thoroughly edu[-]ate and trained for the impor-
tant duties of attending the sick and ailing,
especially of their own sex. She desires to use
her energies in meeting this demand by giving

[Column 2]
instructions both theoretical and practical.
The course will consist of two terms, six
months each, with five lessons a week. The
first lecture will be given on the 26th inst.
Any further information can be obtained by
correspondence, or personal communication at
her residence 11th Ward, Salt Lake City.

	WE HAVE learned by letter of the sudden
death of a young lady whom we met and
formed a pleasant acquaintance with while in
Washington. Miss Minnie Nason died early
in April last of congestion of the lungs. At
noon she was bright and happy, and between
five and six o'clock she was dead; she took a
chill, and congestion of the lungs followed so
quickly she was gone in a few moments. She
was indeed a lovely girl, graceful, sensitive,
refined, and possessed that charming good na-
ture that imparted a kindly feeling to all. We
sympathize deeply with her mother, aunts and
relatives, for we know how deep the wound
must be, but we can only say to them, believe
"Our Father doeth all things well,"

	WE LEFT this city Wednesday morning,
April 30, in company with Miss E. R. Snow,
President of L. D. S. womens' organiza-
tions. We traveled through some of the
settlements North, in Utah and a part of
Idaho called Malad Valley, to visit the sisters'
societies in that vicinity. At Brigham City
Mrs. H. A. Snow, Pres. of R. S. Box Elder
Stake, her counselor S. P. Booth, Sec. Mary E.
Snow and Pres. Harriet Huntsucker joined our 
party. Together we visited several towns in
Box Elder and Oneida Counties, re-organized
two Relief Societies and organized five Primary
Ass[-]ciations. We returned to Ogden in time
to attend the Quarterly Conference of Weber
Stake held May 9th and 10th in the Ogden
Tabernacle. We were all most cordially
welcomed and hospitably entertained; the
brethren and sisters came in wagons from one
settlement to another to hear the sisters talk.
We intend to publish notes made during our
absence, but have not space in this issue.

	THIS NUMBER completes the seventh volume
of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT. This little paper
has now become widely known, and has en-
tered many a home where a Mormon woman
would scarcely be received at all. It is a power
in the hands of Latter-day Saint women,
which should be wielded for good. The EXPO-
NENT speaks brave words for women in encour-
aging them in the elevation of their sex. It is
also a medium through which the organizations
among the sisters are represented and thus be-
come interested in each others welfare and aid
and assist mutually in sustaining home indus-
tries, and in educating the youth of Zion in all
that pertains to practical usefulness and spirit-
ual culture. Sisters, you who appreciate your
position, you cannot afford to be without the
EXPONENT. It will silently strengthen you
mothers, sisters and daughters in your labors
of love. We have ample opportunity of know-
ing that this little paper is doing a good mis-
sionary work for Mormonism. We trust our
patrons will renew their subscriptions prompt-
ly.


FOUR ACCOMPLISHED sisters, Clara, Hilda,
Ellen and Henriette Montalba—of Spanish
blood—live in London. The youngest,
Henriette, is a charming sculptor; the other
three are successful painters,—Miss Clara,
a pupil of Isabey, being especially remark-
able for her venetian scenes. There young
ladies are friends and favorites of the Prin-
cess Louise, and Misses Hilda and Henri-
ette Montalba have crossed the ocean at her
invitation, and are now visiting her at Ri-
deau Hall.


244		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
MRS. GAGE'S SPEECH AT THE WASH-
INGTON CONVENTION.

UNITED STATES RIGHTS VERSUS STATES
RIGHTS.

	Mrs. Gage said, I have never so fully felt
the responsibility of the work in which we
are engaged as at the present time. Never
had such a feeling of solemnity, such a con-
ception of the vastness of our undertaking.
To lift the burden which for ages has
weighed down one-half of humanity, to
meet the wrongs, the accumulated suffer-
ing, the injustice of the world:—to combat
the unceasing prejudice which everywhere
meets the reformer. Fain would I stop
and rest,—shut my ears to the cry of sor-
rowing souls, and rest for the remainder of
my day[-]. But I cannot. The burden of
the world's wrongs is upon me. I cannot,
though I would, shut my eyes to the sor-
rows of the crushed ones of earth. Every-
where the great problems of poverty and
vice and crime rise before me, and remem-
bering the words of the good book regard-
ing the one who grasped the handle of the
plough and looked back, I feel that shrink
from it as I may, I must still work on.
	I shall speak upon the foundation princi-
ples of our government, which, without
conceit to myself, I can say are better un-
derstood by the ladies of the National Wo-
man Suffrage Association, my associates
upon this platform, than by the statesmen
in yonder Capitol. These consist of per-
sonal, individual rights. It is a curious
anomaly in the history of the world that
the rights of property have always been
recognized before the rights of person.
The revolution had for its war-cry, "No
taxation without representation." What
our forefathers wanted was simply protec-
tion of property, and at the meeting of the 
first Congress, Washington indignantly dis-
claimed the intention of the colonists to
frame an independent government. The
English idea of rights has always been the
rights of property. Many persons are
amazed that the woman suffrage question.is
so much farther advanced in Great Britain
than in our own country, for women house-
holders, rate-payers, vote there at all muni-
cipal elections. But it is not themselves,
but property which votes. For instance, a
woman with several grown sons pays a cer-
tain rental for a house in which she lives
with these sons. She pays the rent and
she votes, but her sons not being rent-pay-
ers, do not vote. This is the action of the
law in Scotland and, I doubt not, in Eng-
land also. You see it is not the person who
votes, but the property. Our country start-
ed tnto govermental life under this idea of
property rights, and our nation is not yet
fully impregnated with its own true princi-
ples, which are the rights of person, indi-
vidual rights. I was looking through a
work issued by the International Peace So-
ciety, when I came upon a paragraph ex-
pressing surprise that although the time
was past when subjects were vassals whose
property and lives the sovereign could alike
use at his option, yet now when sovereigns
could not thus take the property of subjects
without the consent of their representatives
they could plunge the country into war to
which these subjects had not consented,
thus controlling their lives where they
could not their property. But this is ex-
plainable when we recognize the singular
fact permeating history, that property has
ever been held more sacred than person
and its rights secured before the rights of
person.

[Column 2]
	At the framing of the Constitution some
twelve years after our Declaration of In-
dependence, an effort was made on part of
certain delegates to allow States influence
in proportion to their property instead of
their population. It was argued that
States should not desire influence only in
proportion to the taxes they paid. But
this proposition was combatted by other
delegates who recognized the real princi-
ples of our government to be those of per-
sonal rights, and they argued that all per-
sons in a state of nature would have equal
right to representation at time of the first
formation of government, and that to allow
property to have greater representation
would be to make slaves of those not pos-
sessing property. So after much debate
the question was finally settled upon the
basis of personal rights; but still, such is
the influence of inherited tendencies: We
find our nation still governed, in great de-
gree, by these fancied rights of property.
We frequently hear men say, "I am will-
ing to have tax-paying women vote."
	In other words, these men are more En-
glish than Ameriean; they are more per-
meated with the rights of property than of
person.
	There is another American idea that is
not understood as it should be; this is, pro-
tection for the right of suffrage, and here
I shall endeavor to show you the principles
of the National Woman's Suffrage Associa-
tion, and their true accordance with the na-
tional idea of personal rights. What we
demand is national protection for national
citizens in the exercise of their right of vot-
ing, and this principle does not confine
itself to women, but applies equally to men.
United Stated rights is the next great ques-
tion to come before the nation. State
Rights we have heard of until we quite
thoroughly understand its demands, but it
is a singular anomaly in the carrying on of
our government that while the nation con-
trols all these minor questions of post-roads,
revenue, tariff, coinage of money, declara-
tion of peace and war, it allows the very
right of self government upon which it
stands as a nation, namely the suffrage, to
be under the control of the States.
	Experience has not yet taught the people
its danger, although the doctrine of State
Rights has been the Rock upon which the
nation has several times nearly foundered.
	Under the Confederatisn of 1778, (July 9,
1778, ratified by eight colonies,) before we
became the United States, * the doctrine of
State Rights was not primarily, a southern
one; it originated in New England, and as
far as fatherhood of this theory is concern-
ed, the North and not the South, must bear
the burden. Calhoun learned its first prin-
ciples in a northern college, that of Yale,
in Connecticut, from President Dwight,
and in a Massachusetts law office, that of
Theophilus Parsons, he was taught it in an
intensified form to take back with him to
South Carolina.
	Women interested themselves in it as
well as men. Miss Pinckney, the only
lady who ever received the honor of a pub-
lic funeral in this country, wrote so vigor-
ously in its favor that it was said the Nulli-
fication Party of South Carolina was con-
solidated by the nib of a lady's pen.
	Miss Pinckney is an illustrious example
of the power, irresponsible though it may
be, that ever has been wielded by woman
in political affairs. To her we may look
back as one of the chief instruments in
bringing about our present remarkable po-
itical complications. As the invention of-
the Cotton Gin was said to have perpetuata
ed slavery, (and its invention was due to a

[Column 3]
woman) so Miss Pinckney's powerful writ-
ings fixed the public thought in the line of
State Rights. She gathered all the fleeting
impressions brought by Calhoun from New
England, consolidated them into a harmoni-
ous whole, and the real State's Rights party
was born. It is always acknowledged that
not the originator of an idea is so truly re-
sponsible for its fatherhood as is the one
who brings it prominently before the world.
This was Miss Pinckney's position as re-
gards the State Rights question. It was
begotten in New England, born in South
Carolina, and reared to manhood by Miss
Pinckney.
	Despite all that man says, despite all the
opposition she meets, woman will play her
part in politics; it may be well, it may be
ill, but always with power. From Mercy
Otis Warren, of Massschusetts, down
through Miss Pinckney, of South Carolina,
to the latest by-play of Agnes Jenks, it is
impossible for our country—or any other—
to eliminate women from politics.
	The question comes down finally to this
one: Shall women be endowed with a re-
sponsible power, or shall she continue to
exercise her rights in an unrecognized and
irresponsible way whose results may bring
harm that responsible recognized power
cannot do?
	It is a matter of regret that women do
not thoroughly inform themselves of wo-
man's powerful influence in the past, upon
politics. At least let them recognize her
power in our own country in the past and
to-day, where we not only have a Jenks
but a "Gail Hamilton" exposing frauds, and
a western wife and daughter openly recog-
nized as obtaining through their influence
a senatorial seat for husband and father.
	But to go back. In 1782 Rhode Island re-
fused compliance with the recommenda-
tion of congress for a tariff dnty on imports
and prize goods, and through the speaker,
Hon. Wm. Bradford, of the Lower House
of Assembly of that State, made their ob-
jections known to congress, under three
heads, which it is not necessary to present
here.
	This action of Rhode Island led to pro-
longed debate and finally to a recommenda-
tion from congress that the States should
invest that body with greater power for
collecting revenue and prosecuting the
war, than it then possessed. So permeated
was the confederacy with the idea of colo-
nial supremacy—remember that although
this was two years after the Declaration
of Independence the union was only a con-
federation of the colonies, not yet having
grown to either the stature of States or of a 
constitutional government—so thoroughly
was this separated sovereignty idea a part
of the confederate idea, that the proposition
to invest congress with greater power was
only agreed to by four of the thirteen ccu-
federate colonies, and yet it was an ob-
viously necessary suggestion provided the
union, such as it was, was to be preserved.
	This non-compliance with what was need-
ed to establish and confirm the nation
again led to active debate. From revenue,
public debt, and the security of property
rights, the debate took wider range, and
the controversy soon based itself upon the
security of personal rights. Then were ut-
tered these memorable words "that it had
ever been the pride and boast of America
that the rights for which she contended
were the rights of human nature" and "that
the citizens of the United States were re-
sponsible for the greatest trust ever confid-
ed to a political society," and that it was
for the people of the United States, by
whose will, and for whose benefit, the Fed-


[Column 1]

eral Government was instituted, to decide
whether they would support their rank as
a nation.
	My own State of New York was the first
to acknowledge the necessity of stronger
power in the national hands. In July, 1782,
the Legislature of this State in a series of
nine concurrent resolutions, pointed out
the defects of the Confederacy, or State
Rights system, and proposed a general con-
vention of the United Colonies for the pur-
pose of amending the form of government.
	Thus, barely two years had elapsed be-
fore the actual necessities of the new-form-
ed republic proved the need of stronger
power in the hands of the general govern-
ment. The subject. however, passed, and
not until 1786, upon a resolution of the
House of Delegates of Virginia, appointing
commissioners to meet with commission-
ers from such other States as might appoint
them, did this very vital question press
itself upon the people with sufficient force
for action.
	In September, 1786, duly appointed com-
missioners from New York, New Jersey,
Pennsylvania, Delaware and Virginia, two
of them Southern and three of them slave
states, met to discuss this now most impor-
tant question. The delegates from these
five states proposed a general convention
of the states to meet in Philadelphia. which
convention framed our National Constitu-
tion.
	Rhode Island, as she was the first state
to exhibit state-right tendencies, was the
last state to ratify the constitution, which
was to take from her all power to question
the right of the United States to lay a duty
upon imported goods. But at various
times and for various pretences, since that
memorable refusal of Rhode Island in 1778,
to be bound by national authority, down to
the present hour, has this conflict between
state and national authority risen. Not
alone to-day, in regard to any supposed
election frauds or intimidation in South
Carolina or Louisana in regard to the
voting of black men, but up in Wyoming,
a territory which nine years ago secured to
women the right of voting, is there a
conflict between state and national power.
I shall not go back to the civil war which
you so well know, but I can be permitted
to refer to some of its results, as it is a sub-
ject of discussion in congress.
	Senator Blaine's recent speech struck the
key-note of Republican policy--mind you,
I say policy, not principle, as far as the
black male citizen is concerned.
	I must be allowed to review Mr. Blaine's
speech. Mr. Blaine is not only the Repub-
lican Senatorial mouth-piece, as far as the
south is concerned, but he is also consid-
ered a presidential candidate for 1880, and
as such, is supposed to stand at the very
foundation stones of the Republican party.
	He declares the issue is not one of mere
sentiment for the rights of the negro, but
one of more portentous magnitude, and that
is, whether the white voter of the North
shall be equal to the white voter of the
South in shaping the policy and fixing the
doctrines of the country.
	There are two curious features to us wo-
men which rise in looking at this speech of-
Senator Blaine. First, we remember his
going through the country in 1872, declar-
ing that the 14th amendment secured the
rights of all citizens to the ballot. He did
not qualify his assertion either by the
words "white" or "male." He simply said
the rights of all citizens were thus secured
by this amendment.
	I came to Washington that following
winter and with the remembrance of this

[Column 2]

speech recently delivered in my own city
of Syracuse, I sought an interview with
Mr. Blaine. I said to him, "Mr. Blaine,
inasmuch as the 14th amendment declares
all persons born or naturalized in the Uni-
ted States to be citizens of the United
States, and inasmuch as the women of the
United States were either born here or have
been naturalized here, they are necessarily
citizens of the United States. You admit
that. Very well, then, in your Syracuse
speech you declared the 14th amendment
secured the right of suffrage to all citizens;
now why are you not consistent? Why do
you not uphold the voting right of women
citizens by a speech in congress?"
	I need not say to you Washington people
what politicians are, for you all know how
easily they dodge a leading question, and
Mr. Blaine dodged my question in a very
peculiar way for he invited me to his house
to see Miss Dodge, otherwise known as
Gail Hamilton. [Laughter and applause.]
So, of course, you may rest assured I
read Mr. Blaine's speech of December 10th,
with an appreciative eye.
	Now while Mr. Blaine was so emphati-
cally pointing out the intimidation of the
black United States citizens of the South
by State Rights men, he forgot to mention
how the United States itself was at that
moment and for years had been oppressing
certain of its own white citizens of the
North.
	He forgot to say that the United States
is inconsistent with itself.
	Although, theoretically, the power over
the suffrage is held to be in the hands of
the States alone, yet practically, the United
States has endowed whatever class it pleas-
ed with the suffrage. Chief Justice Waite
in his decision in the Minor vs. Happersatt
case, against woman's right to vote under
the Fourteenth Amendment, declared the
United States had no voters. But this as-
sertion was false upon the very face of it.
	First, every enfranchised male slave had
the ballot secured him under United States
law, a law which overrode all state provi-
sions against color. At time of ratification
of the last amendments, the State of New
York possessed a color property qualifica-
tion of two hundred and fifty dollars. The
moment these amendments were ratified,
that law became dead on the statute book.
The New York Legislature did not repeal
it. The United States repealed this prop-
erty prohibition, by creating a class of
United States voters out of colored men.
So here is one class of United States voters,
and a clear mistake on the part of Judge
Waite.
	Second, every Southern man disfranchised
because of having taken part in the war,
and who has since been granted amnesty,
has again been made a voter through United
States law; all such men then become
United States voters. Here is a second
class of United States voters, and a second
mistake of Chief Justice Waite.
	Third, the naturalized foreigner secures
his right to vote under United States law,
and cannot vote unless he first becomes a
United States citizen, or announces his in-
tention of so becoming. In Missouri, Ne-
braska, and some other States, the declara-
tion of such intention permits him to vote.
This is a State regulation, but the fact of
his United States citizenship must in some
form first exist. In the naturalized man is
a third class of United States voters. With
one and the same hand he at the same mo-
ment picks up his naturalization papers and
his ballot. It matters not what the state
law may be, the foreigner secures his vote
under United States law. And here is a

[Column 3]

third class of United States voters and a
third mistake of Chief Justice Waite.
	Fourth, the 39th or 40th Congress took a
step farther than this, passing a law that
all foreigners who had served in and been
honorably discharged from the army, should
possess the right to vote, even though they
had not previously filed intention of natur-
alization. Thus at the time of Judge
Waite's decision we already had four classes
of United States voters; the black man, the
amnestied man, the naturalized man, and
the foreigner who fought in the union
army. So you see we already possess four
distinct classes of United States voters, and
when woman demands that her right to the
use of the ballot shall be secured by United
States law, she has four distinguished
precedents, and so far as it has gone, the
United States has exercised its rightful
power. We ask that it should be consistent
with itself and secure the ballot to women.
	While theoretically denying its control
over the suffrage, the United States in
many ways besides those mentioned, prac-
tically acknowledges its possession of this
right.
	In Miss Anthony's case. She was not
prosecuted by the State of New York for
voting. That great State took no action at
all in the matter, it was the general govern-
ment which thrust itself forward and took
up the question. If the United States has
no control over the suffrage, then Miss An-
thony's trial was a clear interference of the
United States with the right of states.
And so great was this interference, it is be-
lieved a judge was especially appointed to
try her case, who left Washington with his
verdict in his pocket already written. Let
none of my audience forget this trial, for in
the future it will be looked upon as one of
the great State Trials of the world, for as
we have shown, if states have the sole con-
trol of the suffrage, there was interference
in the rights of the State of New York by
her trial; and if United States citizens of
any class have a right to be protected in the
use of the ballot, then the United States
very flagrantly and tyrannously interfered
in Miss Anthony's individual right as a
citizen of the United States.
	Then let us look at the Territory of Wyo-
ming. Much has of late been said in regard
to woman not making use of the ballot
there. I am not careful to answer that
statement. I care little about it one way or
the other, as long as her right to vote is
not interfered with. It will be time to re-
quire all women to vote when we have such
law for men; until then let each voter re-
irain from voting at his or her own option;
it is not the vital question. But there is a
point connected with woman's voting in
Wyoming that is well worthy of our con-
sideration. That is, the interference of the
United States with the concomitants of this
right. For a time the women of Wyoming
sat upon juries, and the fact was heralded
over the country that thieves, gamblers,
murderers fled the country rather than fall
into the hands of these women jurors. The
first conviction for a murder in that Terri-
tory, not committed in self-defense came
from a woman jury.
	But of late we have ceased hearing of
women jurors. And why?  Because wo-
man's sacred jury right has been interfered
with by the United States.
	The Marshal of the Territory, an officer
appointed by the United States govern-
ment, has absolutely refused to place the
names of women on the jury lists. Conse-
quently the women of Wyoming are denied
the exercise of this right by United States
power.


Whether the Marshal has been ordered to
omit the names of women, as Judge Hunt
was ordered to convict in Miss Anthony's
case, we do not know and it does not signi-
fy. The duty of the United States is none
the less clear; the territories are in an es-
pecial way the wards of the nation, and
should be protected in all territorial rights.
The Territory of Wyoming having secured
to women the exercise of their right of 
voting, it remains the duty of the general
government to protect them in the exercise
of concomitant rights, of which the jury is
one.
	This deprivation of woman's jury rights
in Wyoming is not only an United States in-
terference with her political rights, but also
an interference with her industrial rights.
It is a well-known fact that some women
earned their first independent dollar by sit-
ting in the jury box. And whatever inter-
feres with woman's industrial rights helps
to force her down, down, down, to those
depths where want of bread has forced so
many women into the gutters of shame.
This is a question of morals as well as a
question of industrial and political rights.
Every adverse touch upon a person's politi-
cal rights touches a hundred other rights
adversely.

To be continued.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		245


246		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


OBITUARY.

	Died on the 5th of April 1879. Mrs. Amanda
Susanna Chamberlain, wife of John A. Cham-
berlain. Deceased was the daughter of Wm. and
M. A. E. Watmough, the latter a lady well
known in this community; was born in Wi-
gan, Lancashire, May 18, 1844, and, her moth-
er being a Latter-day Saint, was baptized and
confirmed when eight years old, by Bishop
Cairns.
	She moved with her parents to Boston in
1856; remained there for six years, and gradu-
ated with honors from the Welles School. In
the June of 1862 she came to Utah and was
married on the 29th of December, 1864. They
resided in Cedar Fort, Utah County, ten years
and then moved to a remote district about ten
miles from Deseret, on the Sevier. She was a 
faithful Saint, a true wife and mother, and 
leaves a husband and three little children to
mourn her loss.


HINTS FOR YOUNG MOTHERS.

	The three requisites for babies are plenty of
sleep, plenty of food, plenty of flannel. The say-
ing that man is a bundle of habits is as true of
babies as it is of grown children. If the infant
is accustomed from its birth to sleep from six
o'clock at night till daylight, the habit of carly
sleep will be formed, and the mother may have
all the evenings to herself.
	If the baby sleeps all night, a long morning
nap will naturally come about dinner-time, after
which the child, except when very young, should
be kept awake until six o'clock. Preseverance
in this routine will soon result in securing quiet
evenings for both the child and the parent.
	Some mothers have a long season every morn-
ing and every night in getting the baby asleep.
They rock them and sing to them till Morpheus
enfolds them. With most children this is entirely
unnecessary. An infant can be accustomed, by
a few days' training, to go to sleep itself for a
morning nap as well as for a longer rest at night.
	A mother has duties to herself as well as to her
offspring. While she should exercise a constant
care in securing its utmost physical comfort, she
should secure rest and recreation for herself. In
no other way can she keep fresh in feeling and


buoyant in spirit. Nothing is so wearing as the
unceasing tending of a fretful baby.
	Every means should be employed to aid the
child in taking care of itself and giving as little
trouble as possible. It may learn in babyhood to
amuse itself with toys or by watching movements
going on around it.
	Fashion as well as good sense requires infants'
dresses to be made with long sleeves, and high
in the neck. Fashion requires children of all ages
to be warmly clad. Flannel should encase the
whole body, with the exception of the head and
hands. The fruitful cause of colic in infants is
the nakedness of their necks and arms.
	Regularity in feeding is as important as either
of the other requisites. Babies cry as often from
being fed too frequently as from hunger. Let the
mother obey the dictates of common sense in this
matter and not force food into a baby's stomach
for every little complaint it makes.
	Children of three or four years old need much
more sleep than they usually have. For irritable
and nervous children sleep it is a specific, and it
can be secured to them only by the force of habit.
Many light forms of disease may be cured by
keeping a child in a uniform temperature and in
quiet. Let the young mothers who read this
article experiment upon these few suggestions,
and we are sure they will have many an hour in
the nursery for reading and thought.—Ex.


NEITHER days nor lives can be made
holy by doing nothing in them. The best
prayer at the beginning of a day is, that we
may not lose its moments; and the best
grace before meat is, the consciousness that
we have justly earned our dinner.—Ruskin.
